**(ii) If $R_S = 1.5\,\text{k}\Omega$, will it fire? (2 marks)**

Let us find the gate current that would flow with $R_S = 1500\,\Omega$:

$$I_G = \frac{V_S - V_{GT}}{R_S} = \frac{15 - 3}{1500} = \frac{12}{1500} = 8\,\text{mA}$$

Comparing with $I_{GT} = 20\,\text{mA}$:

$$I_G = 8\,\text{mA} < I_{GT} = 20\,\text{mA}$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{\text{The SCR will NOT fire}}$ because the available gate current (8 mA) is below the required trigger current (20 mA).

**(iii) Gate Power Dissipation at Triggering: (2 marks)**

$$P_G = V_{GT} \cdot I_{GT} = 3 \times 20 \times 10^{-3} = 60\,\text{mW}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{P_G = 60\,\text{mW}}$

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the **gate triggering of an SCR using a single-pulse train** through an RC network. What are the advantages of pulse triggering over DC triggering?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A thyristor has $I_L = 80\,\text{mA}$, $I_H = 30\,\text{mA}$, $I_{GT} = 10\,\text{mA}$, $V_{GT} = 1.8\,\text{V}$. The load current after triggering rises linearly from 0 to 200 mA in 50 µs and then remains constant. **(i)** What is the minimum gate pulse width required for the SCR to latch? **(ii)** If the gate pulse is removed after 30 µs, will the SCR remain ON? Justify.

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**RC Single-Pulse Trigger Circuit (5 marks):**

The RC network charging circuit is one of the most common SCR triggering methods. The circuit consists of:
- A **DC source** $V_S$
- A **variable resistor** $R$ in series with a **capacitor** $C$
- The capacitor charges through $R$ with a time constant $\tau = RC$
- When capacitor voltage $V_C$ exceeds the **breakover voltage** of a small **DIAC** or **UJT** relaxation oscillator, a **sharp pulse** is delivered to the gate

**Operation Sequence:**
1. Initially, capacitor $C$ is uncharged; $V_C = 0$
2. $C$ charges exponentially through $R$: $V_C(t) = V_S \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$
3. When $V_C$ reaches the peak-point voltage $V_P$ of the UJT (or breakover of DIAC), the UJT fires
4. A sharp **positive gate pulse** of short duration (< 10 µs) is delivered to the SCR gate
5. The SCR turns ON if $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ and the anode current can reach $I_L$

**Advantages of Pulse Triggering over DC Triggering (2 marks):**
1. **Lower average gate power dissipation** — pulses are narrow, so thermal stress on the gate junction is minimized.
2. **Better isolation** — pulse transformers provide galvanic isolation easily, impossible with continuous DC.
3. **Reduced false triggering** — short pulses are less susceptible to noise integration.
4. **Better timing accuracy** — sharp pulses allow precise firing angle control.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["V_S DC Source"] --> R["Variable Resistor R"]
    R --> C["Capacitor C\nCharges to V_P"]
    C --> U["UJT Relaxation\nOscillator"]
    U -->|"Sharp pulse"| P["Pulse Transformer\nIsolates HV side"]
    P --> G["SCR Gate\nFires the device"]
    
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style U fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9900
    style G fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34
```

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Minimum Gate Pulse Width for Latching: (4 marks)**

For the SCR to latch, the anode current $I_A$ must reach $I_L = 80\,\text{mA}$ **before** the gate pulse is removed.

The anode current rises linearly: at time $t$, $I_A(t) = \left(\dfrac{200\,\text{mA}}{50\,\mu\text{s}}\right) \cdot t$

We need $I_A(t_p) = I_L$:

$$t_p = I_L \cdot \frac{50\,\mu\text{s}}{200\,\text{mA}} = 80 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$t_p = \frac{80 \times 50}{200}\,\mu\text{s} = \frac{4000}{200}\,\mu\text{s} = 20\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{t_{p(min)} = 20\,\mu\text{s}}$

**(ii) If Gate Pulse Removed After 30 µs, Will SCR Stay ON? (3 marks)**

At $t = 30\,\mu\text{s}$, the anode current is:

$$I_A(30\,\mu\text{s}) = \left(\frac{200\,\text{mA}}{50\,\mu\text{s}}\right) \cdot 30\,\mu\text{s} = 4 \cdot 30 = 120\,\text{mA}$$

Comparison:
$$I_A = 120\,\text{mA} > I_L = 80\,\text{mA}$$

Since the anode current **exceeded** the latching current **before** the gate pulse was removed, the SCR has successfully latched.

**After 30 µs**, the load current continues to rise to 200 mA and stays constant. As long as $I_A > I_H = 30\,\text{mA}$, the SCR remains ON.

**Answer:** $\boxed{\text{Yes, the SCR will remain ON.}}$

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU Students Lose Marks (Dec 2023 / May 2024 patterns):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_H$:** Students frequently write "$I_H > I_L$" — this is **always wrong**. $I_L$ > $I_H$ always.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting units in gate resistor calculation:** Writing $R_S = 12/0.02$ without converting mA to A → answer is **wrong by factor 1000**.
> 
> 3. **Drawing gate characteristic curve without labeling axes:** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if $V_{GK}$ and $I_G$ axes are missing or unlabelled.
> 
> 4. **Assuming DC triggering is preferred:** Modern KTU paper emphasizes **pulse triggering** — students writing "DC triggering is best" lose marks. Pulse triggering is the industrial standard.
> 
> 5. **Incorrect pulse width calculation:** Many students forget that the gate pulse must remain **until $I_A \geq I_L$**, not just for some arbitrary duration.

---

### 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Gate Characteristics** = $V_{GK}$ vs $I_G$ curve of the gate-cathode junction
- ⭐ **Three Regions:** Non-Triggering ($< I_{GD}$), Uncertain ($I_{GD} < I_G < I_{GT}$), Reliable Triggering ($\geq I_{GT}$)
- ⭐ **$I_L > I_H$** — latching current always greater than holding current
- ⭐ **Gate Resistor Formula:** $R_S = \dfrac{V_S - V_{GT}}{I_{GT}}$
- ⭐ **Gate Power:** $P_{G(av)} = V_{GT} \cdot I_{GT} \cdot D$ where $D$ = duty cycle
- ⭐ **Pulse width requirement:** Gate pulse must remain until $I_A \geq I_L$
- ⭐ **Pulse triggering > DC triggering** (lower power, better isolation, noise immune)
- ⭐ **Safety margin:** Always drive 2×–3× above $I_{GT}$ to compensate temperature & aging
- ⭐ **$P_{GM}$ limit:** Never exceed peak gate power or junction will burn out
- ⭐ **Temperature effect:** $I_{GT}$ and $I_L$ decrease with temperature; $I_{GD}$ increases

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gate Drive Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT747 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gate Drive Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Gate Drive Circuit (GDC)** is a dedicated low-power interface circuit that translates a low-power logic-level control signal (typically **3.3 V**, **5 V**, or **15 V** from a microcontroller or PWM controller) into a properly conditioned, high-current, often isolated signal capable of swiftly switching the gate of a power semiconductor device such as a **Power MOSFET**, **IGBT**, **BJT**, **GTO**, or **SCR** between its fully-ON and fully-OFF states.

The KTU 2024 syllabus defines a gate drive circuit as a *signal-conditioning and isolation sub-system* that must simultaneously satisfy four engineering constraints: (i) sufficient gate charge delivery $(Q_g)$, (ii) sufficient peak gate current $(I_{g,peak})$, (iii) galvanic isolation between logic and power domains, and (iv) protection against abnormal operating conditions such as **dv/dt**, **short-circuit desaturation (DESAT)**, and **under-voltage lockout (UVLO)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** A gate drive is **NOT** a simple logic buffer. It is a *power amplifier* (Class-B push-pull, totem-pole, or bootstrap configuration) with isolation, level-shifting, and protection features. Marks are awarded for explicitly stating these four functional blocks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steering Wheel" of Power Electronics

Imagine a **commercial passenger aircraft**:

- The **captain's joystick** in the cockpit moves only a few centimeters and requires only a small force — this is your *low-power PWM control signal* (3.3 V / 5 V from a microcontroller).
- However, the **rudder and ailerons** on the wings weigh hundreds of kilograms and must move massive aerodynamic surfaces — these are your *power semiconductor switches* (IGBTs rated for 1200 V / 600 A).
- The **hydraulic servo system** between the joystick and the wings is your **Gate Drive Circuit**. It amplifies the captain's tiny input, provides isolation (electrical separation between cockpit and wings), ensures rapid response, and includes safety interlocks (overload protection) so the plane does not crash.

In the same way, a gate drive is the *muscle and nervous system* that allows a fragile digital controller to safely command an industrial-scale power switch.

### 1.3 Why Gate Drive Circuits Are Necessary

| Reason | Physical Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Logic Level Mismatch** | A microcontroller outputs **3.3 V** CMOS. An IGBT needs typically **+15 V** to fully turn ON and **−5 V to −15 V** to firmly turn OFF. |
| **Gate Charge Requirement** | A power MOSFET/IGBT gate looks like a non-linear capacitor ($C_{iss}$). A finite *gate charge* $Q_g$ must be supplied to switch the device. |
| **Source/Emitter Referencing** | The emitter/source of a low-side device is at ground, but a high-side device's source/emitter swings with the load. The driver must handle this floating reference. |
| **Isolation** | The control circuit and power circuit are at vastly different potentials (e.g., 400 V bus). Direct connection is unsafe. |
| **Protection** | Without a driver, the device can be destroyed by dv/dt-induced spurious turn-on, short circuits, or UVLO events. |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard Power Metric — A typical **1200 V / 75 A IGBT** (e.g., Infineon IKW75N120T2) requires a gate charge of approximately **$Q_g \approx 310$ nC** at $+15$ V gate drive. A digital controller cannot supply this charge in microseconds — only a dedicated gate drive can.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Block Decomposition of a Gate Drive Circuit

A textbook-quality gate drive circuit (e.g., **UCC27211**, **FAN7390**, **HCPL-3160**) contains **four mandatory sub-blocks**:

**1. Isolation Barrier**
- **Optical (Optocoupler):** Uses an LED and phototransistor separated by a transparent dielectric. Provides isolation up to $\mathbf{5\ kV_{RMS}}$.
- **Magnetic (Pulse Transformer):** Uses a small high-frequency ferrite transformer. Provides isolation up to $\mathbf{4.5\ kV_{RMS}}$ and is suited for short pulses.
- **Capacitive (Digital Isolator):** Uses CMOS logic and an on-chip $SiO_2$ capacitor (e.g., **Si8261**, **ADuM1411**). Provides $\mathbf{5\ kV_{RMS}}$ with high CMRR.

**2. Level Shifter / Voltage Translator**
- Translates the logic-level PWM (3.3 V / 5 V) up to the gate-bias voltage ($+15$ V, often with a negative rail of $-5$ V to $-9$ V).

**3. Output Stage (Push-Pull / Totem-Pole)**
- A complementary pair of transistors (one PNP/PMOS sourcing current, one NPN/NMOS sinking current).
- **Source current** ($I_{g,source}$) charges the gate.
- **Sink current** ($I_{g,sink}$) discharges the gate.

**4. Protection Logic**
- **UVLO (Under-Voltage Lockout):** Disables output if $V_{CC}$ falls below the threshold (typically 11 V to 12 V for IGBTs).
- **DESAT (Desaturation Detection):** Monitors collector-emitter voltage during ON-state. If $V_{CE}$ exceeds a reference (e.g., 7 V), a short circuit is flagged and the gate is slowly turned OFF.
- **Active Miller Clamp:** A low-impedance path from gate to emitter that prevents dv/dt-induced spurious turn-on in half-bridge configurations.

### 2.2 Why Each Block Exists — The "Why & How"

- **Why Isolation?** A shorted power device will pull the high-voltage DC bus directly into the logic ground, destroying the controller. Isolation breaks this galvanic path.
- **Why Push-Pull Output?** A single-ended output cannot both source and sink current quickly. The Miller plateau region requires bidirectional gate current.
- **Why Negative Gate Voltage ($-V_{GE}$)?** A positive dv/dt across the OFF-state device induces a current through the Miller capacitor $C_{gd}$ into the gate. If the gate impedance is high, this raises the gate voltage above the threshold and *spuriously turns ON* the device. A negative bias prevents this.
- **Why DESAT Protection?** A short circuit at the load causes the IGBT to exit saturation. $V_{CE}$ rises rapidly, and the device enters its *active region* where power dissipation is enormous (can exceed rated power by 10×). DESAT detects this within **$\mathbf{2\ \mu s}$** and shuts the device down safely.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Symbol | Expression | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gate Charge** | $Q_g$ | $Q_g = \int i_g(t)\,dt$ | Coulombs (C) | Total charge required to charge the gate. |
| 2 | **Average Gate Drive Current** | $I_{g,avg}$ | $I_{g,avg} = Q_g \cdot f_{sw}$ | Amperes (A) | Average current the driver must continuously supply. |
| 3 | **Peak Gate Current** | $I_{g,peak}$ | $I_{g,peak} = \dfrac{V_{g,on} - V_{g,off}}{R_g}$ | Amperes (A) | Instantaneous peak current at turn-on transient. |
| 4 | **Gate Drive Power** | $P_{drive}$ | $P_{drive} = Q_g \cdot V_{g,on} \cdot f_{sw}$ | Watts (W) | Continuous power dissipated by gate driver. |
| 5 | **Switching Loss (per cycle)** | $E_{sw}$ | $E_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{dc} I_{load} (t_{on} + t_{off})$ | Joules (J) | Energy lost per switching transition. |
| 6 | **Total Switching Loss** | $P_{sw}$ | $P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off})$ | Watts (W) | Average power loss due to switching. |
| 7 | **Miller Charge** | $Q_{gd}$ | Read from datasheet | Coulombs (C) | Charge to overcome the Miller plateau. |
| 8 | **Bootstrap Capacitor** | $C_{boot}$ | $C_{boot} \ge \dfrac{Q_g}{\Delta V_{boot,max}}$ | Farads (F) | Minimum bootstrap capacitance. |
| 9 | **UVLO Threshold (typical IGBT)** | $V_{UVLO}$ | $\mathbf{11\ V}$ to $\mathbf{13\ V}$ | Volts (V) | Minimum supply for reliable operation. |
| 10 | **Propagation Delay** | $t_{pd}$ | Datasheet value (typ. $\mathbf{30{-}100\ ns}$) | Seconds (s) | Delay between input logic and gate output. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No `|` symbol inside tables:** The vertical pipe `|` is reserved exclusively as a markdown column delimiter. Absolute values use `$\vert \cdot \vert$` and "such that" uses `$\mid$`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gate drive circuits are the backbone of every modern power converter:

- **EV Traction Inverters:** SiC MOSFET gate drivers with DESAT protection (e.g., **UCC21750**) enable 800 V battery systems to drive motors with 250 kW peak power.
- **Solar Inverters:** Bootstrap high-side gate drivers (e.g., **IR2110**) are used in every H-bridge and three-phase inverter topology.
- **Industrial SMPS:** Isolated gate drivers with integrated DC-DC converters (e.g., **UCC21540**) provide reinforced isolation up to 5 kV for IGBTs in 3-phase motor drives.
- **Renewable Energy HVDC:** Modular multilevel converters (MMC) require thousands of isolated gate drivers operating in synchrony.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Gate Drive Current

A power semiconductor gate behaves as a non-linear capacitor. To transition the device from OFF to ON, a total charge $Q_g$ must be delivered by the driver. Since the device switches at a frequency $f_{sw}$ (number of switching events per second), the *amount of charge delivered per second* is the average current.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Charge per switching cycle} &= Q_g \quad [\text{Coulombs}] \\[4pt]
\text{Number of switching events per second} &= f_{sw} \quad [\text{Hz}] \\[4pt]
\therefore \text{Average gate current, } I_{g,avg} &= \frac{\text{Charge per second}}{\text{Second}} \\[4pt]
I_{g,avg} &= Q_g \cdot f_{sw}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** If a MOSFET has $Q_g = 50\ \text{nC}$ and switches at $f_{sw} = 100\ \text{kHz}$, then the average gate current is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{g,avg} &= 50 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{C} \times 100 \times 10^{3}\ \text{Hz} \\[4pt]
&= 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A} = 5\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the *continuous* current the driver's power supply must deliver.

### 3.2 Derivation: Peak Gate Current

During the **turn-on transient**, the gate-source capacitance ($C_{gs}$) and gate-drain capacitance ($C_{gd}$) are charged from the OFF-state voltage ($V_{g,off}$, typically $-5$ V) to the ON-state voltage ($V_{g,on}$, typically $+15$ V). The peak current is limited by the **external gate resistor** $R_g$.

Applying Ohm's law to the gate loop at the moment of switching:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{g,on} - V_{g,off} &= I_{g,peak} \cdot R_g + V_{g,internally\,at\,that\,instant} \\[4pt]
\text{Assuming the internal gate is at } V_{g,off} \text{ initially:} \quad I_{g,peak} \cdot R_g &= V_{g,on} - V_{g,off} \\[4pt]
\therefore I_{g,peak} &= \frac{V_{g,on} - V_{g,off}}{R_g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For an IGBT driver with $V_{g,on} = +15$ V, $V_{g,off} = -5$ V, and $R_g = 10\ \Omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{g,peak} &= \frac{+15 - (-5)}{10} = \frac{20}{10} = 2.0\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **2 A peak** pulse must be supplied in a few hundred nanoseconds — clearly beyond a microcontroller's capability.

### 3.3 Derivation: Continuous Gate Drive Power Loss

The driver's output stage dissipates power each time it charges and discharges the gate capacitance. Energy stored in a capacitor $C$ charged to voltage $V$ is $\tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$. Since the gate is charged and discharged once per cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy dissipated per cycle, } E_{cycle} &= V_{g,on} \cdot Q_g \quad \text{(equivalent to } Q_g \cdot V_{g,on}) \\[4pt]
\text{Multiplying by switching frequency:} \quad P_{drive} &= V_{g,on} \cdot Q_g \cdot f_{sw}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** IGBT with $Q_g = 310$ nC, $V_{g,on} = 15$ V, $f_{sw} = 20$ kHz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{drive} &= 15 \times 310 \times 10^{-9} \times 20 \times 10^{3} \\[4pt]
&= 15 \times 6.2 \times 10^{-6} = 93 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{W} \\[4pt]
&= 93\ \mu\text{W (negligible compared to switching/conduction losses)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why designers often use a **gate drive transformer** or a **bootstrap supply** to power the driver — its power consumption is small but it must be *isolated* and *stable*.

### 3.4 Derivation: Switching Energy Loss in the Power Device

When the device transitions between ON and OFF, both voltage and current are simultaneously non-zero, causing dissipation. Assuming a linear transition over times $t_{on}$ and $t_{off}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{on} &= \int_{0}^{t_{on}} v(t) \cdot i(t)\,dt \\[4pt]
\text{Assuming } v(t) = V_{dc}\left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{on}}\right), \quad i(t) = I_{load} \cdot \frac{t}{t_{on}} \\[4pt]
E_{on} &= \int_{0}^{t_{on}} V_{dc}\left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{on}}\right) \cdot I_{load} \cdot \frac{t}{t_{on}}\,dt \\[4pt]
E_{on} &= \frac{V_{dc} \cdot I_{load}}{t_{on}} \int_{0}^{t_{on}} \left(t - \frac{t^2}{t_{on}}\right) dt \\[4pt]
E_{on} &= \frac{V_{dc} \cdot I_{load}}{t_{on}} \left[\frac{t_{on}^2}{2} - \frac{t_{on}^2}{3}\right] \\[4pt]
E_{on} &= \frac{V_{dc} \cdot I_{load}}{t_{on}} \cdot \frac{t_{on}^2}{6} = \frac{1}{6} V_{dc} \cdot I_{load} \cdot t_{on}
\end{aligned}
$$

A simpler and widely-used engineering approximation is:

$$
E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{dc} \cdot I_{load} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off})
$$

This loss is the primary reason gate drive circuits must switch *quickly* — every extra microsecond of transition time wastes energy as heat.

### 3.5 Derivation: Bootstrap Capacitor Sizing

A **bootstrap circuit** uses a capacitor $C_{boot}$ that is recharged when the low-side switch is ON, and then supplies the floating high-side driver. The capacitor must retain sufficient voltage after delivering the gate charge.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Voltage droop on bootstrap capacitor:} \quad \Delta V_{boot} &= \frac{Q_{total}}{C_{boot}} \\[4pt]
\text{where } Q_{total} = Q_g + I_{leak} \cdot t_{on,max} + I_{q,driver} \cdot t_{on,max} \\[4pt]
\text{For conservative design (ignoring leakage):} \quad C_{boot} &\ge \frac{Q_g}{\Delta V_{boot,max}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Design rule of thumb (KTU-lab standard):** Choose $C_{boot}$ such that $\Delta V_{boot} \le 0.1 \cdot V_{CC}$.

**Numerical Example:** IGBT with $Q_g = 310$ nC, $V_{CC} = 15$ V, allowable droop $\Delta V = 0.5$ V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{boot} &\ge \frac{310 \times 10^{-9}}{0.5} = 620 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F} = 0.62\ \mu\text{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

A standard **1 µF / 25 V ceramic** capacitor is selected for margin.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Isolation Technique

| Parameter | Optocoupler (e.g., HCPL-3120) | Pulse Transformer | Digital Isolator (e.g., ADuM1411) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Isolation Voltage** | 5 kV (typical) | 4.5 kV | 5 kV |
| **CMR (dv/dt immunity)** | Low (~10 kV/µs) | Very High (~50 kV/µs) | Very High (>25 kV/µs) |
| **Propagation Delay** | 100–500 ns | 30–100 ns | 20–50 ns |
| **Duty Cycle Range** | DC to high (no limit) | Limited (must return to zero) | DC to high |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Use Case** | IGBT modules, motor drives | Short-pulse SiC drivers, gate drive transformers | High-speed SiC/GaN half-bridges |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — General Architecture of a Gate Drive Circuit

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *functional architecture* and signal flow of a typical isolated gate drive circuit driving a half-bridge IGBT module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctrlA[CONTROLLER MCU] -->|PWM Signal 3.3 V| inA[LOGIC INPUT BUFFER]
    ctrlB[ISOLATION BARRIER OPTO COUPLER] --> inA
    inA --> levelA[LEVEL SHIFTER 3.3 V TO 15 V]
    levelA --> uvloA[UVLO DETECTION]
    uvloA --> ppA[PUSH PULL OUTPUT STAGE]
    ppA --> millerA[ACTIVE MILLER CLAMP]
    ppA --> gateA[IGBT GATE EMITTER]
    gateA --> desatA[DESAT DETECTION]
    desatA -->|FAULT FEEDBACK| ctrlA
    ppA --> bootA[BOOTSTRAP DIODE AND CAPACITOR]
    bootA -->|SUPPLY 15 V| ppA
```

### 4.2 Signal Flow — Turn-On Sequence of a Gate Driver

This flowchart shows the *chronological decision-making process* inside a gate driver during a turn-on event, including the protection interrupts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stateA[START] --> stateB{VCC above UVLO threshold}
    stateB -- NO --> stateC[OUTPUT LATCHED OFF]
    stateB -- YES --> stateD[INPUT PWM HIGH]
    stateD --> stateE[CHARGE GATE THROUGH RG ON]
    stateE --> stateF{MILLER PLATEAU DETECTED}
    stateF -- YES --> stateG[WAIT PLATEAU DURATION]
    stateF -- NO --> stateH[CONTINUE CHARGING]
    stateG --> stateI[CHECK DESAT]
    stateH --> stateI
    stateI -- VCE below DESAT threshold --> stateJ[ON STATE CONFIRMED]
    stateI -- VCE above DESAT threshold --> stateK[SOFT TURN OFF FAULT]
    stateJ --> stateL[END]
    stateK --> stateM[REPORT FAULT TO MCU]
    stateM --> stateL
    stateC --> stateL
```

### 4.3 Bootstrap High-Side Driver Functional Architecture

The bootstrap high-side driver is a critical sub-block for half-bridge and three-phase inverters. The following diagram maps its *functional architecture* (the actual physical schematic is shown in every datasheet for IR2110 / IR2113 / IRS2003).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOW_SIDE_BLOCK[LOW SIDE BLOCK]
        LS_CTRL[LOW SIDE LOGIC] --> LS_OUT[PUSH PULL LOW SIDE]
        LS_OUT --> LO_PIN[LO OUTPUT PIN]
        LS_OUT --> VB_CHARGE[BOOTSTRAP CHARGE PATH]
    end
    subgraph BOOTSTRAP[BOOTSTRAP NETWORK]
        VB_CHARGE --> DB[BOOTSTRAP DIODE]
        DB --> CB[BOOTSTRAP CAPACITOR]
        CB --> VS_PIN[VS FLOATING GROUND]
    end
    subgraph HIGH_SIDE_BLOCK[HIGH SIDE BLOCK]
        VS_PIN --> LVL_SHIFT[LEVEL SHIFTER TO FLOATING DOMAIN]
        LVL_SHIFT --> HS_OUT[PUSH PULL HIGH SIDE]
        HS_OUT --> HO_PIN[HO OUTPUT PIN]
        HS_OUT --> UVLO_HS[HIGH SIDE UVLO]
    end
    VCC[15 V SUPPLY] --> DB
    HO_PIN --> IGBT_GATE[IGBT GATE]
    LO_PIN --> IGBT_E[IGBT EMITTER OR LOW SIDE MOSFET]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Schematic — Optocoupler-Isolated IGBT Gate Driver

The following Mermaid block diagram emulates the conceptual schematic of an optocoupler-based IGBT gate driver (functional, not pin-accurate).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mcuA[MCU 3.3 V PWM] --> r1A[LIMITING RESISTOR 470 OHM]
    r1A --> ledA[OPTO LED INSIDE HCPL 3120]
    ledA --> gndA[GROUND]
    ledA -.->|OPTICAL BEAM| pdA[PHOTODIODE STAGE]
    pdA --> ampA[GAIN STAGE]
    ampA --> uvloA[UVLO COMPARATOR]
    uvloA --> ppA[PUSH PULL 15 V OUTPUT]
    ppA --> rgA[GATE RESISTOR RG 10 OHM]
    rgA --> gateA[IGBT GATE]
    ppA --> clmpA[MILLER CLAMP NMOS]
    clmpA --> gateA
    desatA[DESAT BLANKING DIODE] --> cmpA[DESAT COMPARATOR 7 V REF]
    cmpA --> faultA[FAULT OPEN DRAIN]
    faultA --> mcuA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block diagrams instead of circuit schematics?** The Mermaid renderer does not support physical electronic schematic symbols (resistors, capacitors, IGBT symbols, ground arrows). The KTU-2024 expected answer for Module 1 can use *either* a hand-drawn schematic *or* a labelled block diagram. The above block diagrams cover both functionalities and signal flow paths, which earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a gate drive circuit. List any two essential functions it must perform for an IGBT-based inverter.*

**Model Answer:**

A **gate drive circuit** is an interface sub-system that conditions a low-power logic signal into a high-current, often isolated, voltage waveform suitable for switching the gate of a power semiconductor device such as an IGBT or MOSFET.

**Two essential functions (any two, 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Level shifting and amplification:** Converts a **3.3 V / 5 V** CMOS PWM signal into a **+15 V / −5 V** gate-bias waveform capable of sourcing/sinking peak currents of **1–5 A**.
2. **Galvanic isolation:** Provides dielectric isolation (up to **5 kV RMS**) between the low-voltage controller and the high-voltage power stage, protecting the controller from bus transients.
3. **Protection:** Implements **UVLO, DESAT, and Miller clamp** functions to safeguard the power device from abnormal conditions.

*(Total: 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series-2]**
*What is meant by a bootstrap capacitor in a high-side gate driver? Why is its value critical?*

**Model Answer:**

A **bootstrap capacitor** ($C_{boot}$) is an external energy-storage element that supplies the floating supply voltage to a **high-side gate driver** in half-bridge and three-phase inverter topologies. It is recharged through a bootstrap diode whenever the low-side switch is ON (when the high-side emitter is near ground).

**Why its value is critical (3 marks):**

When the high-side switch turns ON, $C_{boot}$ must deliver the gate charge $Q_g$ of the high-side device *plus* the quiescent current of the driver *plus* leakage. The minimum value is given by:

$$
C_{boot} \ge \frac{Q_g}{\Delta V_{boot,max}}
$$

If $C_{boot}$ is too small, the voltage droop causes the high-side gate voltage to fall below the UVLO threshold, resulting in **intermittent high-side turn-OFF**, which can destroy the half-bridge. If it is too large, the recharge time during the low-side ON interval becomes insufficient, again leading to droop. A typical value is **1 µF to 10 µF ceramic (X7R)**, sized to keep $\Delta V_{boot} \le 0.1 \cdot V_{CC}$.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of an isolated gate drive circuit for an IGBT. Discuss the role of **optocoupler isolation** and **push-pull output stage**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 1200 V, 100 A IGBT has a total gate charge $Q_g = 320$ nC and is switched at $f_{sw} = 25$ kHz. The gate drive voltage is $V_{g,on} = +15$ V with $V_{g,off} = -5$ V. The external gate resistor is $R_g = 12\ \Omega$. Calculate:
   (i) Average gate current
   (ii) Peak gate current
   (iii) Continuous gate drive power
   (iv) The minimum bootstrap capacitance if the allowable droop is $0.5$ V. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Blocks of an Isolated IGBT Gate Drive (7 marks):**

Refer to **SECTION 4.4** for the labelled block diagram (5 functional blocks).

1. **Input Buffer / Logic Receiver (1 mark):** Receives the low-voltage PWM (3.3 V / 5 V) from the controller. Provides hysteresis (Schmitt trigger) for noise immunity.

2. **Optocoupler Isolation (2 marks):** An **LED** on the input side emits photons, which are received by a **photodiode / phototransistor** on the output side. The two sides are separated by a transparent dielectric (typically silicone or epoxy) that supports up to **5 kV RMS**. This breaks the galvanic path and protects the controller from a **1200 V bus short**. A key parameter is the **common-mode transient immunity (CMTI)**, typically **15–25 kV/µs** for modern optocouplers (e.g., HCPL-3160).

3. **Level Shifter + Push-Pull Output Stage (2 marks):** A complementary emitter-follower (totem-pole) pair — one transistor **sources** current to charge the gate, the other **sinks** current to discharge it. This bidirectional current capability is essential to rapidly overcome the **Miller plateau** during switching. The output is referenced to the IGBT's emitter (which can be floating for high-side).

4. **Protection Logic: UVLO + DESAT (1.5 marks):** UVLO disables output if $V_{CC} < 12$ V. DESAT monitors $V_{CE,sat}$ during ON state; if it exceeds ~7 V, a short circuit is detected and the gate is slowly turned off to limit di/dt and prevent dv/dt-induced over-voltage.

5. **Bootstrap / Isolated DC-DC Supply (0.5 marks):** Provides the floating supply to the high-side driver.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $Q_g = 320\ \text{nC} = 320 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{C}$, $f_{sw} = 25\ \text{kHz} = 25 \times 10^{3}\ \text{Hz}$, $V_{g,on} = 15\ \text{V}$, $V_{g,off} = -5\ \text{V}$, $R_g = 12\ \Omega$, $\Delta V_{boot} = 0.5\ \text{V}$.

**(i) Average gate current (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{g,avg} &= Q_g \cdot f_{sw} \\[4pt]
&= 320 \times 10^{-9} \times 25 \times 10^{3} \\[4pt]
&= 8.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A} = \mathbf{8\ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Peak gate current (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{g,peak} &= \frac{V_{g,on} - V_{g,off}}{R_g} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{15 - (-5)}{12} = \frac{20}{12} = \mathbf{1.67\ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Continuous gate drive power (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{drive} &= V_{g,on} \cdot Q_g \cdot f_{sw} \\[4pt]
&= 15 \times 320 \times 10^{-9} \times 25 \times 10^{3} \\[4pt]
&= 120 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W} = \mathbf{120\ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Minimum bootstrap capacitance (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{boot} &\ge \frac{Q_g}{\Delta V_{boot,max}} \\[4pt]
&\ge \frac{320 \times 10^{-9}}{0.5} \\[4pt]
&\ge 640 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F} = \mathbf{0.64\ \mu F}
\end{aligned}
$$

A **1 µF / 25 V X7R ceramic capacitor** is chosen for design margin (factor of 1.5×).

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **bootstrap high-side gate drive circuit** used in a half-bridge inverter. Why is a negative gate bias often used during turn-OFF? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a circuit diagram, describe the function of **DESAT (desaturation) protection** in an IGBT gate driver. A 600 V IGBT in a half-bridge has $t_{on} = 80$ ns and $t_{off} = 120$ ns. The DC bus voltage is $400$ V and the load current is $50$ A. Calculate the switching power loss. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bootstrap High-Side Gate Drive Operation (7 marks):**

Refer to **SECTION 4.3** for the block diagram.

**Operation phases (4 marks):**

- **Phase 1 — Low-side ON (recharge):** When the low-side switch $Q_2$ is ON, the high-side emitter $V_S$ is pulled near ground. The bootstrap diode $D_b$ is forward-biased from $V_{CC}$ (15 V), and the bootstrap capacitor $C_{boot}$ charges to approximately $V_{CC} - V_{D} \approx 14.3$ V. The voltage stored is $V_{BS} = V_{boot} - V_S \approx 15$ V across the floating supply rails of the high-side driver.

- **Phase 2 — High-side ON (discharge):** When $Q_1$ is switched ON, $V_S$ swings rapidly to the DC bus voltage (e.g., 400 V). The bootstrap diode is now reverse-biased, isolating $C_{boot}$ from $V_{CC}$. The high-side driver draws its supply from $C_{boot}$, which delivers the gate charge $Q_g$ to $Q_1$. The voltage on $C_{boot}$ droops by $\Delta V = Q_g / C_{boot}$.

- **Phase 3 — Dead-time:** Both switches are OFF for a short interval (typically 200–500 ns) to prevent shoot-through.

- **Phase 4 — Recharge resumes** when $Q_2$ turns ON again.

**Why negative gate bias is used (3 marks):**

In a half-bridge, when the high-side switch is ON, a high **dv/dt** (typically 5–10 kV/µs) appears across the low-side switch during the high-side turn-ON transient. This dv/dt drives a displacement current $i = C_{gd} \cdot dv/dt$ through the **Miller capacitor** into the gate of the low-side device. If the gate impedance is high, the gate voltage can rise above the threshold $V_{th}$, causing **spurious turn-on** (also called *shoot-through* or *cross-conduction*). Applying a **negative bias of −5 V to −9 V** ensures that even with the Miller-induced voltage bump, the gate remains below $V_{th}$ — typically $V_{th} \approx 5.5$ V for an IGBT, so a negative rail of −5 V gives a comfortable **10 V noise margin**.

**(b) DESAT Protection + Switching Loss Calculation (7 marks):**

**DESAT operation (3 marks):**

DESAT (desaturation) protection monitors the collector-emitter voltage $V_{CE}$ of the IGBT during its ON state. Under normal operation, the IGBT is saturated and $V_{CE} \approx 1.5$–$3$ V. Under a short-circuit condition (e.g., load short to ground), the IGBT cannot sustain saturation, and $V_{CE}$ rises to the bus voltage. The DESAT circuit works as follows:

1. A **high-voltage blocking diode** $D_{desat}$ allows sensing only when the IGBT is ON.
2. An internal **current source** ($I_{chg} \approx 250\ \mu A$) charges an external blanking capacitor $C_{blank}$.
3. If $V_{CE} > V_{DESAT,ref} \approx 7$ V for a duration exceeding the **blanking time** ($t_{blank} \approx 1$–$3$ µs, set by $C_{blank}$), the comparator trips.
4. The gate driver then initiates a **soft turn-off** (slow discharge of the gate through a high resistance) to limit the di/dt and prevent destructive over-voltage ($V = L \cdot di/dt$).
5. A **FAULT** signal is sent to the MCU, which can disable all PWM outputs.

This protects the IGBT from the **massive power dissipation** that occurs in the active region ($P = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$ can reach 10× the rated power).

**Switching power loss (4 marks):**

**Given:** $t_{on} = 80$ ns $= 80 \times 10^{-9}$ s, $t_{off} = 120$ ns $= 120 \times 10^{-9}$ s, $V_{dc} = 400$ V, $I_{load} = 50$ A. Assume a switching frequency of $f_{sw} = 20$ kHz (typical — not given, must be stated as an assumption; if a different frequency is given in the original question, substitute).

**Energy loss per cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{sw} &= \frac{1}{2} V_{dc} \cdot I_{load} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 400 \times 50 \times (80 + 120) \times 10^{-9} \\[4pt]
&= 10000 \times 200 \times 10^{-9} \\[4pt]
&= 2.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{J} = \mathbf{2\ mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Average switching power loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sw} &= E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw} \\[4pt]
&= 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 \times 10^{3} \\[4pt]
&= 40\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing gate charge $Q_g$ with gate-source capacitance $C_{gs}$.** $Q_g$ is *integrated* charge from the datasheet curve, not a simple $CV$.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative rail.** Students often write only $V_{g,on} = +15$ V and ignore $V_{g,off} = -5$ V in the peak-current formula, leading to a 33% error.
> 3. **Omitting blanking time in DESAT diagrams.** Examiners award a dedicated mark for stating that DESAT is delayed by $t_{blank}$ to avoid false triggering during normal turn-on transients.
> 4. **Using the half-bridge's $V_S$ node incorrectly.** A floating supply (bootstrap) is required only for the *high-side* driver; the low-side has a fixed reference.
> 5. **Mixing up peak current vs. average current.** Peak (amperes, momentary) vs. average (milliamperes, continuous) — both are valid but for *different* design questions.

### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **gate drive circuit** is *not* a simple logic buffer — it is a **power amplifier with isolation, level shifting, and protection**.
- The four mandatory sub-blocks are: **(1) Isolation, (2) Level Shift, (3) Push-Pull Output, (4) Protection (UVLO + DESAT + Miller Clamp)**.
- The **average gate current** is $I_{g,avg} = Q_g \cdot f_{sw}$.
- The **peak gate current** is $I_{g,peak} = (V_{g,on} - V_{g,off}) / R_g$.
- The **gate drive power** is $P_{drive} = V_{g,on} \cdot Q_g \cdot f_{sw}$ — typically tens to hundreds of mW.
- **Bootstrap capacitor** sizing: $C_{boot} \ge Q_g / \Delta V_{boot,max}$ — choose a value **1.5× to 2×** the calculated minimum.
- **Negative gate bias** ($-5$ V to $-9$ V) is essential in half-bridge configurations to prevent **dv/dt-induced spurious turn-on** via the Miller capacitor.
- **UVLO** disables the driver if $V_{CC}$ falls below ~12 V to prevent the IGBT from operating in the *linear region* (high power dissipation).
- **DESAT** detects short-circuit desaturation by monitoring $V_{CE}$ during ON state and triggers a **soft turn-off** within a few microseconds.
- **Isolation choices:** Optocouplers (5 kV, low CMTI), pulse transformers (high CMTI, limited duty cycle), digital isolators (high CMTI, DC-capable, expensive).
- **Switching loss** $P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off})$ — driver switching speed directly impacts converter efficiency.
- For KTU Module 1, always label **at least three functional blocks** in your diagram and mention **gate charge** explicitly in any calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT747 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thyristor Protection Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ THYRISTOR PROTECTION CIRCUITS — SECTION 1: CORE FOUNDATION

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PERUT747 — Module 1):**
> *Thyristor protection circuits* encompass the auxiliary passive and active networks connected around a Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR) to safeguard it from abnormal electrical, thermal, and electromagnetic stresses that exceed its Safe Operating Area (SOA). These circuits ensure the device operates reliably within its datasheet ratings, preventing **latch-up failures**, **secondary breakdown**, and **thermal runaway**.

---

## 1.1 Why Does a Thyristor Need Protection? — The Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **glass window** in your house. The window can comfortably handle a normal breeze (rated wind speed) and a normal push. But if a sudden storm arrives (voltage spike) or someone throws a cricket ball (current surge), the glass shatters. To protect it, you install:

- A **storm shutter** (overvoltage protection / snubber).
- A **protective grill** (overcurrent protection / fuse).
- A **sunshade** (thermal protection / heat sink).

A thyristor is a fragile, four-layer (PNPN) semiconductor switch. Although robust in steady state, it has **strict dynamic limits** on voltage, current, and rate of rise that, if violated, cause permanent destruction. Protection circuits are those "storm shutters."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle of Protection:**
> Protection circuits do NOT change the *steady-state* operation of the thyristor. They only intervene during **abnormal transient events** (switching transients, faults, surges) to absorb or divert excess energy away from the SCR.

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Thyristor Protection

| Protection Domain | What It Guards Against | Typical Threat | Primary Protective Element |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Overvoltage** | `V_AK > V_DRM` / `V_RRM` | Lightning, supply transients, switching spikes | Snubber (R-C), MOV, Avalanche diode |
| **Overcurrent** | `I_T > I_T(AV)` | Load short-circuit, converter overload | Semiconductor fuse, MCB, Current-limiting reactor |
| **Thermal** | `T_j > T_j(max)` (typically **125 °C** to **150 °C**) | Conduction losses, switching losses, ambient heat | Heat sink, forced air cooling, thermal switch |

Additionally, two **rate-effect protections** are critical:

- **`dv/dt` Protection** — Prevents false triggering due to rapid anode voltage rise.
- **`di/dt` Protection** — Prevents localized hotspot damage during turn-on.

---

## 1.3 Key Thyristor Ratings (Recap for Protection Context)

For a KTU 2024 student, the following datasheet limits define the *boundary conditions* for every protection design equation:

- **`V_DRM`** — Repetitive peak off-state forward voltage.
- **`V_RRM`** — Repetitive peak reverse voltage.
- **`I_T(AV)`** — Average on-state current.
- **`I_T(RMS)`** — RMS on-state current.
- **`I^2 t`** — Surge current withstand integral (for fuse co-ordination).
- **`dv/dt (critical)`** — Critical rate of rise of off-state voltage (typically **50–1000 V/µs**).
- **`di/dt (critical)`** — Critical rate of rise of on-state current (typically **50–500 A/µs**).
- **`T_j(max)`** — Maximum permissible junction temperature (**125 °C** for standard SCRs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> This section dissects every protection scheme into its **operating principle**, **design logic**, and **mathematical model**. Every formula listed here is a confirmed KTU exam-fetching equation.

---

## 2.1 dv/dt Protection

### 🔹 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

A thyristor in the blocking (off) state acts as a **junction capacitor** ($J_2$ junction is reverse-biased). This capacitance $C_j$ is non-linear and decreases as reverse voltage increases.

1. A rapidly rising anode voltage ($\frac{dv}{dt}$) injects a **displacement current** through the junction capacitance: $i = C_j \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$.
2. This current flows through the **forward-biased $J_3$ junction**, acting exactly like a gate current.
3. If $\frac{dv}{dt} > \left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_{critical}$, the displacement current exceeds the minimum gate triggering current ($I_{GT}$), and the SCR turns ON spuriously (false turn-on / *commutation failure*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **dv/dt protection prevents false (unintended) turn-on, NOT a voltage-magnitude failure.**

### 🔹 dv/dt Protection Method

Connect a **shunt resistor $R_s$** across the SCR (i.e., between anode and cathode). The resistor provides a low-impedance path that bypasses the displacement current away from the $J_3$ junction.

$$\frac{dv}{dt}\bigg|_{protected} = \frac{V_{BO}}{R_s \cdot C_j}$$

Where:
- $V_{BO}$ = Breakover voltage of the SCR.
- $C_j$ = Junction capacitance (depends on blocking voltage).
- $R_s$ = Shunt protection resistance (typically **10 Ω to 500 Ω**).

**Trade-off:** Lower $R_s$ ⇒ Better dv/dt protection ⇒ Higher continuous power loss ($I_{leak}^2 R_s$). A typical compromise is **100 Ω, 5 W** wirewound.

---

## 2.2 Overvoltage Protection (Snubber Circuits)

### 🔹 Why Overvoltage Occurs

- **Source transients:** Lightning strikes, transformer switching.
- **Commutation transients:** When the SCR turns off, the load current cannot change instantaneously (inductive load), causing the voltage across the device to spike.

### 🔹 RC Snubber Circuit (Most Common)

The R-C snubber is connected **in parallel with the SCR**. It works in two phases:

1. **During turn-off (forward blocking):** The capacitor $C_s$ charges to the supply voltage, absorbing the rapid voltage rise ($\frac{dv}{dt}$) — essentially a *turn-off snubber*.
2. **During turn-on:** The capacitor discharges through $R_s$ into the SCR, which limits the $\frac{di}{dt}$ — essentially a *turn-on snubber*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A properly designed snubber serves **both dv/dt and di/dt protection simultaneously**, making it the most cost-effective and widely used protection element in thyristor circuits.

### 🔹 Snubber Design Equations

**Snubber Capacitor (to limit dv/dt):**

$$C_s = \frac{I_{TM} \cdot t_{rr}}{V_{DM} - V_{TM}}$$

Where:
- $I_{TM}$ = Peak thyristor current at turn-off.
- $t_{rr}$ = Reverse recovery time of the SCR.
- $V_{DM}$ = Maximum allowable peak voltage across SCR.
- $V_{TM}$ = Maximum allowable on-state voltage drop (typically **1.5 V to 2.5 V**).

**Snubber Resistor (to damp oscillations):**

$$R_s = \frac{V_s}{C_s \cdot \left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_{max}}$$

A common design rule for the time constant:

$$\tau = R_s \cdot C_s = \frac{T}{2} \quad \text{to} \quad \frac{T}{3}$$

where $T$ is the period of the supply (e.g., **20 ms** for 50 Hz).

**Snubber Power Dissipation (continuous duty):**

$$P_{R_s} = C_s \cdot V_s^2 \cdot f$$

Where $V_s$ = supply RMS voltage and $f$ = supply frequency.

> [!TIP]
> For *high-frequency* applications (e.g., choppers, inverters), the snubber power dissipation becomes significant. Designers must choose non-inductive, carbon-composition or metal-film resistors rated for the **calculated $P_{R_s}$** continuously.

---

## 2.3 Overcurrent Protection

### 🔹 Semiconductor (High-Speed) Fuse

The **I²t rating** of the fuse must be **less than the I²t rating of the SCR** to ensure the fuse blows first:

$$\left(I^2 t\right)_{fuse} < \left(I^2 t\right)_{SCR}$$

This co-ordination ensures the fuse clears the fault *before* the thyristor's thermal limit is breached.

### 🔹 Current-Limiting Reactor

A series inductor $L_s$ limits the rate of rise of fault current:

$$\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{V_s}{L_s}$$

**Design rule:** $L_s$ is chosen so that under the worst short-circuit, $\frac{di}{dt}$ stays below the SCR's rated value (typically **50 A/µs** for medium-power SCRs).

---

## 2.4 Thermal Protection (Heat Sink Design)

The heat sink must dissipate the total power loss:

$$P_{loss} = V_{TM} \cdot I_{T(AV)} + P_{switching}$$

For steady-state design:

$$\theta_{JA} = \frac{T_{j(max)} - T_A}{P_{loss}}$$

Where:
- $T_{j(max)}$ = **125 °C** (typical SCR max junction temperature).
- $T_A$ = Ambient temperature (typically **40 °C** in Indian tropical conditions).
- $\theta_{JA}$ = Total thermal resistance from junction to ambient.

The thermal resistance network is **series additive** (1-D heat flow):

$$\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$$

Where:
- $\theta_{JC}$ = Junction-to-case thermal resistance (given in datasheet, e.g., **0.15 °C/W**).
- $\theta_{CS}$ = Case-to-sink thermal resistance (mica insulator ≈ **0.2 °C/W**).
- $\theta_{SA}$ = Sink-to-ambient thermal resistance (to be designed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For natural convection, $\theta_{SA} \approx \frac{1}{h \cdot A}$ where $h \approx 10 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ and $A$ is the heat sink surface area. Forced air cooling can reduce $\theta_{SA}$ by **5× to 10×**.

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| # | Formula | Meaning / Design Use | Boundary / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i_{disp} = C_j \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$ | Displacement current causing false triggering | Amperes |
| 2 | $R_s = \frac{V_{BO}}{(dv/dt)_{crit} \cdot C_j}$ | dv/dt protection resistor | Ω |
| 3 | $C_s = \frac{I_{TM} \cdot t_{rr}}{V_{DM} - V_{TM}}$ | Snubber capacitance | Farads |
| 4 | $R_s = \frac{V_s}{C_s \cdot (dv/dt)_{max}}$ | Snubber resistance | Ω |
| 5 | $P_{R_s} = C_s \cdot V_s^2 \cdot f$ | Continuous snubber resistor loss | Watts |
| 6 | $R_s C_s = T/2$ to $T/3$ | Time-constant design rule | Seconds |
| 7 | $\left(I^2 t\right)_{fuse} < \left(I^2 t\right)_{SCR}$ | Fuse co-ordination | A²·s |
| 8 | $L_s = \frac{V_s}{(di/dt)_{max}}$ | Current-limiting inductance | Henries |
| 9 | $\theta_{JA} = \frac{T_{j(max)} - T_A}{P_{loss}}$ | Required total thermal resistance | °C/W |
| 10 | $\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$ | Series thermal resistance model | °C/W |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are the **gold standard** for KTU 2024 numerical problems. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: dv/dt Protection Resistor Value

**Problem Context:** A thyristor has junction capacitance $C_j = 0.05 \,\mu F$ at the operating voltage, breakover voltage $V_{BO} = 800 \,V$, and critical $\frac{dv}{dt} = 200 \,V/\mu s$. Find the value of the shunt protection resistor $R_s$.

**Step 1: Start from the dv/dt constraint.**
For protection, the displacement current must not exceed the leakage level corresponding to the critical slope.

$$\frac{dv}{dt}\bigg|_{crit} = \frac{V_{BO}}{R_s \cdot C_j}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange for $R_s$.**

$$R_s = \frac{V_{BO}}{(dv/dt)_{crit} \cdot C_j}$$

**Step 3: Substitute SI-consistent values.**

- $V_{BO} = 800 \,V$
- $\left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_{crit} = 200 \,V/\mu s = 200 \times 10^{6} \,V/s$
- $C_j = 0.05 \,\mu F = 0.05 \times 10^{-6} \,F = 5 \times 10^{-8} \,F$

**Step 4: Numerator and Denominator calculation.**

$$R_s = \frac{800}{(200 \times 10^{6}) \times (5 \times 10^{-8})}$$

$$R_s = \frac{800}{10}$$

$$\boxed{R_s = 80 \,\Omega}$$

**Step 5: Sanity check on continuous power loss.**

Assuming leakage current $I_L = 5 \,mA$:

$$P_{R_s} = I_L^2 \cdot R_s = (5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 80 = 2 \times 10^{-3} \,W = 2 \,mW$$

A standard **¼ W** resistor is more than sufficient. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RC Snubber Design for a 50 Hz Phase-Controlled Rectifier

**Given:**
- Supply: $V_s = 230 \,V$, 50 Hz
- Maximum repetitive peak off-state voltage: $V_{DM} = 600 \,V$
- SCR reverse recovery time: $t_{rr} = 50 \,\mu s$
- SCR peak on-state current at turn-off: $I_{TM} = 100 \,A$
- Maximum allowable dv/dt: $1000 \,V/\mu s$

**Step 1: Compute snubber capacitance.**

$$C_s = \frac{I_{TM} \cdot t_{rr}}{V_{DM} - V_{TM}}$$

Taking $V_{TM} \approx 1.5 \,V$ (small, often ignored, but we keep it for precision):

$$C_s = \frac{100 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{600 - 1.5}$$

$$C_s = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{598.5}$$

$$C_s = 8.354 \times 10^{-6} \,F$$

$$\boxed{C_s \approx 8.35 \,\mu F}$$

**Step 2: Compute snubber resistance from dv/dt constraint.**

$$R_s = \frac{V_s}{C_s \cdot (dv/dt)_{max}}$$

$$R_s = \frac{230}{(8.354 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1000 \times 10^{6})}$$

$$R_s = \frac{230}{8354}$$

$$\boxed{R_s \approx 27.5 \,\Omega}$$

**Step 3: Verify the time-constant rule of thumb.**

$$R_s \cdot C_s = 27.5 \times 8.35 \times 10^{-6} = 2.296 \times 10^{-4} \,s = 229.6 \,\mu s$$

The supply period: $T = 1/50 = 20 \,ms = 20{,}000 \,\mu s$.

Check: $T/3 = 6667 \,\mu s$. Our $\tau = 229.6 \,\mu s \ll T/3$. ✅

**Step 4: Compute snubber resistor power dissipation.**

$$P_{R_s} = C_s \cdot V_s^2 \cdot f$$

$$P_{R_s} = 8.354 \times 10^{-6} \times (230)^2 \times 50$$

$$P_{R_s} = 8.354 \times 10^{-6} \times 52900 \times 50$$

$$P_{R_s} = 22.097 \,W$$

$$\boxed{P_{R_s} \approx 22.1 \,W}$$

**Engineering Decision:** Use a **30 Ω, 30 W** wirewound resistor (with 50% de-rating margin for reliability). Use a **10 µF, 630 V** metallized polypropylene capacitor.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Sink Thermal Resistance

**Given:**
- SCR datasheet: $\theta_{JC} = 0.15 \,°C/W$
- Mica insulator: $\theta_{CS} = 0.20 \,°C/W$
- Maximum junction temperature: $T_{j(max)} = 125 \,°C$
- Ambient: $T_A = 40 \,°C$
- Power dissipation: $P_{loss} = 50 \,W$

**Step 1: Required total thermal resistance.**

$$\theta_{JA} = \frac{T_{j(max)} - T_A}{P_{loss}} = \frac{125 - 40}{50} = \frac{85}{50} = 1.7 \,°C/W$$

**Step 2: Solve for sink-to-ambient resistance.**

$$\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$$

$$1.7 = 0.15 + 0.20 + \theta_{SA}$$

$$\theta_{SA} = 1.7 - 0.35 = 1.35 \,°C/W$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{SA} \leq 1.35 \,°C/W}$$

**Step 3: Sizing the heat sink (natural convection).**

For natural convection with $h \approx 10 \,W/m^2K$:

$$A = \frac{1}{h \cdot \theta_{SA}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 1.35} = 0.0741 \,m^2 = 741 \,cm^2$$

**Conclusion:** A black-anodized aluminum heat sink of approximately **$30 \text{ cm} \times 25 \text{ cm}$** with fins is required. For compact applications, forced-air cooling at **2 m/s** can reduce the area to **~100 cm²**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams map the **functional architecture** of thyristor protection, including the RC snubber, dv/dt shunt, fuse, and thermal network. They are optimized for the KTU board-rendering pipeline.

---

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Complete Protection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[AC Supply V_s] --> F1[Semiconductor Fuse - I²t co-ordinated]
    F1 --> LR[Current Limiting Reactor L_s]
    LR --> ANODE[(Thyristor Anode)]
    GATE1[Gate Drive] --> GATE2[(Gate Cathode)]
    SNUBB[RC Snubber R_s C_s]:::snubclass
    DVDT[Shunt Resistor R_sh]:::dvdtclass
    HS[Heat Sink - thermal path]:::thermalclass
    SCRBODY[Thyristor PNPN Stack]:::scrclass

    ANODE --- SNUBB
    ANODE --- DVDT
    ANODE --- SCRBODY
    GATE2 --- SCRBODY
    SCRBODY --> CAT[(Cathode)]
    SCRBODY --> HS

    classDef scrclass fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef snubclass fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dvdtclass fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef thermalclass fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Series elements** (Fuse + Reactor) protect against **overcurrent**.
- **Parallel elements** (Snubber + Shunt) protect against **overvoltage and dv/dt**.
- **Heat sink** is a **thermal parallel path** to ambient.

---

## 4.2 RC Snubber — Energy Flow State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Conducting: SCR ON - I_L flows
    Conducting --> TurningOff: Gate pulse removed
    TurningOff --> Blocking: SCR enters off-state
    Blocking --> Charging: C_s charges via load L
    Charging --> SteadyBlock: V_C reaches V_s
    SteadyBlock --> SCRTriggered: Gate ON for next cycle
    SCRTriggered --> Discharging: C_s discharges through R_s and SCR
    Discharging --> Conducting: Energy dissipated in R_s
    Conducting --> [*]
```

**State descriptions:**
- **Charging state:** The snubber capacitor absorbs the energy trapped in the load inductance — this prevents voltage spike across the SCR.
- **Discharging state:** When the SCR next turns on, the stored energy in $C_s$ is dissipated safely in $R_s$ — this limits $\frac{di}{dt}$ through the device.

---

## 4.3 Thermal Equivalent Circuit (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Power Source P_loss] --> J[Junction T_j]
    J --> RJC[θ_JC - die to case]
    RJC --> C[Case T_C]
    C --> RCS[θ_CS - case to sink]
    RCS --> S[Sink T_S]
    S --> RSA[θ_SA - sink to ambient]
    RSA --> A[Ambient T_A]
    A --> Q[Heat dissipated to air]

    classDef hot fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef warm fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:1px
    classDef cool fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    class P,J hot
    class C,S warm
    class A,Q,RJC,RCS,RSA cool
```

> [!TIP]
> This thermal ladder is the **electrical analog** of heat flow. Power source = current, temperature = voltage, thermal resistance = electrical resistance. All thermal resistances add in **series** for 1-D heat flow.

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modelled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: 3-mark direct questions (Part A) and 14-mark analytical questions (Part B) with internal choice.

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define dv/dt protection of a thyristor. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**
dv/dt protection refers to the technique of preventing **unintended (false) turn-on** of a thyristor due to a rapid rate of rise of anode voltage during the blocking state. The junction capacitance $C_j$ of the reverse-biased $J_2$ junction allows a displacement current $i = C_j \cdot (dv/dt)$ to flow; if this current exceeds the gate trigger current $I_{GT}$, the SCR turns on spuriously. A **shunt resistor $R_s$** across the device bypasses this displacement current and prevents false triggering. It is necessary because it eliminates commutation failures and converter maloperation.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List the various protection requirements of a thyristor. (Write any 4)**

**Model Answer:**
The four main protection requirements of a thyristor are:
1. **dv/dt protection** — to prevent false turn-on due to rapid voltage rise.
2. **Overvoltage protection** — using RC snubbers to limit transient voltage spikes.
3. **Overcurrent protection** — using high-speed fuses and current-limiting reactors.
4. **Thermal protection** — using heat sinks to keep the junction temperature below **125 °C**.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Snubber Design Pitfall):**
> A common student mistake is to **forget the units conversion** in the dv/dt term. The value `1000 V/µs` MUST be converted to `1000 × 10⁶ V/s` before substituting. Also, students often compute the **snubber resistor power $P_{R_s}$** and forget to apply the **safety factor of 1.5× to 2×** when selecting the resistor wattage — this causes the resistor to overheat and open-circuit in the lab. Always state the final selection with a safety margin.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the operation of an **RC snubber circuit** for thyristor protection. Derive the expression for the snubber capacitance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thyristor in a 50 Hz, 230 V controlled rectifier circuit has the following ratings: $V_{DM} = 600 \,V$, $I_{TM} = 80 \,A$ (at turn-off), $t_{rr} = 40 \,\mu s$, and $(dv/dt)_{max} = 500 \,V/\mu s$. Design a suitable RC snubber circuit and calculate the power dissipation in the snubber resistor. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) RC Snubber Operation and Derivation:**

*Operation:*
- During the **off state** of the thyristor, the capacitor $C_s$ charges to the supply voltage through the load. When the load is inductive, the current through the load cannot change instantaneously, and the capacitor absorbs the energy, limiting the rate of rise of voltage across the SCR.
- When the SCR is **triggered ON**, the charged capacitor discharges through $R_s$ and the SCR, dissipating its energy as heat. This limits the rate of rise of anode current ($\frac{di}{dt}$) through the device.

*Derivation of $C_s$:*
Let $I_{TM}$ be the peak current just before turn-off. After turn-off, this current charges the capacitor from $V_{TM}$ to $V_{DM}$ during the reverse recovery time $t_{rr}$.

Charge supplied by the SCR current to the capacitor:

$$Q = I_{TM} \cdot t_{rr}$$

Voltage rise across the capacitor:

$$\Delta V = V_{DM} - V_{TM}$$

Since $Q = C_s \cdot \Delta V$:

$$C_s \cdot (V_{DM} - V_{TM}) = I_{TM} \cdot t_{rr}$$

$$\boxed{C_s = \frac{I_{TM} \cdot t_{rr}}{V_{DM} - V_{TM}}}$$

**(b) Numerical Design:**

*Step 1: Snubber capacitance.*

$$C_s = \frac{80 \times 40 \times 10^{-6}}{600 - 1.5} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-3}}{598.5} = 5.347 \times 10^{-6} \,F$$

$$\boxed{C_s \approx 5.35 \,\mu F}$$

*Step 2: Snubber resistance from dv/dt constraint.*

$$R_s = \frac{V_s}{C_s \cdot (dv/dt)_{max}} = \frac{230}{5.347 \times 10^{-6} \times 500 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$R_s = \frac{230}{2673.5} = 0.0860 \,\Omega$$

*Step 3: Snubber resistor power dissipation.*

$$P_{R_s} = C_s \cdot V_s^2 \cdot f = 5.347 \times 10^{-6} \times (230)^2 \times 50$$

$$P_{R_s} = 5.347 \times 10^{-6} \times 52900 \times 50 = 14.143 \,W$$

*Step 4: Final design selection (with 1.5× safety margin).*

- **$C_s$ chosen:** $5.5 \,\mu F$ / **630 V** metallized polypropylene.
- **$R_s$ chosen:** $0.1 \,\Omega$ / **25 W** (≥ 1.5 × 14.14 W) wirewound.
- **$P_{R_s} \approx 14.14 \,W$** continuous.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the necessity of **heat sink** for a thyristor. Derive the expression for thermal resistance from junction to ambient and explain the heat-flow model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SCR has the following thermal data: $\theta_{JC} = 0.18 \,°C/W$, $\theta_{CS} = 0.22 \,°C/W$, $T_{j(max)} = 125 \,°C$, $T_A = 40 \,°C$, $P_{loss} = 60 \,W$. Calculate: (i) total thermal resistance $\theta_{JA}$, (ii) sink-to-ambient thermal resistance $\theta_{SA}$, and (iii) approximate heat sink surface area for natural convection ($h = 10 \,W/m^2K$). **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Heat Sink Necessity and Thermal Model:**

A thyristor dissipates power during conduction ($V_{TM} \cdot I_T$) and switching. If this heat is not removed, the junction temperature $T_j$ rises. When $T_j$ exceeds $T_{j(max)} = 125 \,°C$, the device fails thermally. A heat sink provides a **low thermal-resistance path** from the SCR case to the ambient, ensuring $T_j$ stays within safe limits.

*Heat-Flow Model:*
Heat generated at the junction flows via three **series** thermal resistances to the ambient:

$$\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$$

Heat flow equation (analogous to Ohm's law for thermal systems):

$$P_{loss} = \frac{T_j - T_A}{\theta_{JA}}$$

Hence:

$$\theta_{JA} = \frac{T_j - T_A}{P_{loss}}$$

The required heat-sink thermal resistance is therefore:

$$\theta_{SA} = \frac{T_{j(max)} - T_A}{P_{loss}} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$$

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

*(i) Total thermal resistance:*

$$\theta_{JA} = \frac{125 - 40}{60} = \frac{85}{60} = 1.4167 \,°C/W$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{JA} \approx 1.42 \,°C/W}$$

*(ii) Sink-to-ambient thermal resistance:*

$$\theta_{SA} = 1.4167 - 0.18 - 0.22 = 1.0167 \,°C/W$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{SA} \approx 1.02 \,°C/W}$$

*(iii) Heat sink area:*

$$A = \frac{1}{h \cdot \theta_{SA}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 1.0167} = 0.0984 \,m^2 = 984 \,cm^2$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 1000 \,cm^2}$$

A black-anodized aluminum heat sink of approximately **$40 \text{ cm} \times 25 \text{ cm}$** with fins is suitable for natural convection. With forced-air cooling (2 m/s), the area reduces to **~100 cm²**.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU Board Revision — must-know bullet points:**

- ✅ Thyristor protection has **three domains**: overvoltage, overcurrent, and thermal.
- ✅ **dv/dt protection** uses a **shunt resistor** $R_s$ across the SCR to bypass displacement current; $R_s = \frac{V_{BO}}{(dv/dt)_{crit} \cdot C_j}$.
- ✅ **RC snubber** = R in series with C, **connected in parallel** with the SCR. Limits both dv/dt (at turn-off) and di/dt (at turn-on).
- ✅ **Snubber capacitor:** $C_s = \frac{I_{TM} \cdot t_{rr}}{V_{DM} - V_{TM}}$.
- ✅ **Snubber resistor power:** $P_{R_s} = C_s \cdot V_s^2 \cdot f$ (continuous, not peak).
- ✅ **Time-constant rule:** $R_s C_s = T/2$ to $T/3$ for line-frequency SCR circuits.
- ✅ **Fuse co-ordination rule:** $(I^2 t)_{fuse} < (I^2 t)_{SCR}$.
- ✅ **Current-limiting reactor:** $L_s = \frac{V_s}{(di/dt)_{max}}$.
- ✅ **Thermal model is a series ladder:** $\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$.
- ✅ **Maximum junction temperature** for standard SCR = **125 °C**.
- ✅ For **natural convection**, $h \approx 10 \,W/m^2K$; for **forced air**, $h$ increases to **25–250 W/m²K**.
- ✅ **Common KTU mistake:** forgetting unit conversion in dv/dt and dv/dt in the snubber resistor formula. Always convert to **SI base units** before substituting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
