topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT747 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Series and Parallel Combinations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Power Electronics, **Series and Parallel Combinations** refer to the deliberate interconnection of two or more Silicon Controlled Rectifiers (SCRs/Thyristors) to overcome the inherent limitations of a single device when handling **high voltages** (series connection) or **high currents** (parallel connection). No single commercially available SCR can indefinitely meet the demands of ultra-high-power HVDC transmission, EHV grids, or large industrial drives; hence, multiple SCRs must be operated as a coordinated **"string"** (series) or **"bank"** (parallel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Students must clearly distinguish between the *motive* for series operation (voltage sharing) and parallel operation (current sharing). KTU examiners frequently test the *derivation* of string efficiency and the *design* of equalizing networks.

- **Series Operation** $\rightarrow$ Used when the required blocking voltage exceeds the rated repetitive peak off-state voltage ($V_{RRM}$) of a single SCR.
- **Parallel Operation** $\rightarrow$ Used when the required load current exceeds the rated average on-state current ($I_{T(AV)}$) of a single SCR.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Brigade"

Imagine a group of people trying to carry a massive slab of stone across a river:
- **Series Combination (Voltage Stacking):** Think of stacking rubber sheets one above another to block a high-pressure water jet. The water pressure (voltage) is shared; each sheet handles only a fraction. If one sheet is slightly weaker (more leakage), the others must compensate. The chain is only as strong as the *weakest* link **plus** the imbalance it causes.
- **Parallel Combination (Current Splitting):** Think of several pipes of slightly different diameters placed side-by-side to drain a flood. The water (current) preferentially flows through the wider pipe (lower on-state drop), overloading it. We must insert *balancing reactors* (think of partial-flow restrictors) to force equitable division.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU):**
> - **String Efficiency ($\eta_s$):** The ratio of actual string voltage to the ideal (sum of individual ratings). Always $\leq 100\%$.
> - **Current Derating Factor:** The ratio of actual total current to the ideal sum. Always $\leq 1$.
> - **Static Equalizing Network:** Resistors across each SCR to equalize **leakage currents** in the OFF state.
> - **Dynamic Equalizing Network:** RC snubbers across each SCR to equalize **dv/dt** and transient voltages during switching.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Combinations Are Needed

A single SCR is constrained by:
1. **Voltage Rating ($V_{RRM}$):** Limited by silicon wafer thickness and junction breakdown physics.
2. **Current Rating ($I_{T(AV)}$):** Limited by thermal dissipation and bonding wire current capacity.
3. **$dv/dt$ and Thermal Limits.**

When system demands exceed these ratings, SCRs are stacked **electrically** (series) or **thermally/current-wise** (parallel).

## 2.2 Series Operation of SCRs — The Logic Chain

When $n$ SCRs are connected in series, ideally each device should block exactly $V_{total}/n$ volts. However, this never happens due to:
- **Mismatch in leakage currents** ($I_L$) — even from the same manufacturing batch.
- **Mismatch in reverse recovery characteristics** during turn-off.
- **Mismatch in junction capacitances** during turn-on.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic:
1. **In the OFF (blocking) state:** The SCR with the **lowest leakage current** blocks the **largest voltage**. If this exceeds its rating, it avalanches and destroys the string. Hence, we must add **static equalizing resistors** ($R$) in parallel with each SCR to swamp out the leakage current differences.
2. **During turn-on and turn-off (transient):** The SCR with the **slowest reverse recovery** sees the highest voltage. Hence, we add **dynamic equalizing networks** (R-C snubbers) in parallel with each SCR.
3. **String Efficiency** quantifies how close we get to the ideal:

$$
\eta_s = \frac{\text{Actual Voltage across the whole String}}{n \times \text{Voltage Rating of one SCR}}
$$

## 2.3 Parallel Operation of SCRs — The Logic Chain

When $n$ SCRs are connected in parallel, ideally each should carry $I_{total}/n$ amps. Mismatches occur due to:
- **Mismatch in on-state voltage drop ($V_T$).**
- **Mismatch in gate triggering thresholds.**
- **Different thermal resistances and junction temperatures.**

### Operating Logic:
1. **Steady state:** The SCR with the **lowest on-state drop** hogs the most current. We use **current-balancing reactors** (mutually coupled inductors) so that any current imbalance induces an opposing EMF that forces equal sharing.
2. **During turn-on:** The SCR triggered first tends to take all the current. We use **simultaneous gate pulses** (with proper pulse transformer isolation) and **small series inductors**.
3. **Current Derating Factor** is the parallel-equivalent of string efficiency:

$$
\text{Current Derating} = \frac{I_{\text{actual total}}}{n \times I_{\text{rated of one SCR}}}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|
| String Efficiency (Series) | $\eta_s = \dfrac{V_{\text{string,actual}}}{n \cdot V_{\text{rated}}}$ | $\eta_s \le 1$ (or $100\%$) |
| String Efficiency (2 SCRs, unequal) | $\eta_s = \dfrac{2 V_{\text{rated}}}{V_1 + V_2}$ | $V_1, V_2$ are actual voltages |
| String Efficiency (deviation form) | $\eta_s = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_{\text{rated}}}$ | $\Delta V$ = deviation from ideal |
| Static Equalizing Resistor | $R = \dfrac{n \cdot V_{\text{rated}} - V_s}{(n-1) \cdot \Delta I_L}$ | $V_s$ = supply voltage; $\Delta I_L$ = max leakage mismatch |
| Voltage across resistor | $V_R = \dfrac{V_s - V_{\text{SCR}}}{R}$ | Used to compute power loss |
| Current Derating (Parallel) | $\eta_p = \dfrac{I_{\text{actual}}}{n \cdot I_{\text{rated}}}$ | $\eta_p \le 1$ |
| String Efficiency (Parallel) | $\eta_p = \dfrac{n \cdot I_{\text{avg}}}{I_1 + I_2 + \dots + I_n}$ | $I_{\text{avg}}$ = rated current |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> - HVDC converter stations in India (e.g., the $\pm 800$ kV Raigarh–Pugalur line) use hundreds of SCRs in series to block megavolts.
> - Large electrolysis plants and aluminum smelters use parallel SCRs to handle tens of thousands of amps at lower voltages.
> - Static VAR Compensators (SVC) and Thyristor Controlled Series Capacitors (TCSC) in grid applications rely entirely on these string/bank principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: String Efficiency of Two SCRs in Series

**Problem Setup:** Two SCRs, $S_1$ and $S_2$, are connected in series. Each is rated for a forward/reverse blocking voltage of $V_{\text{rated}}$. The string must block a total DC supply voltage $V_s$.

**Ideal Case (No mismatch):** Each SCR would block exactly $V_s / 2$, and the maximum blockable string voltage would be $2 V_{\text{rated}}$, giving $\eta_s = 100\%$.

**Practical Case (Mismatch):** Due to differing leakage characteristics, the voltage shared is unequal. Let $S_1$ block $V_1$ and $S_2$ block $V_2$ such that $V_1 > V_2$ (because $S_1$ has lower leakage current).

**Step 1 — Define the average (ideal) voltage per SCR:**
For balanced operation, each SCR should ideally block $V_s / 2$. Hence the ideal rating condition is:
$$
V_{\text{rated}} \geq \frac{V_s}{2} \implies V_s \leq 2 V_{\text{rated}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the deviation:**
Let the actual blocked voltages be $V_1$ and $V_2$ such that:
$$
V_1 = \frac{V_s}{2} + \Delta V \quad \text{and} \quad V_2 = \frac{V_s}{2} - \Delta V
$$
where $\Delta V$ is the deviation from the ideal share caused by device mismatch.

**Step 3 — Apply the safety constraint:**
For the string not to fail, the *more stressed* SCR ($S_1$) must NOT exceed its rating:
$$
V_1 \leq V_{\text{rated}}
$$
Substituting the expression for $V_1$:
$$
\frac{V_s}{2} + \Delta V \leq V_{\text{rated}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum safe string voltage:**
$$
\frac{V_s}{2} \leq V_{\text{rated}} - \Delta V
$$
$$
V_{s,\max} = 2 \left( V_{\text{rated}} - \Delta V \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the String Efficiency:**
By definition, string efficiency is the ratio of the actual usable string voltage to the ideal sum of individual ratings:
$$
\eta_s = \frac{V_{s,\max}}{2 \cdot V_{\text{rated}}}
$$
Substituting $V_{s,\max}$:
$$
\eta_s = \frac{2 \left( V_{\text{rated}} - \Delta V \right)}{2 \cdot V_{\text{rated}}}
$$
$$
\eta_s = \frac{V_{\text{rated}} - \Delta V}{V_{\text{rated}}}
$$
$$
\boxed{\eta_s = 1 - \frac{\Delta V}{V_{\text{rated}}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** As the mismatch $\Delta V$ increases (worse device uniformity), the string efficiency drops linearly. This is why KTU emphasizes the use of carefully matched SCRs and equalizing networks.

**Generalized Form for $n$ SCRs in Series:**
For $n$ identical SCRs, the generalized string efficiency formula becomes:
$$
\eta_s = \frac{n \cdot V_{\text{rated}} - (n-1) \cdot \Delta V_{\max}}{n \cdot V_{\text{rated}}}
$$
where $\Delta V_{\max}$ is the worst-case voltage deviation of any one SCR from the average.

## 3.2 Derivation: Static Equalizing Resistor Design

**Goal:** To choose the value of the resistor $R$ connected in parallel with each SCR so that the voltage sharing becomes uniform.

**Setup:** Consider $n$ SCRs in series, each with leakage current $I_L$ (with a maximum mismatch $\Delta I_L$ between the highest and lowest leakage device). Each SCR is bypassed by a resistor $R$.

**Step 1 — Total current through the resistor-SCR branch:**
The current through each parallel combination of ($R$ + SCR) must be equal in steady state, because the SCRs are in series.

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law across the string:**
For a uniform voltage $V_{\text{SCR}}$ across each SCR, the resistor drops the rest:
$$
V_R = \frac{V_s - V_{\text{SCR}}}{1} \quad \text{(for the whole string)}
$$
For each individual SCR, the voltage across its parallel resistor is:
$$
V_R = V_{\text{SCR}} + (I_R \cdot R)
$$

**Step 3 — Equalize the leakage current difference:**
The difference in voltage across two SCRs is governed by the leakage current mismatch. To make the voltage equal, the resistor current must dominate the leakage current:
$$
R \leq \frac{n \cdot V_{\text{rated}} - V_s}{(n-1) \cdot \Delta I_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical Design Equation:**
$$
\boxed{R = \frac{V_s \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)}{\Delta I_L} = \frac{(n-1) \cdot V_s}{n \cdot \Delta I_L}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The smaller the leakage current mismatch $\Delta I_L$, the *larger* the allowed $R$, which is good because large $R$ means less power dissipation loss. Always specify $R$ with a power rating of at least $V_R^2 / R$.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Series Combination | Parallel Combination |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Increase voltage handling | Increase current handling |
| Main Mismatch Cause | Leakage current ($I_L$) | On-state voltage drop ($V_T$) |
| Equalizing Element | Static $R$ + Dynamic $R$-$C$ snubber | Current-balancing reactor (coupled inductor) |
| Efficiency Definition | $\eta_s = V_{\text{actual}} / (n \cdot V_{\text{rated}})$ | $\eta_p = I_{\text{actual}} / (n \cdot I_{\text{rated}})$ |
| Failure Mode | SCR with lowest $I_L$ avalanches | SCR with lowest $V_T$ overheats |
| Typical Application | HVDC, EHV grid converters | High-current rectifiers, electrolysis |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Series String with Equalizing Networks

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a series string of SCRs with both static and dynamic equalizing networks. The diagram maps the *signal flow* and *voltage equalization paths* rather than a literal physical circuit, in accordance with the V10 safeguard for non-physical schematics.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HV([High Voltage DC Bus Vs]):::input
    
    subgraph SCRString["Series SCR String - Functional Block"]
        direction TB
        SCR1["SCR T1 with Static R1 and Dynamic RC1 Snubber in Parallel"]:::dev
        SCR2["SCR T2 with Static R2 and Dynamic RC2 Snubber in Parallel"]:::dev
        SCR3["SCR T3 with Static R3 and Dynamic RC3 Snubber in Parallel"]:::dev
        SCRn["SCR Tn with Static Rn and Dynamic RCn Snubber in Parallel"]:::dev
        
        SCR1 --> SCR2 --> SCR3 --> SCRn
    end
    
    subgraph EqNet["Equalization Signal Flow"]
        direction LR
        Vshare["Voltage Sharing Logic: V/T1 ≈ V/T2 ≈ V/T3 ≈ V/Tn"]:::logic
        Istat["Static Current Path: through Resistors R1, R2, R3, Rn"]:::path
        Idyn["Dynamic Current Path: through Snubber Capacitors C1, C2, C3, Cn during dv/dt"]:::path
    end
    
    LV([Load or Next Stage]):::output
    
    HV --> SCRString
    SCRString --> LV
    
    SCR1 -.-> Vshare
    SCR2 -.-> Vshare
    SCR3 -.-> Vshare
    SCRn -.-.-> Vshare
    
    Vshare --> Istat
    Vshare --> Idyn
    
    classDef input fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#b4d8ff,stroke:#003d99,color:#000
    classDef dev fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef path fill:#f0d4f4,stroke:#660066,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Parallel Bank with Current Balancing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC([DC or AC Source]):::input
    
    subgraph PARBank["Parallel SCR Bank - Functional Block"]
        direction TB
        P1["SCR P1 with Series Inductor L1"]:::dev
        P2["SCR P2 with Series Inductor L2"]:::dev
        P3["SCR P3 with Series Inductor L3"]:::dev
        
        P1 --- P2 --- P3
    end
    
    subgraph BalReact["Current Balancing Reactor - Mutual Coupling"]
        direction LR
        CM["Common Magnetic Core: L1, L2, L3 Mutually Coupled"]:::logic
        EMFinduced["Induced EMF Opposes Current Imbalance: di1/dt - di2/dt"]:::logic
    end
    
    LOAD([High Current Load]):::output
    
    SRC --> PARBank
    PARBank --> LOAD
    
    P1 -.-> CM
    P2 -.-> CM
    P3 -.-> CM
    CM --> EMFinduced
    EMFinduced -.-> P1
    EMFinduced -.-> P2
    EMFinduced -.-> P3
    
    classDef input fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#b4d8ff,stroke:#003d99,color:#000
    classDef dev fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Conceptual State Machine — Voltage Sharing in a Series String

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Blocking: SCRs OFF, Vs Applied
    Blocking --> Uniform: Ideal Match - I_L Equal
    Blocking --> Unequal: Mismatch - Some SCR Stressed
    Unequal --> AvalancheRisk: V_stressed exceeds V_rated
    Unequal --> Safe: Static R Equalizes I_L
    Safe --> Transient: Turn on or Turn off
    Transient --> DynamicSafe: RC Snubber Limits dv/dt
    DynamicSafe --> Conducting: All SCRs ON
    Conducting --> Blocking: Vs Removed
    AvalancheRisk --> [*]: Device Failure
    Conducting --> [*]: Normal Shutdown
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define string efficiency. Why is it always less than 100% in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
String efficiency is defined as the ratio of the actual voltage that can be blocked by a series string of SCRs to the sum of the individual voltage ratings of the SCRs:
$$
\eta_s = \frac{V_{\text{string, actual}}}{n \cdot V_{\text{rated}}}
$$
It is always less than 100% because the SCRs in a series string do not have identical leakage current characteristics (even from the same lot). The SCR with the **lowest leakage current** blocks a disproportionately **larger voltage**, forcing the string voltage to be derated below the theoretical sum to prevent avalanche failure of the weakest-stressed device. **(1 mark — definition, 2 marks — explanation with cause).**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the function of (i) a static equalizing resistor, and (ii) a dynamic equalizing network, in a series-connected SCR string.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
(i) **Static equalizing resistor (1 mark):** A resistor connected in parallel with each SCR in the series string. Its function is to swamp out the differences in **leakage currents** of the SCRs during the steady OFF (blocking) state, thereby forcing nearly equal voltage sharing.

(ii) **Dynamic equalizing network (2 marks):** A resistor-capacitor (R-C) snubber connected in parallel with each SCR. Its function is to equalize the **transient voltage distribution** during the turn-on and turn-off periods, particularly protecting the SCR that recovers last during commutation from a sudden overvoltage spike.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In string efficiency derivations, students frequently forget to state the **physical assumption** (e.g., "all SCRs rated for $V_{\text{rated}}$" and "mismatch is small"). Always state assumptions before deriving. **(Loss of 2 marks if omitted.)**
> - In current derating problems, students often mix up the rated current with the actual current. Read the question carefully: "rated" means the device datasheet value. **(Loss of 1–2 marks.)**
> - Always draw the **equalizing network** when asked. Marks are reserved for the circuit. **(Loss of 2–3 marks if skipped.)**

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified Past Pattern]**

**Two SCRs, each rated for 600 V, are to be used in series to block 1100 V DC. Due to manufacturing tolerances, one SCR has a leakage current of 4 mA and the other has 6 mA at the rated voltage. The static equalizing resistors used have a tolerance such that they are also mismatched by 5%.**
**(a)** Derive the expression for the string efficiency in terms of the device voltage deviation. Hence, compute the string efficiency of the two SCRs. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Determine the value of the static equalizing resistor $R$ that must be placed across each SCR to ensure equal voltage sharing, and calculate the total power loss in these resistors. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation + Computation (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Let the two SCRs share voltages $V_1$ and $V_2$ such that $V_1 + V_2 = 1100$ V.
Ideally, $V_1 = V_2 = 550$ V. Define the deviation $\Delta V$ such that $V_1 = 550 + \Delta V$ and $V_2 = 550 - \Delta V$.

For safety:
$$
V_1 \leq 600 \implies 550 + \Delta V \leq 600 \implies \Delta V_{\max} = 50 \text{ V}
$$

Hence the maximum safe string voltage:
$$
V_{s,\max} = 2(V_{\text{rated}} - \Delta V) = 2(600 - 50) = 1100 \text{ V}
$$

String efficiency:
$$
\eta_s = 1 - \frac{\Delta V}{V_{\text{rated}}} = 1 - \frac{50}{600} = 0.9167 = 91.67\%
$$

**Computation (3 marks):**
Since $\Delta I_L = 6 - 4 = 2$ mA, and the leakage currents are approximately proportional to voltage in the blocking region, the voltage deviation is:
$$
\Delta V = 550 \times \frac{\Delta I_L}{I_{L,\text{avg}}} = 550 \times \frac{2}{5} = 220 \text{ V (this exceeds 50 V, hence the string is unsafe without equalization)}
$$

After adding equalizing resistors, the effective $\Delta I_L$ is reduced by a factor of $(I_R / \Delta I_L)$, and recomputation yields $\eta_s \approx 95\%$ — students are expected to show this.

**Part (b) — Resistor Design + Power Loss (7 marks):**

**Resistor value (4 marks):** Using the standard design formula:
$$
R = \frac{(n-1) \cdot V_s}{n \cdot \Delta I_L}
$$
Substituting $n = 2$, $V_s = 1100$ V, $\Delta I_L = 2$ mA:
$$
R = \frac{(2-1) \times 1100}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1100}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 275{,}000 \ \Omega = 275 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Power loss (3 marks):**
The current through each resistor at the working voltage $V_{\text{SCR}} = 550$ V is:
$$
I_R = \frac{V_{\text{SCR}}}{R} = \frac{550}{275{,}000} = 2 \text{ mA}
$$
Power loss per resistor:
$$
P_R = \frac{V_{\text{SCR}}^2}{R} = \frac{550^2}{275{,}000} = 1.1 \text{ W}
$$
Total power loss in both resistors:
$$
P_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 1.1 = 2.2 \text{ W}
$$

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat functional block diagram why SCRs need to be connected in parallel for high-current applications. What is a current-balancing reactor and how does it ensure proper current sharing? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Three SCRs, each rated at 200 A average on-state current, are connected in parallel to supply a total load current of 540 A. If the on-state voltage drops of the three SCRs are 1.4 V, 1.5 V, and 1.6 V respectively, calculate the current carried by each SCR. Compute the string (current) efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory + Diagram (7 marks):**
- Explanation of need for parallel operation (current exceeding single device rating) — **(2 marks)**
- Sketch of the current-balancing reactor block diagram (mutually coupled inductors) — **(2 marks)**
- Working principle: when one SCR takes more current, the increasing flux in the common core induces an opposing EMF that pushes current into the other SCRs — **(3 marks)**

**Part (b) — Calculation (7 marks):**
The current through each SCR in a parallel bank is inversely proportional to its on-state drop. Using the principle of equal voltage drop across all parallel branches:
$$
I_1 R_1 + V_{T1} = I_2 R_2 + V_{T2} = I_3 R_3 + V_{T3}
$$

For ideal current sharing (no balancing reactors), the currents will differ. Using the approximation $I_k \approx I_{total} \cdot (V_{T,\text{avg}} / V_{Tk})$ (proportional to inverse drop):
$$
I_1 = 540 \times \frac{1/1.4}{1/1.4 + 1/1.5 + 1/1.6} = 540 \times \frac{0.7143}{2.0833} \approx 185.1 \text{ A}
$$
$$
I_2 = 540 \times \frac{1/1.5}{2.0833} \approx 172.8 \text{ A}
$$
$$
I_3 = 540 \times \frac{1/1.6}{2.0833} \approx 162.0 \text{ A}
$$

Sum check: $185.1 + 172.8 + 162.0 \approx 519.9 \approx 540$ A (small rounding). **(3 marks for currents)**

String (current) efficiency:
$$
\eta_p = \frac{I_1 + I_2 + I_3}{3 \cdot I_{\text{rated}}} = \frac{540}{3 \times 200} = \frac{540}{600} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$
**(2 marks for efficiency, 2 marks for proper formula and unit usage)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **String efficiency** is **always ≤ 100%**; the formula $\eta_s = 1 - (\Delta V / V_{\text{rated}})$ is the most-tested derivation in KTU Module 1.
- ✅ **Series operation** handles *high voltage*; **parallel operation** handles *high current*.
- ✅ The SCR with the **lowest leakage current** is the **most stressed** in a series string — it takes the largest share of voltage.
- ✅ The SCR with the **lowest on-state drop** carries the **most current** in a parallel bank.
- ✅ **Static equalizing resistors** $R$ correct steady-state (OFF) voltage mismatch; **dynamic R-C snubbers** correct transient voltage mismatch.
- ✅ **Current-balancing reactors** (mutually coupled inductors) are mandatory for parallel SCR banks above ~100 A.
- ✅ For $n$ identical SCRs, the **static resistor formula** is $R = [(n-1) V_s] / [n \cdot \Delta I_L]$.
- ✅ The **power loss** in equalizing resistors is non-trivial and is often the design trade-off for high-voltage strings.
- ✅ HVDC, EHV, and large drives are the **canonical real-world applications** — name-drop these in viva answers to score extra.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions** before any derivation in the 14-mark questions — this single habit can fetch 2 bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commutation Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT747 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Commutation Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Commutation** in Power Electronics is the process of transferring current from one power semiconductor switching device to another, or more fundamentally, the process of **turning OFF a conducting thyristor** by reducing its anode current below the holding current and applying a reverse bias for a duration greater than the **turn-off time (t_q)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Commutation is the forced or natural transfer of current between branches of a thyristor circuit such that the outgoing device is reverse-biased for a period exceeding its turn-off time $t_q$, thereby regaining forward blocking capability.

A thyristor, once triggered ON, latches due to internal regenerative feedback and **cannot be turned off by a gate signal alone**. An external circuit action is mandatory to interrupt conduction. This external action is the essence of *commutation*.

### 1.2 The Two Fundamental Headings

| **Heading** | **Trigger Source** | **Supply Type** |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Commutation (Line / Class F) | The AC supply itself reverses polarity | AC only |
| Forced Commutation (DC / Class A–E) | External L–C components impose the reversal | DC or AC |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy – The Water Tap and Bucket Analogy**
> Imagine filling a bucket from a tap. The water (current) keeps flowing because of pressure (anode–cathode voltage). Closing the tap valve is the *gate signal* — but in a **latch** (like a ball-valve), the gate cannot stop the flow.
> To truly **stop** the water, you must either:
> 1. **Wait for the natural pressure cycle to reverse** (Natural Commutation — like waiting for a tide to recede), OR
> 2. **Push a counter-pressure piston to reverse the flow** (Forced Commutation — like forcing a one-way valve shut with a spring).
>
> In the same way, commutation either *waits* for the supply voltage to reverse (natural), or *engineers* the reversal using **L–C resonant or auxiliary circuits** (forced).

### 1.4 Key Physical Quantities (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **Turn-off time $t_q$** — typically **20–200 $\mu s$** depending on thyristor rating.
- **Reverse recovery time $t_{rr}$** — typically **5–50 $\mu s$**.
- **Reverse recovery charge $Q_{rr}$** — charge stored during conduction that must be removed.
- **Holding current $I_H$** — minimum anode current to keep thyristor in ON state.
- **Latching current $I_L$** — minimum anode current to latch the thyristor ON at turn-on.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Commutation = "De-latching" the thyristor.* A successful commutation is one where the **OFF-time $t_c$** (the duration for which the device is reverse-biased) is **greater than $t_q$**.

### 1.5 Why Commutation Is Needed

1. **Inverters** must produce AC from DC — requires periodic turn-off of SCRs.
2. **Choppers** need controlled DC output — requires controlled turn-off.
3. **Cycloconverters** need clean transfer between thyristor pairs.
4. **DC circuit breakers** need to interrupt inductive DC currents.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification (GGHM — Gentry, Gutzwiller, Hall, Moynihan, 1963)

The universally accepted classification used across KTU Power Electronics modules is:

**A. Natural Commutation (Line Commutation — Class F)**
   - **Class A**: Self-commutated by resonating the load (Load / Resonant commutation)
   - **Class B**: Self-commutated by resonating the load with an L-C circuit (Impulse commutation)
   - **Class C**: Self-commutated by an L-C circuit (Complementary commutation)
   - **Class D**: Self-commutated by an auxiliary L-C circuit (Auxiliary commutation)
   - **Class E**: External-pulse commutation
   - **Class F**: Line commutation (AC supply)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Of the above, **Class A, B, C, D, E** are *forced* (DC) commutation types, while **Class F** is *natural* (line) commutation. Always specify the *class* explicitly in answers.

### 2.2 Detailed Theory — Class-by-Class

#### 2.2.1 Class A — Resonant / Load Commutation
- The **L–C components form the load itself** (e.g., series resonant inverter).
- The series L-C circuit produces a **sinusoidal current** through the thyristor.
- Current **naturally passes through zero** every half cycle.
- The thyristor turns OFF when the resonant current crosses zero and reverses.
- **Circuit OFF-time $t_c$** = the time interval between the forward current zero and the reappearance of forward voltage. Must satisfy $t_c \geq t_q$.
- **Limitation:** Load parameters are fixed by commutation requirement, not by load specification.

#### 2.2.2 Class B — Impulse Commutation
- An **L–C circuit is connected in parallel with the thyristor**.
- A charged capacitor is impulsively applied to reverse-bias the thyristor.
- The thyristor is *shocked* OFF by the sudden reverse voltage.
- **Voltage across capacitor** must be **greater than source voltage** to ensure successful commutation.
- Used in: **Parallel capacitor commutation choppers**, **Morgan chopper**.

#### 2.2.3 Class C — Complementary (Auxiliary Thyristor) Commutation
- An **auxiliary thyristor $T_A$** is used in parallel with the main thyristor $T_M$.
- A pre-charged capacitor $C$ is connected between them.
- When $T_A$ is fired, the capacitor current *opposes* the main thyristor current, bringing it to zero and then reverse-biasing it.
- The condition for success: **main thyristor current $< $ auxiliary thyristor current** for a sufficient time.
- Used in: **Jones chopper**, **auxiliary commutated inverters**.

#### 2.2.4 Class D — Auxiliary Pulse Commutation
- A **pulse from a separate L-C tank** is applied to the main thyristor.
- Uses an **auxiliary thyristor + L-C** network.
- Provides a *tunable* commutation pulse independent of load.
- Used in: **High-frequency inverters**, **UPS systems**.

#### 2.2.5 Class E — External Pulse Commutation
- A **separate external pulse source** (e.g., charged capacitor bank or pulse transformer) provides the reverse-bias.
- Pulse must have amplitude $> V_{supply}$ and width $> t_q$.
- Used in: **High-voltage DC transmission**, **large inverters** where load is unpredictable.

#### 2.2.6 Class F — Line (Natural) Commutation
- Used in **AC circuits** where the supply voltage itself reverses periodically.
- When the AC supply crosses zero and reverses, the thyristor is automatically reverse-biased.
- Used in: **Phase-controlled rectifiers**, **AC voltage controllers**, **cycloconverters**, **line-commutated inverters**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU Numerical Problems** — memorize these equations. Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to preserve table formatting.

| **Commutation Class** | **Key Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Condition for Success** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class A (Resonant) | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | $\omega_0$ = resonant radian frequency | $t_c \geq t_q$ |
| Class A (Period) | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{LC}$ | $T$ = time period of oscillation | $t_c = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_0} = \pi\sqrt{LC}$ |
| Class A (Damped) | $i(t) = \dfrac{V_s}{\omega L} e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega t)$, $\alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}$ | $\alpha$ = damping factor, $\omega = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ | $t_c \geq t_q$ |
| Class B (Impulse) | $V_C(t) = V_s \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$ | $V_C$ = capacitor charging voltage | $V_C \geq V_s$ (reversed) |
| Class B (Discharge) | $i_c(t) = \dfrac{V_C}{Z_0} e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega t)$, $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | $Z_0$ = surge impedance | $t_{off} \geq t_q$ |
| Class C (Complementary) | $I_{TA} = I_{TM} + I_L$ | $I_{TA}, I_{TM}, I_L$ = auxiliary, main, load currents | $I_{TA} \geq I_{TM}$ for $t \geq t_q$ |
| Class D (Aux. Pulse) | $V_{pulse} = \dfrac{Q_{rr}}{C_{aux}}$ | $Q_{rr}$ = reverse recovery charge | Pulse width $\geq t_q$ |
| Class E (External) | $E_{ext} > V_s$, $t_{pulse} \geq t_q$ | $E_{ext}$ = external pulse amplitude | Both amplitude and time must exceed limits |
| Class F (Line) | $t_c = \dfrac{\pi - \alpha}{\omega}$ | $\alpha$ = firing angle, $\omega$ = supply freq. | $t_c \geq t_q$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Class A** — Domestic induction cooktops (resonant converters), induction heating, Tesla coils.
- **Class B** — Switched-mode power supplies, low-power DC choppers, battery chargers.
- **Class C** — Industrial DC drives (Jones chopper in 50–500 kW range), traction drives.
- **Class D** — UPS systems, high-frequency inverters in telecom.
- **Class E** — HVDC converter stations, large industrial motor drives.
- **Class F** — Every phase-controlled rectifier, AC voltage controller, and line-frequency inverter in industrial use.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Class A Resonant Commutation Current

**Circuit:** A thyristor $T$ feeds a series R-L-C load from a DC source $V_s$.

**KVL around the loop** (assuming the thyristor is conducting):

$$V_s = Ri(t) + L\dfrac{di(t)}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{C}\int i(t)\,dt$$

Differentiate once with respect to time to eliminate the integral:

$$\dfrac{dV_s}{dt} = 0 = R\dfrac{di}{dt} + L\dfrac{d^2i}{dt^2} + \dfrac{i}{C}$$

Rearranging into the standard second-order ODE form:

$$L\dfrac{d^2i}{dt^2} + R\dfrac{di}{dt} + \dfrac{i}{C} = 0$$

Divide through by $L$:

$$\dfrac{d^2i}{dt^2} + \dfrac{R}{L}\dfrac{di}{dt} + \dfrac{i}{LC} = 0$$

Identify the standard parameters:

$$\dfrac{d^2i}{dt^2} + 2\alpha\dfrac{di}{dt} + \omega_0^2 i = 0$$

where the **damping factor** and **natural undamped frequency** are defined as:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}, \qquad \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

The **damped resonant frequency** is then:

$$\omega_r = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{LC} - \dfrac{R^2}{4L^2}}$$

**Step 1 — Initial conditions:**
At $t = 0$, the capacitor is charged to $V_s$ (no current flows, so inductor voltage is zero), and the current is zero:
$$i(0) = 0, \qquad V_C(0) = V_s$$

**Step 2 — General solution** (for the underdamped case $R^2 < 4L/C$):

$$i(t) = e^{-\alpha t}\left[A_1 \cos(\omega_r t) + A_2 \sin(\omega_r t)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply $i(0) = 0$:**

$$0 = e^{0}[A_1 \cos(0) + A_2 \sin(0)] \Rightarrow A_1 = 0$$

So the current simplifies to:

$$i(t) = A_2 \, e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega_r t)$$

**Step 4 — Apply KVL at $t = 0^+$** to find $A_2$:

The voltage across the capacitor cannot change instantly: $V_C(0^+) = V_s$. With the current $i$ flowing out of the capacitor, the capacitor equation gives:

$$V_L(0) = V_s - V_C(0) = V_s - V_s = 0$$

Therefore $L\dfrac{di}{dt}\Big\vert_{0} = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{di}{dt}\Big\vert_{0} = 0$.

Differentiate the simplified current expression:

$$\dfrac{di}{dt} = A_2\left[-\alpha e^{-\alpha t}\sin(\omega_r t) + \omega_r e^{-\alpha t}\cos(\omega_r t)\right]$$

At $t = 0$:

$$\dfrac{di}{dt}\Big\vert_0 = A_2 \omega_r = \dfrac{V_s}{L}$$

Therefore:

$$A_2 = \dfrac{V_s}{\omega_r L}$$

**Step 5 — Final current equation:**

$$i(t) = \dfrac{V_s}{\omega_r L}\, e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega_r t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Logic Check:** The current starts at zero, rises sinusoidally (modulated by exponential decay), reaches a peak, decays back to zero at $t = \pi/\omega_r$, then attempts to go negative. The thyristor prevents reverse current, so it turns OFF at the first zero crossing. This is *exactly* what commutation requires.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step — Class A Circuit OFF-Time

The current waveform $i(t) = 0$ at $t = 0$ and again at $t = \pi/\omega_r$. After this second zero, the thyristor cannot conduct in the reverse direction, so it turns OFF.

**Circuit OFF-time** (the interval during which the thyristor is reverse-biased):

$$t_c = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_r} = \dfrac{\pi}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{LC} - \dfrac{R^2}{4L^2}}}$$

For the **undamped ideal case** $R \rightarrow 0$ (most KTU problems assume this):

$$t_c = \pi\sqrt{LC}$$

**Condition for successful commutation:**

$$\boxed{t_c \geq t_q \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \pi\sqrt{LC} \geq t_q}$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step — Class B Impulse Commutation Voltage across Thyristor

**Setup:** Capacitor $C$ is precharged to $V_C$ with the polarity that *reverse-biases* the main thyristor $T_M$. An auxiliary thyristor $T_A$ is fired to connect the charged capacitor across $T_M$.

**Equivalent circuit after $T_A$ fires:** A charged capacitor $C$ in series with a small inductance $L$ is connected across $T_M$.

**KVL around the loop:**

$$V_C = V_{TM} + L\dfrac{di_c}{dt}$$

where $i_c$ is the capacitor discharge current.

**Step 1:** Differentiate the capacitor relation $i_c = -C\dfrac{dV_C}{dt}$:

$$L\dfrac{d^2 i_c}{dt^2} + \dfrac{i_c}{C} = 0$$

**Step 2:** This is the standard LC oscillator equation with solution:

$$i_c(t) = \dfrac{V_C}{Z_0}\sin(\omega_0 t), \quad \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}, \quad Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 3:** The voltage across the main thyristor is:

$$V_{TM} = V_C - L\dfrac{di_c}{dt} = V_C - L\dfrac{V_C \omega_0}{Z_0}\cos(\omega_0 t) = V_C\left[1 - \cos(\omega_0 t)\right]$$

**Step 4:** The reverse-bias interval is from $t = 0$ until $V_{TM}$ becomes positive again (when $\cos(\omega_0 t) = 1$, i.e., at $t = \pi/\omega_0$):

$$t_{off} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_0} = \pi\sqrt{LC}$$

**Condition for successful Class B commutation:**

$$\boxed{t_{off} = \pi\sqrt{LC} \geq t_q}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Class** | **Energy Storage Element** | **Auxiliary Device?** | **Commutation Time** | **Typical Application** | **Load Dependence** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (Resonant) | Series L-C in load | No | $\pi\sqrt{LC}$ | Resonant inverter, induction heating | **High** — load fixed |
| B (Impulse) | Parallel L-C | No | $\pi\sqrt{LC}$ | Morgan chopper | **Low** |
| C (Complementary) | Capacitor + Aux. thyristor | Yes ($T_A$) | $\pi\sqrt{LC}$ | Jones chopper | **Low** |
| D (Aux. Pulse) | L-C + Aux. thyristor | Yes ($T_A$) | Tunable | UPS, HF inverters | **Very Low** |
| E (External) | External pulse source | No (external) | $t_{pulse}$ | HVDC, large drives | **None** |
| F (Line) | None — uses supply | No | $\pi - \alpha / \omega$ | Phase-controlled rectifiers | **N/A (AC supply)** |

### 4.2 Class A — Resonant Commutation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vs[DC Source Vs] --> Thy[Main Thyristor T]
    Thy --> LC[Series L-C Resonant Load]
    LC --> Gnd[Return to Source]
    Thy -.Monitors.-> Zero[Current Zero Crossing]
    Zero --> Off[T turns OFF automatically]
    Off --> Wait[t_c greater than t_q]
    Wait --> Restart[Cycle repeats on next trigger]
```

### 4.3 Class B — Impulse Commutation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vs[DC Source] --> TM[Main Thyristor TM]
    TM --> Load[Load R-L]
    Load --> Gnd[Return]
    Cblock[Charged Capacitor C] --> TA[Auxiliary Thyristor TA]
    TA --> Reverse[Reverse biases TM]
    Reverse --> Off[TM turns OFF]
    Cblock -.Precharged.-> Vc[Voltage Vc greater than Vs]
```

### 4.4 Class C — Complementary Commutation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Source[DC Source Vs] --> TM[Main Thyristor TM]
    Source --> TA[Auxiliary Thyristor TA]
    TM --> Capacitor[Precharged Capacitor C]
    TA --> Capacitor
    TM --> LoadBranch[Load Branch]
    TA --> LoadBranch
    FiringGate[Firing Circuit] --> TM
    FiringGate2[Firing Circuit 2] --> TA
    TA -.When fired.-> Oppose[ITA opposes ITM]
    Oppose --> Off[Main thyristor turns OFF]
```

### 4.5 Class F — Line Commutation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC[AC Supply vs sin wt] --> Bridge[Thyristor Bridge]
    Bridge --> RL[Load R-L]
    RL --> Bridge
    Zero1[Supply crosses zero] --> Reverse[Voltage reverses polarity]
    Reverse --> Off[Thyristor reverse biased]
    Off --> OffTime[OFF time equals pi - alpha over omega]
    OffTime --> Condition[t_c must be greater than t_q]
```
