## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define commutation in thyristor circuits. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

Commutation is the process of turning off a conducting thyristor by reducing its anode current below the holding current and applying a reverse bias for a duration greater than its turn-off time $t_q$.

It is necessary because a thyristor, once latched ON, cannot be turned off by its gate signal alone (unlike a BJT or MOSFET). The internal regenerative feedback sustains conduction as long as the anode current exceeds the latching current. Therefore, an external commutation circuit is mandatory in any application requiring controlled turn-off, such as inverters, choppers, and DC circuit breakers.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2. List any three forced commutation classes. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Class** | **Name** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| Class A | Resonant (Load) Commutation | Series resonant inverter for induction heating |
| Class B | Impulse Commutation | Morgan chopper in DC motor drives |
| Class C | Complementary (Auxiliary Thyristor) Commutation | Jones chopper in industrial DC drives |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 1]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram and waveform, explain the operation of **Class A resonant commutation**. Derive the expression for the current through the thyristor and state the condition for successful commutation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Class A resonant commutation circuit has $L = 20\ \mu H$, $C = 5\ \mu F$, and load resistance $R = 0.5\ \Omega$. The thyristor has a turn-off time of $t_q = 40\ \mu s$. Determine whether commutation is successful. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Circuit Description:** A thyristor $T$ feeds a series R-L-C combination from a DC source $V_s$. The L-C components *constitute the load* and form a resonant tank.

**Operation:**
1. When the thyristor is triggered at $t = 0$, current begins to flow through the series R-L-C circuit.
2. The capacitor initially charged to $V_s$ (with polarity aiding conduction) begins to discharge through the inductor.
3. The current rises sinusoidally, reaches a peak, then falls to zero at $t = \pi / \omega_r$.
4. After the first zero crossing, the current attempts to reverse, but the thyristor blocks reverse current → it turns OFF naturally.
5. The OFF interval is $t_c = \pi / \omega_r$, and the thyristor regains blocking capability if $t_c \geq t_q$.

**Current derivation:** Already shown in Section 3.1 above. Final expression:

$$i(t) = \dfrac{V_s}{\omega_r L} \, e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega_r t), \quad \alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}, \quad \omega_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{LC} - \dfrac{R^2}{4L^2}}$$

**Condition for success:**

$$\boxed{t_c = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_r} \geq t_q}$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $L = 20\ \mu H = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ H$, $C = 5\ \mu F = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ F$, $R = 0.5\ \Omega$, $t_q = 40\ \mu s = 40 \times 10^{-6}\ s$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L} = \dfrac{0.5}{2 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{0.5}{40 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.25 \times 10^{4}\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\omega_0$:**

$$\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{20 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{100 \times 10^{-12}}} = \dfrac{1}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 1 \times 10^{5}\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\omega_r$:**

$$\omega_r = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2} = \sqrt{(1 \times 10^5)^2 - (1.25 \times 10^4)^2} = \sqrt{10^{10} - 1.5625 \times 10^8} = \sqrt{9.844 \times 10^9} = 9.92 \times 10^4\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute circuit OFF-time $t_c$:**

$$t_c = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_r} = \dfrac{3.1416}{9.92 \times 10^4} = 3.17 \times 10^{-5}\ s = 31.7\ \mu s$$

**Step 5 — Compare with $t_q$:**

$$t_c = 31.7\ \mu s \quad \text{vs} \quad t_q = 40\ \mu s$$

Since $t_c < t_q$, **commutation FAILS**. The thyristor will turn ON again before it has fully regained forward blocking capability.

**Remedy:** Increase L or C to lengthen $t_c$. For successful commutation, we need $t_c = 40\ \mu s$, which requires $\omega_r = \pi / t_q = 7.85 \times 10^4\ \text{rad/s}$. Adjusting L or C will achieve this.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain **Class B impulse commutation** and **Class C complementary commutation** with neat diagrams. Compare their features. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Class B impulse commutation chopper has $C = 20\ \mu F$ and the resonant inductor $L = 5\ \mu H$. If the source voltage is $V_s = 100\ V$, determine: (i) the surge impedance, (ii) the peak discharge current, and (iii) whether commutation is successful for a thyristor with $t_q = 30\ \mu s$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Class B — Impulse Commutation:**
- A charged capacitor $C$ is connected across the main thyristor $T_M$ through a small inductor $L$.
- The capacitor is precharged to a voltage $V_C > V_s$ with the polarity that *reverse-biases* $T_M$.
- When auxiliary thyristor $T_A$ is fired, the capacitor discharges through $L$ and the main thyristor, applying a sudden reverse voltage across $T_M$.
- The reverse voltage appears for a time $t_{off} = \pi\sqrt{LC}$, which must exceed $t_q$.
- **Used in:** Morgan chopper, impulse-commutated choppers.

**Class C — Complementary Commutation:**
- Two thyristors — main ($T_M$) and auxiliary ($T_A$) — share a common precharged capacitor $C$.
- When $T_M$ is conducting and load current $I_L$ flows, firing $T_A$ causes the capacitor current $I_{TA}$ to *oppose* $I_{TM}$.
- When $I_{TA} > I_{TM}$ (i.e., the capacitor current exceeds the main thyristor current), $T_M$ experiences a net reverse current and turns OFF.
- The auxiliary thyristor continues to conduct until the capacitor voltage falls below the supply.
- **Used in:** Jones chopper, complementary commutated inverters.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Feature** | **Class B (Impulse)** | **Class C (Complementary)** |
|---|---|---|
| Auxiliary thyristor | Optional / not always required | **Mandatory** |
| Energy source | Precharged capacitor | Precharged capacitor |
| Turn-off method | Reverse voltage impulse | Reverse current through $T_A$ |
| Commutation time | $\pi\sqrt{LC}$ | $\pi\sqrt{LC}$ (approx.) |
| Load dependence | Low | Very low |
| Application | Morgan chopper | Jones chopper |
| Complexity | Simple | Moderate |

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $C = 20\ \mu F = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ F$, $L = 5\ \mu H = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ H$, $V_s = 100\ V$, $V_C$ (assumed $\geq V_s$) $= 100\ V$, $t_q = 30\ \mu s$.

**(i) Surge Impedance:**

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{20 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{0.25} = 0.5\ \Omega$$

**(ii) Peak Discharge Current:**

$$I_{peak} = \dfrac{V_C}{Z_0} = \dfrac{100}{0.5} = 200\ A$$

**(iii) Check for successful commutation:**

First, compute the resonant angular frequency:

$$\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{100 \times 10^{-12}}} = \dfrac{1}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 1 \times 10^{5}\ \text{rad/s}$$

Circuit OFF-time:

$$t_{off} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_0} = \dfrac{3.1416}{1 \times 10^{5}} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-5}\ s = 31.4\ \mu s$$

Compare with $t_q$:

$$t_{off} = 31.4\ \mu s \quad \text{vs} \quad t_q = 30\ \mu s$$

Since $t_{off} > t_q$ (margin of $1.4\ \mu s$), **commutation is SUCCESSFUL** ✓.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the condition $t_c \geq t_q$ explicitly.** Many students derive the equations but omit the final feasibility condition. This costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Class A and Class B commutation.** Class A: *load* is the L-C; Class B: L-C is in *parallel* with the thyristor. Examiners test this distinction.
> 3. **Using the wrong damping factor.** For underdamped cases, students often write $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ without damping. Use $\omega_r = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ when $R > 0$.
> 4. **Not drawing the waveform.** A question worth 7 marks in the **drawing + explanation** category *always* requires a labeled waveform (current and voltage vs time) showing the zero-crossing and OFF interval.
> 5. **Mixing up natural and forced commutation classes.** Memorize: *Forced = Class A to E*; *Natural = Class F* (only).
> 6. **Unit errors in numerical problems.** Always carry $\mu$ through to the final answer and convert. Many students lose 1 mark by writing $t_c = 31.7$ instead of $t_c = 31.7\ \mu s$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Commutation Types**
>
> ☐ **Commutation** = turning off a thyristor; needs reverse bias for $t \geq t_q$.
> ☐ **Two main heads:** Natural (Class F) and Forced (Class A–E).
> ☐ **Class A — Resonant:** L-C is the load; current zero-crossing turns OFF the thyristor. $t_c = \pi\sqrt{LC}$.
> ☐ **Class B — Impulse:** Parallel L-C; sudden reverse voltage impulse. $V_C \geq V_s$.
> ☐ **Class C — Complementary:** Auxiliary thyristor diverts current. $I_{TA} \geq I_{TM}$.
> ☐ **Class D — Auxiliary pulse:** L-C + auxiliary thyristor; tunable pulse.
> ☐ **Class E — External pulse:** External source provides reverse bias; pulse width $\geq t_q$.
> ☐ **Class F — Line (Natural):** AC supply reverses naturally; used in phase-controlled rectifiers.
> ☐ **Master condition (all classes):** $t_{off} \geq t_q$.
> ☐ **Master resonant formula:** $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$, $T = 2\pi\sqrt{LC}$.
> ☐ **Class A OFF-time:** $t_c = \pi\sqrt{LC}$ (ideal), $\pi/\omega_r$ (with damping).
> ☐ **Surge impedance:** $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ — important for Class B numericals.
> ☐ **GGHM classification** (1963) is the universally accepted standard — quote it in answers.
> ☐ **Real-world anchors:** Morgan chopper = Class B, Jones chopper = Class C, HVDC = Class E, Rectifiers = Class F.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single-Phase Uncontrolled and Controlled Rectifiers, Three-Phase Controlled Rectifiers, Inductive and Capacitive Loads, Power Factor Improvement in Rectifiers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT747 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Single-Phase Uncontrolled and Controlled Rectifiers, Three-Phase Controlled Rectifiers, Inductive and Capacitive Loads, Power Factor Improvement in Rectifiers

# MODULE 2: RECTIFIERS — UNCONTROLLED & CONTROLLED CONVERTERS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This module is the **most heavily weighted unit** in PERUT747 and forms the backbone of Power Electronics-II. Mastery of waveforms, RMS averages, and firing angle (α) relations is mandatory.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Rectifier?

A **rectifier** is a power electronic converter that converts **AC (Alternating Current) supply into DC (Direct Current) load**. It is the dual of an inverter and sits at the heart of every DC drive, HVDC link, battery charger, SMPS, and DC welding set.

- **Uncontrolled Rectifier:** Uses only **diodes**. The DC output voltage is *fixed* by the transformer turns ratio and supply magnitude. The user cannot adjust the DC level.
- **Controlled Rectifier:** Uses **thyristors (SCRs)**. The DC output is *continuously variable* by changing the **firing angle α** (the delay in triggering the SCR from the instant it becomes forward-biased). The full designation is *Phase-Controlled Rectifier (PCR)*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model**
> Think of AC as an oscillating tide. A **diode** is like a *one-way valve* that lets water flow in only the positive direction (half-wave) or both half-cycles redirected the same way (full-wave). A **thyristor** is the same valve but with a *latch* that requires an external "tap" (gate pulse) to open. The longer you wait to tap (larger **α**), the less water (DC voltage) you get. **α = 0°** = full flow; **α = 90°** (single-phase full converter) = zero average; **α → 180°** = negative flow (inverter mode).

### 1.2 Controlled Rectifier Classification

```
                PHASE-CONTROLLED RECTIFIERS
                          |
        +-----------------+-----------------+
        |                                   |
  HALF CONTROLLED                       FULLY CONTROLLED
  (SEMI-CONVERTER)                      (FULL CONVERTER)
  Diodes + Thyristors                   All Thyristors
  α range: 0 to 180°                    α range: 0 to 180°
  Cannot go to inverter                 Can operate in INVERTER
  mode cleanly                          mode (90° < α < 180°)
```

### 1.3 Single-Phase Topologies (Quick Index)

| Topology | Devices | Pulses/cycle | Ripple Freq. | Avg. V_dc (α=0) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Half-Wave (M1) | 1 Diode / 1 SCR | 1 | 50 Hz | V_m/π |
| Full-Wave Center-Tap (M2) | 2 Diodes / 2 SCRs | 2 | 100 Hz | 2V_m/π |
| Bridge (M6) — Uncontrolled | 4 Diodes | 2 | 100 Hz | 2V_m/π |
| Semi-Converter (M6 hybrid) | 2 D + 2 SCR | 2 | 100 Hz | V_m(1+cosα)/π |
| Full-Converter (M6) | 4 SCRs | 2 | 100 Hz | 2V_m cosα/π |

### 1.4 Three-Phase Topologies (Quick Index)

| Topology | Devices | Pulses/cycle | Ripple Freq. | Avg. V_dc (α=0) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3-φ Half-Wave (M3) | 3 SCRs | 3 | 150 Hz | 3√3 V_m / 2π |
| 3-φ Full-Bridge (M6) | 6 SCRs | 6 | 300 Hz | 3√3 V_m / π |

> [!NOTE]
> **V_m** = peak phase voltage for star supply, or **√2 × V_RMS(line-to-neutral)**. For delta supply use line-to-line peak **V_mL**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles — Pulse-by-Pulse Logic

### 2.1.1 Single-Phase Half-Wave Rectifier (Uncontrolled)
- **Circuit:** 1 diode + R load.
- **Operation:** Diode D conducts only when V_s > 0. From ωt = 0 to π, V_o = V_m sin(ωt). From π to 2π, D is reverse-biased → V_o = 0.
- **Conduction angle:** θ_c = π (180°) per cycle.

### 2.1.2 Single-Phase Half-Wave with R-L Load
- **Freewheeling is absent** (no FWD). The load current, once started, cannot fall to zero because the inductor opposes di/dt < 0.
- Diode D continues to conduct past π until the load current naturally falls to zero at some angle β > π.
- **β** (extinction angle) is found by: i_L(β) = 0.

### 2.1.3 Single-Phase Full-Wave Bridge (Uncontrolled, M6)
- **Pair D1-D2** conducts during 0 to π (positive half).
- **Pair D3-D4** conducts during π to 2π (negative half, redirected to load).
- Output is full-wave rectified → V_o = |V_m sin(ωt)|.
- Conduction angle per diode = π, but **per pair = π**.

### 2.1.4 Single-Phase Fully-Controlled Bridge (M6, Full Converter)
- All four devices are SCRs (T1, T2, T3, T4).
- T1-T2 fired at α, T3-T4 fired at α+π.
- **For 0 ≤ α ≤ 90°:** Rectifier mode (V_dc positive).
- **For α = 90°:** V_dc = 0 (boundary).
- **For 90° < α < 180°:** INVERTER mode (V_dc negative, power flows from DC → AC).
- With R-L load, the output voltage can swing negative during commutation overlap and inverter mode.

### 2.1.5 Single-Phase Semi-Converter (Half-Controlled)
- Two SCRs (T1, T2) on one leg + two diodes (D1, D2) on the other.
- SCR T1 fires at α; it conducts up to π. At π, the polarity reverses; D2 (freewheeling) takes over, short-circuiting the load.
- **The output voltage can NEVER go negative** → average is always positive.
- **α range:** 0 to 180° (rectifier only).

### 2.1.6 Three-Phase Half-Wave (M3) Controlled
- 3 SCRs, one per phase. The phase with the highest instantaneous voltage gets the gate pulse; its SCR conducts for 120°.
- Natural commutation points occur at 30°, 150°, 270° (phase-to-phase crossings).
- **α = 0:** conduction starts at 30° (from phase crossing). **α** is measured from these natural commutation points.
- For 0 ≤ α ≤ 90°: V_dc positive (rectifier).
- For 30° < α ≤ 90°: discontinuous conduction (with R load).
- For 90° < α < 180°: inverter mode.

### 2.1.7 Three-Phase Full-Bridge (M6) Controlled
- 6 SCRs. Conduction sequence: T1→T2→T3→T4→T5→T6, each for 60°.
- **6-pulse output** — ripple frequency = 6× supply = 300 Hz (50 Hz supply).
- Highly favored in industrial DC drives (6-pulse converters).

### 2.1.8 Effect of Source Inductance (L_s) — Commutation Overlap (μ)
- Due to source inductance, current cannot commutate instantly from one SCR to another.
- During overlap, **two SCRs conduct simultaneously**, and the output voltage is the **average** of the two incoming phase voltages.
- **Overlap angle μ** depends on load current, source L_s, and α.

### 2.1.9 Effect of Load Inductance (L)
- Smooths output current → continuous conduction.
- Causes **negative output voltage** during commutation for full converter.
- Extends conduction past π (current continuity).

### 2.1.10 Effect of Capacitive Load / C-Filter
- Capacitor charges to V_peak and holds it → nearly constant DC.
- Drawing current only in narrow pulses near V_peak → high peak current, **poor power factor**, lots of harmonic distortion.
- Common in **uncontrolled** rectifiers (cheap DC supplies).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — The "Must-Memorize" Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Memorize these formulas *symbol by symbol*. The single most common mistake is using V_mL (line peak) where V_m (phase peak) is needed, or vice-versa. Always check the circuit diagram.

### A. Single-Phase Uncontrolled Rectifiers (R Load, No α)

| Quantity | Half-Wave | Full-Wave Center-Tap | Full-Wave Bridge |
|---|---|---|---|
| **V_avg (V_dc)** | V_m/π | 2V_m/π | 2V_m/π |
| **V_rms** | V_m/2 | V_m/√2 | V_m/√2 |
| **Ripple Factor γ** | 1.21 | 0.482 | 0.482 |
| **Efficiency η** | 40.6% | 81.2% | 81.2% |
| **TUF (CT)** | 0.287 | 0.693 | 0.810 |
| **Rectification Efficiency** | (V_dc/V_rms)² | 0.8104 | 0.8104 |
| **PIV per diode** | V_m | 2V_m | V_m |

Where **V_m = √2 V_s** (RMS supply).

### B. Single-Phase Controlled Rectifiers (R-L Load, Continuous Conduction)

| Quantity | Half Converter (Semi) | Full Converter (Full) |
|---|---|---|
| **V_dc** | (V_m/π)(1 + cos α) | (2V_m/π) cos α |
| **V_rms** | (V_m/√2) √[(1/π)(π − α + sin(2α)/2)] | (V_m/√2) |
| **I_s (RMS supply)** | I_o √[(π−α)/π] (half-converter) | I_o (full, square) |
| **PF (displacement, R-L)** | (2√2/π) cos(α/2) | (2√2/π) cos α |
| **α range (rectifier)** | 0 to 180° | 0 to 90° |
| **α range (inverter)** | Not possible cleanly | 90° to 180° |

### C. Three-Phase Controlled Rectifiers

| Quantity | 3-φ Half-Wave (M3) | 3-φ Full-Bridge (M6) |
|---|---|---|
| **V_dc (continuous cond.)** | (3√3 V_m)/(2π) cos α | (3√3 V_m)/π cos α |
| **V_dc (α=0, R-load limit)** | 0 at α = 30° (avg) | Continuous down to 0 at α=90° |
| **PIV per SCR** | √3 V_m (= V_mL) | √3 V_m (= V_mL) |
| **Ripple freq.** | 3f = 150 Hz | 6f = 300 Hz |
| **XFMR utilization (sec.)** | 0.675 (3-φ half) | 0.955 (3-φ full) |

### D. Commutation Overlap (Source Inductance L_s) — Single-Phase Full Converter

$$V_{dc}(\text{actual}) = \frac{2V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha - \frac{2\omega L_s I_o}{\pi}$$

The second term is the **overlap voltage drop** — purely resistive-like behavior of L_s in the average sense.

### E. Power Factor Relationships

$$PF_{true} = \underbrace{\frac{I_{s1}\cos\phi_1}{I_s}}_{\text{distortion}} \times \underbrace{\cos\phi_1}_{\text{displacement}}$$

- **Displacement Power Factor (DPF)** = cos φ₁, where φ₁ = angle between fundamental supply voltage and fundamental supply current.
- **Distortion Power Factor** = I_{s1} / I_s (ratio of fundamental to total RMS current).
- **True PF** = DPF × Distortion PF.

For full converter, DPF = cos α → **at α = 0, PF ≈ 0.9**; at α = 60°, PF = 0.45. **PF degrades severely with firing angle advance.**

### F. Power Factor Improvement Methods

1. **Extinction Angle Control (Semi-converter trick):** Use semi-converter instead of full converter at high α — output is zero during freewheel period → current is a symmetric square → only odd harmonics.
2. **Asymmetric (Sequential) Firing:** Pulse thyristors asymmetrically to cancel lower-order harmonics.
3. **Forced Commutation / PWM Rectifiers:** Replace phase-controlled with IGBT PWM rectifiers → nearly unity PF.
4. **AC-side Filters (Passive & Active):** Shunt passive filters or active power filters (APF) to absorb harmonics.
5. **Synchronous Link / Phase-Shift Transformer:** Use 12-pulse or 24-pulse converters (multi-pulse) — characteristic harmonics are pushed to high orders (11th, 13th, 23rd, 25th), making filtering trivial.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Average Output Voltage of Single-Phase Full-Bridge (Uncontrolled)

> **KTU Frequent Question:** "Derive the expression for average and RMS output voltage of a single-phase full-wave bridge rectifier with R load."

**Setup:** V_s(t) = V_m sin(ωt). The bridge rectifies both half cycles, so:
$$V_o(\omega t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t) & 0 \le \omega t \le \pi \\ -V_m \sin(\omega t) & \pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases} = \vert V_m \sin(\omega t) \vert$$

### Step 1: Average DC Voltage

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} V_o(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} V_m \vert \sin(\omega t) \vert\, d(\omega t)$$

By symmetry of |sin| about π/2 and periodicity, integrate over half cycle and double:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2 \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{0}^{\pi}$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\big[(-\cos\pi) - (-\cos 0)\big] = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\big[(+1) - (-1)\big] = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}$$

**Numerical check:** V_s = 230 V RMS → V_m = 325.27 V → V_dc = 207 V (the familiar "average" of a 230 V full-wave rectified sine).

### Step 2: RMS Output Voltage

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} V_o^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \Big[\omega t - \frac{\sin 2\omega t}{2}\Big]_{0}^{2\pi}$$

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi}\cdot 2\pi = \frac{V_m^2}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = V_s$$

> **Intuition:** Full-wave rectification doubles the average and **preserves the RMS** of the sine wave (because squaring the negative half makes it identical to the positive half).

### Step 3: Ripple Factor

$$\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_m/\sqrt{2}}{2V_m/\pi}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} = 0.482$$

### Step 4: Rectification Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{V_{dc}^2 / R}{V_{rms}^2 / R} = \frac{V_{dc}^2}{V_{rms}^2} = \frac{(2V_m/\pi)^2}{V_m^2/2} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} = 0.8104 = 81.04\%$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — V_dc of Single-Phase Full Converter (Fully Controlled, M6) with R-L Load

**Setup:** T1-T2 pair fires at α. Conduction lasts π radians. Output waveform during conduction:
$$V_o = V_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{for} \quad \alpha \le \omega t \le \alpha + \pi$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi}$$

Using the identity $\cos(\alpha + \pi) = -\cos\alpha$:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\big[(-\cos(\alpha+\pi)) - (-\cos\alpha)\big] = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\big[\cos\alpha + \cos\alpha\big]$$

$$\boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha}$$

**Significance:**
- α = 0° → V_dc = 2V_m/π (maximum, equal to uncontrolled bridge).
- α = 90° → V_dc = 0 (boundary between rectifier and inverter).
- α = 135° → V_dc = −2V_m cos 45° / π ≈ −0.45 V_m (**inverter mode**, power flows DC → AC).

---

## 3.3 Derivation — V_dc of Single-Phase Semi-Converter (Half-Controlled)

**Setup:** T1 fires at α, conducts to π. Diode D_FW (freewheel) takes over from π to π+α. Output:
- For α ≤ ωt ≤ π: V_o = V_m sin(ωt)
- For π ≤ ωt ≤ π+α: V_o = 0 (D_FW conducts, shorting load)

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi}$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\big[-\cos\pi + \cos\alpha\big] = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$$

> **Comparison with full converter:** The semi-converter cannot make V_dc negative — its output ranges from 2V_m/π (α=0) down to 0 (α=180°). It is preferred in **low-power reversible drives** where inverter mode is not needed and harmonic performance matters.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — V_dc of 3-φ Full-Bridge (M6) Controlled

**Setup:** Conduction of T1 spans 30° + α to 150° + α (one SCR pair conducts for 60°). Average over a 60° interval:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3}{\pi}\int_{\pi/6 + \alpha}^{\pi/2 + \alpha} V_{LL,\text{line-to-line peak}}\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

Using V_mL = √3 V_m (line peak to phase peak), and integrating:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

where **V_m = peak phase voltage**. This is the **workhorse formula** for industrial DC drives.

**Limit checks:**
- α = 0° → V_dc = 3√3 V_m / π ≈ 1.654 V_m.
- α = 90° → V_dc = 0 (transition to inverter).
- α > 90° → V_dc negative (inverter mode — used in **regenerative braking** of DC motors).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — KTU Numerical Problem Patterns

| # | Problem Pattern | Key Formulae |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Avg & RMS output for 1-φ half-wave | V_dc = V_m/π, V_rms = V_m/2 |
| 2 | Rectification efficiency, ripple factor, TUF | Use 0.482, 0.8104, 0.287 etc. |
| 3 | Full converter firing angle for given V_dc | α = cos⁻¹(π V_dc / 2V_m) |
| 4 | Semi-converter firing angle for given V_dc | α = cos⁻¹(π V_dc / V_m − 1) |
| 5 | 3-φ full converter V_dc and harmonic order | V_dc = 3√3 V_m cosα/π; n=6k±1 |
| 6 | Commutation overlap voltage drop | ΔV = 2ωL_s I_o / π (1-φ) |
| 7 | Power factor of full converter | PF = (2√2/π) cos α (R-L, square wave) |
| 8 | Input current THD and DPF of semi-converter | I_s1 vs I_s relation, harmonic spectrum |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Phase Full-Bridge (M6) Fully-Controlled Rectifier — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    AC[AC Source V_s = V_m sin wt] -->|Phase| TRA[Center Tapped or Bridge Transformer]
    TRA --> Ls1[Source Inductance L_s]
    Ls1 --> B1[Bridge Leg 1: T1 and T3]
    B1 --> LOAD[DC Load R-L-E]
    TRA --> Ls2[Source Inductance L_s]
    Ls2 --> B2[Bridge Leg 2: T2 and T4]
    B2 --> LOAD
    LOAD --> FW[Freewheel Path D_FW optional]
    GC[Gate Pulse Generator firing angle alpha] --> B1
    GC --> B2
    GC --> FW
    LOAD --> SENS[Current and Voltage Sensors]
    SENS --> GC
```

> **Block-level note:** The AC source charges the transformer; L_s models practical source impedance; SCRs T1-T4 form the controllable bridge; firing pulses from the gate circuit synchronized to the supply zero-crossing define the angle α.

## 4.2 Three-Phase Full-Bridge (M6) — Conduction Sequence

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[R phase] --> T1[Thyristor T1]
    Y[Y phase] --> T3[Thyristor T3]
    B[B phase] --> T5[Thyristor T5]
    T1 --> T4[T4 negative leg]
    T3 --> T6[T6 negative leg]
    T5 --> T2[T2 negative leg]
    T4 --> N[DC Negative Terminal]
    T6 --> N
    T2 --> N
    P[DC Positive Terminal] --> LOAD[DC Load]
    N --> LOAD
    SEQ[Conduction Sequence T1 T2 T3 T4 T5 T6 60 degree each] --> T1
    SEQ --> T3
    SEQ --> T5
    SEQ --> T4
    SEQ --> T6
    SEQ --> T2
```

## 4.3 Power Factor Improvement Strategy — Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Rectifier Causing Poor PF] --> Q1{Is Inverter Mode Needed?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| A1[Use Full Converter and Active Filter or 12-pulse]
    Q1 -->|No| A2[Use Semi Converter less harmonics at high alpha]
    A2 --> Q2{Is Cost Critical?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A3[Add Passive LC Filter on AC side]
    Q2 -->|No| A4[Replace with IGBT PWM Rectifier Unity PF]
    A1 --> A5[Shunt Active Power Filter APF]
    A3 --> A6[Capacitor Bank for Reactive Compensation]
    A4 --> A7[Vector Control Near Sinusoidal Current]
    A5 --> DONE[Improved PF above 0.95]
    A6 --> DONE
    A7 --> DONE
```

## 4.4 Half-Wave Rectifier Waveform Map (R-Load, Uncontrolled)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input V_s = V_m sin wt] --> B[Diode conducts 0 to 180 degree]
    B --> C[Output V_o = V_m sin wt for half cycle]
    B --> D[Output V_o = 0 for next half cycle]
    C --> E[Average V_dc = V_m by pi]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Ripple Factor 1.21]
    E --> G[Efficiency 40.6 percent]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Confusing V_m (phase peak) with V_mL (line peak):** For a 415 V, 3-phase star supply, V_m = √2 × 415/√3 ≈ 339 V, while V_mL = √2 × 415 ≈ 587 V. Mixing these gives a 1.73× error — **direct 7-mark penalty** in numericals.
> 2. **Forgot to include source inductance drop:** Whenever L_s is given, V_dc reduces by 2ωL_s I_o/π. Many students write the *ideal* formula and lose 4 marks.
> 3. **Semi-converter drawn with negative output:** The semi-converter **cannot produce negative V_dc**. If the question asks for α = 150° on a semi-converter, V_dc = 0, not negative.
> 4. **Confusing continuous vs discontinuous conduction for 3-φ half-wave with R load:** The conduction is discontinuous for 30° < α < 90° with R load. Use the *correct* integral limits.
> 5. **Displacement PF = True PF (mistaken assumption):** True PF = DPF × distortion PF. Only for pure sinusoidal current are they equal.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Compare semi-converter and full-converter in terms of output voltage range and operation mode.**

**Model Answer:**
A semi-converter (half-controlled) uses two thyristors and two diodes; its output voltage varies from **V_dc(max) = 2V_m/π at α = 0°** to **V_dc = 0 at α = 180°** following **V_dc = (V_m/π)(1 + cos α)**. It operates **only in rectifier mode** (V_dc is always non-negative) because the freewheeling diode prevents the output from going negative.

A full-converter uses four thyristors; its output follows **V_dc = (2V_m/π) cos α**, ranging from +2V_m/π at α = 0° to −2V_m/π at α = 180° (with zero crossing at α = 90°). It can operate in both **rectifier mode (0° ≤ α < 90°)** and **inverter mode (90° < α < 180°)**, enabling power flow from DC to AC. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Define displacement power factor and distortion power factor for a phase-controlled rectifier.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Displacement Power Factor (DPF)** is the cosine of the angle between the fundamental component of the supply current and the supply voltage: **DPF = cos φ₁**, where φ₁ is the phase shift introduced by firing angle α. For an ideal full converter, DPF = cos α.

The **Distortion Power Factor** is the ratio of the RMS value of the fundamental current to the total RMS supply current: **DPF_dist = I_{s1} / I_s**. It quantifies the degradation of PF due to harmonic currents.

The **True Power Factor** = DPF × Distortion Power Factor. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Full-Conversion Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. You must attempt **one** of the two sub-questions. We provide BOTH a Question A and a Question B with full model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Single-Phase Full Converter with R-L Load

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**A single-phase full-converter (M6) is supplied from 230 V, 50 Hz AC through a transformer. The load consists of R = 10 Ω and L = 50 mH in series, with a back-EMF E = 100 V. The converter operates in continuous conduction mode with firing angle α = 30°.**

**(a)** Derive the average output voltage expression for a single-phase full converter and compute the numerical value of V_dc, average load current I_o, and the power delivered to the load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the source has inductance L_s = 5 mH, compute the commutation overlap angle μ and the new average output voltage. Also calculate the supply power factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: V_dc expression (derivation):**

For a 1-φ full converter with R-L load in continuous conduction, each thyristor pair conducts for π radians. With firing angle α:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

**Step 2: Compute V_m and V_dc:**

V_s(RMS) = 230 V → V_m = √2 × 230 = **325.27 V**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi}\cos 30° = \frac{650.54}{\pi} \times 0.866 = 207.07 \times 0.866 = 179.32 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Average load current:**

$$I_o = \frac{V_{dc} - E}{R} = \frac{179.32 - 100}{10} = 7.932 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Power to load:**

$$P_{load} = V_{dc} \cdot I_o = 179.32 \times 7.932 = 1422.4 \text{ W}$$

Alternatively, P = E·I_o + I_o²·R = 100×7.932 + 7.932²×10 = 793.2 + 629.2 = 1422.4 W ✓ **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Commutation overlap angle μ:**

For 1-φ full converter: $V_m(\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu)) = \omega L_s I_o$

$$\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu) = \frac{\omega L_s I_o}{V_m}$$

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\frac{\omega L_s I_o}{V_m} = \frac{314.16 \times 5\times 10^{-3} \times 7.932}{325.27} = \frac{12.456}{325.27} = 0.03829$$

$$\cos 30° - \cos(30° + \mu) = 0.03829$$
$$0.8660 - \cos(30° + \mu) = 0.03829$$
$$\cos(30° + \mu) = 0.8277$$
$$30° + \mu = \cos^{-1}(0.8277) = 34.14°$$
$$\mu = 4.14° \text{ (≈ 0.072 rad)}$$

**Step 2: New V_dc with overlap:**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha - \frac{2\omega L_s I_o}{\pi} = 179.32 - \frac{2 \times 12.456}{\pi} = 179.32 - 7.93 = 171.39 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Supply power factor (R-L, square-wave current, ideal):**

For full converter, the supply current is a quasi-square wave. DPF = cos α = cos 30° = 0.866.
Distortion PF (for square wave) = (2√2)/π = 0.9.

$$PF_{true} = DPF \times Dist. PF = 0.866 \times 0.9 = 0.779$$

> **Check:** With overlap, DPF slightly degrades further: cos(α + μ/2) ≈ cos 32.07° = 0.847, so True PF ≈ 0.847 × 0.9 ≈ 0.762. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Three-Phase Full Converter (M6)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**A three-phase, fully-controlled bridge rectifier is supplied from a 415 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase star-connected source. The load is R-L (highly inductive) with continuous load current I_o = 50 A. The firing angle is α = 45°.**

**(a)** Derive the average output voltage of a 3-φ full-bridge controlled rectifier and compute the numerical value of V_dc, load power, and the displacement power factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the harmonic orders present in the supply current, calculate the RMS value of the fundamental supply current, and discuss one method to improve the power factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Derive V_dc:**

For 3-φ M6, line-to-line voltage v_ab = √3 V_m sin(ωt). The DC output is the average of 6 pulses per cycle, each 60° wide:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3}{\pi}\int_{\pi/6 + \alpha}^{\pi/2 + \alpha} \sqrt{3}\,V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

**Step 2: Compute V_m and V_dc:**

Phase RMS = 415/√3 = 239.6 V → V_m = √2 × 239.6 = **338.86 V**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} \times 338.86}{\pi}\cos 45° = \frac{3 \times 1.732 \times 338.86}{3.1416} \times 0.7071$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1760.3}{\pi} \times 0.7071 = 560.36 \times 0.7071 = 396.27 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Load power:**

$$P_{load} = V_{dc} \cdot I_o = 396.27 \times 50 = 19{,}813.5 \text{ W} \approx 19.81 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: DPF:**

For 3-φ full converter, DPF = cos α = cos 45° = **0.7071** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Harmonic orders in supply current:**

For a 6-pulse converter, the characteristic supply current harmonics are of the order:
$$h = 6k \pm 1, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$
i.e., **5th, 7th, 11th, 13th, 17th, 19th, ...**

The fundamental (k=0, h=1) is the desired in-phase component.

**Step 2: RMS value of fundamental supply current:**

For a 6-pulse converter, the supply current is a quasi-square wave with peak = I_o. Its fundamental RMS is:

$$I_{s1} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} I_o = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} \times 50 = \frac{2.449 \times 50}{3.1416} = 38.97 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Power factor improvement method:**

**Method 1 — 12-pulse converter (Phase-Shift Transformer):** Use a Y-Y and Y-Δ transformer combination (30° phase shift) feeding two 6-pulse bridges in series/parallel. The 5th and 7th harmonics cancel, leaving only 11th, 13th, 23rd, 25th… (12-pulse harmonics = 12k ± 1). This significantly improves PF and reduces THD.

**Method 2 — Active Power Filter (APF):** Shunt APF injects compensating currents at the AC side to cancel 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th harmonics, restoring nearly unity PF.

**Method 3 — PWM Rectifier (Active Front End):** Replace SCR bridge with IGBT-based PWM rectifier. By controlling IGBT pulses (SVM/SPWM), the input current is forced to be sinusoidal and in-phase with voltage, achieving PF ≈ 1.0. **(7 marks)**

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Half-wave uncontrolled:** V_dc = V_m/π; ripple factor 1.21; efficiency 40.6%.
- [x] **Full-wave uncontrolled bridge:** V_dc = 2V_m/π; V_rms = V_s; ripple 0.482; efficiency 81%.
- [x] **Full converter (1-φ M6):** V_dc = (2V_m/π) cos α; rectifier 0–90°, inverter 90°–180°.
- [x] **Semi-converter (1-φ):** V_dc = (V_m/π)(1 + cos α); always positive; no inverter mode.
- [x] **3-φ half-wave (M3):** V_dc = (3√3 V_m / 2π) cos α; PIV = V_mL.
- [x] **3-φ full-bridge (M6):** V_dc = (3√3 V_m / π) cos α; 6-pulse; harmonics 6k ± 1.
- [x] **R-L load effect:** Smooths current → continuous conduction → full converter can produce negative V_dc.
- [x] **C-filter (capacitive) load:** Holds V_o ≈ V_peak; current drawn in narrow pulses; high THD, poor PF.
- [x] **Source inductance L_s:** Causes commutation overlap μ; V_dc drops by 2ωL_s I_o / π (1-φ) or 3ωL_s I_o / π (3-φ).
- [x] **Power factor improvement:** (1) Replace SCR with PWM IGBT rectifier, (2) 12/24-pulse converter, (3) Shunt APF, (4) AC-side passive filter.
- [x] **DPF vs True PF:** DPF = cos α (full conv.); True PF = DPF × (I_{s1}/I_s). Always < DPF.
- [x] **PIV rule:** Uncontrolled bridge diode = V_m; SCR in 1-φ center-tap = 2V_m; SCR in 3-φ = V_mL.
- [x] **Critical KTU traps:** Don't confuse V_m (phase peak) vs V_mL (line peak = √3 V_m); semi-converter cannot go negative; for 3-φ half-wave with R load, conduction is discontinuous for α > 30°.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing Note:** Module 2 carries roughly **35–40%** of KTU ESE marks for PERUT747. Focus numerical practice on full-converter, semi-converter, and 3-φ full-bridge. Draw waveforms precisely — KTU examiners **deduct marks for unlabeled axes** or **missing conduction intervals**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC-DC Converters: Step Up and Step Down Chopper, Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost Converters, Isolated and Non-Isolated Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT747 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DC-DC Converters: Step Up and Step Down Chopper, Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost Converters, Isolated and Non-Isolated Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a DC-DC Converter?

A **DC-DC Converter** is a power electronic circuit that converts a DC source voltage from one level to another (either step-up or step-down) by temporarily storing energy in a magnetic field (inductor) or an electric field (capacitor) and then releasing it at a different voltage level. In KTU terminology, these are also referred to as **Choppers** — circuits that "chop" a fixed DC input into a variable DC output by varying the duty cycle of a high-frequency switching device (typically an **IGBT, MOSFET, or BJT**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Chopper** is a high-speed ON/OFF semiconductor device that connects source to load and disconnects the load from source at a fast chopping rate. The average output voltage is controlled by varying the duty ratio (D = T_on / T_total).

## 1.2 Classification of DC-DC Converters (KTU Framework)

DC-DC converters are broadly classified into two categories:

| Category | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-Isolated Converters** | Input and output share a common ground; no galvanic isolation | Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost, Cuk, SEPIC, Zeta |
| **Isolated Converters** | Uses a high-frequency transformer for galvanic isolation between input and output | Flyback, Forward, Push-Pull, Half-Bridge, Full-Bridge |

### 1.2.1 Chopper Classification by Voltage Transfer

- **Step-Down Chopper (Buck Converter):** Output voltage < Input voltage (V_o < V_s)
- **Step-Up Chopper (Boost Converter):** Output voltage > Input voltage (V_o > V_s)
- **Step-Up/Down Chopper (Buck-Boost / Cuk):** Output voltage can be either greater or less than input, with optional inversion

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a DC-DC converter as a **bicycle pump with a pressure regulator**.
> - The **input DC source** is like the high-pressure air tank.
> - The **switch (chopper)** is the foot pedal — pressing it briefly gives small puffs (low D), pressing it long gives bigger puffs (high D).
> - The **inductor** acts like a flywheel that stores energy during the ON state and releases it during the OFF state.
> - The **capacitor** is like a water tank that smooths out the pressure to a steady flow.
> - The **diode** is a one-way valve that prevents backflow of current.
> - The **load** is the tire or device receiving the regulated pressure/voltage.

By adjusting *how long* you press the pedal (duty cycle D), you regulate the average pressure (voltage) delivered. This is the essence of **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) control**.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

The following metrics are evaluated in KTU board examinations:

- **Duty Cycle (D):** $D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_s}$
- **Switching Frequency (f_s):** Typically **20 kHz to 500 kHz** in modern SMPS
- **Ripple Factor:** Ratio of AC ripple to DC component in output
- **Efficiency (η):** Typically **85% to 98%** in well-designed converters
- **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM):** Inductor current never falls to zero
- **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM):** Inductor current falls to zero during a portion of the switching cycle
- **Boundary Conduction Mode (BCM):** Inductor current just touches zero at the end of the switching cycle

## 1.5 Why DC-DC Converters are Essential

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** DC-DC converters are the **backbone of modern power electronics**. They are used in:
> - **SMPS (Switched Mode Power Supplies)** for computers and laptops
> - **Battery Management Systems (BMS)** in EVs and smartphones
> - **Solar PV MPPT systems** (boost converter for grid-tie inverters)
> - **DC Motor Drives** (chopper-fed traction control)
> - **LED Drivers** and consumer electronics
> - **Data center 48V → 1V VRMs** (using multi-phase buck converters)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles in Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)

In all non-isolated DC-DC converters, the operating principle relies on two states:
1. **Switch ON state (duration D·T_s):** Energy stored in the inductor (current builds up).
2. **Switch OFF state (duration (1-D)·T_s):** Stored energy is delivered to the load.

For **volt-second balance** on the inductor in steady state:

$$V_L \cdot t_{on} + V_L \cdot t_{off} = 0$$

This means the average voltage across the inductor over one switching cycle must be zero, which forms the **fundamental basis** for deriving all voltage transfer ratios.

## 2.2 Step-Down Chopper (Buck Converter) — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Circuit Topology
A buck converter consists of:
- A switching element **S** (MOSFET/IGBT)
- A **freewheeling diode D**
- An **output filter inductor L**
- An **output filter capacitor C**
- A **load resistor R**

### 2.2.2 Operation in CCM

**Mode 1 (Switch ON, 0 ≤ t ≤ D·T_s):**
- Switch S is closed
- Diode D is reverse-biased (OFF)
- Inductor stores energy; current rises linearly
- Voltage across inductor: $V_L = V_s - V_o$

**Mode 2 (Switch OFF, D·T_s ≤ t ≤ T_s):**
- Switch S is open
- Diode D is forward-biased (ON), providing a path for inductor current
- Inductor releases energy to the load
- Voltage across inductor: $V_L = -V_o$

### 2.2.3 Derivation of Voltage Transfer Ratio

Applying volt-second balance on the inductor:

$$(V_s - V_o) \cdot D \cdot T_s = V_o \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s$$

Solving for $V_o$:

$$\frac{V_o}{V_s} = D$$

## 2.3 Step-Up Chopper (Boost Converter) — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 Circuit Topology
A boost converter has the same components as the buck converter, but arranged differently:
- Inductor is placed in series with the input source
- Switch is connected from the inductor node to ground
- Diode connects the inductor-switch node to the output capacitor and load

### 2.3.2 Operation in CCM

**Mode 1 (Switch ON, 0 ≤ t ≤ D·T_s):**
- Switch S is closed; diode D is reverse-biased
- Inductor stores energy from the source
- Capacitor discharges through the load
- Voltage across inductor: $V_L = V_s$

**Mode 2 (Switch OFF, D·T_s ≤ t ≤ T_s):**
- Switch S is open; diode D is forward-biased
- Inductor releases energy, and together with source, charges the capacitor
- Voltage across inductor: $V_L = V_s - V_o$

### 2.3.3 Derivation of Voltage Transfer Ratio

Applying volt-second balance on the inductor:

$$V_s \cdot D \cdot T_s + (V_s - V_o) \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s = 0$$

Solving for $V_o$:

$$\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{1}{1-D}$$

## 2.4 Step-Up/Down Chopper (Buck-Boost Converter) — Detailed Analysis

### 2.4.1 Circuit Topology
The buck-boost converter is a cascade of buck and boost topologies:
- Inductor in series with input
- Switch to ground at the inductor-switch node
- Diode reversed in polarity compared to boost
- Output is **inverted** (negative polarity with respect to common ground)

### 2.4.2 Derivation of Voltage Transfer Ratio

$$V_o = -V_s \cdot \frac{D}{1-D}$$

The negative sign indicates **output voltage polarity inversion**. The magnitude is:

$$\vert V_o \vert = V_s \cdot \frac{D}{1-D}$$

- If D < 0.5 → step-down (buck mode)
- If D > 0.5 → step-up (boost mode)

## 2.5 Cuk Converter (Mentioned in KTU 2024 Advanced Syllabus)

The Cuk converter provides a non-pulsating input and output current with an inverted output:

$$\frac{V_o}{V_s} = -\frac{D}{1-D}$$

It uses a **coupling capacitor** for energy transfer, providing lower ripple than the standard buck-boost.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Converter Type | Voltage Transfer Ratio (M) | Output Polarity | Inductor Voltage (ON) | Inductor Voltage (OFF) | Boundary Condition (CCM/DCM) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Buck** | $D$ | Same as input | $V_s - V_o$ | $-V_o$ | $L_{min} = \frac{(1-D) \cdot R}{2 f_s}$ |
| **Boost** | $\frac{1}{1-D}$ | Same as input | $V_s$ | $V_s - V_o$ | $L_{min} = \frac{D(1-D)^2 \cdot R}{2 f_s}$ |
| **Buck-Boost** | $-\frac{D}{1-D}$ | Inverted | $V_s$ | $V_o$ | $L_{min} = \frac{(1-D)^2 \cdot R}{2 f_s}$ |
| **Cuk** | $-\frac{D}{1-D}$ | Inverted | $V_s$ | $V_o - V_s$ | $L_{min}$ uses coupling cap |
| **SEPIC** | $\frac{D}{1-D}$ | Same as input | $V_s$ | $V_o$ | $L_{min}$ uses coupling cap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Conventions used in KTU exams:**
> - $V_s$ = Input (Source) DC Voltage in **Volts (V)**
> - $V_o$ = Output DC Voltage in **Volts (V)**
> - $D$ = Duty Cycle (dimensionless, $0 < D < 1$)
> - $f_s$ = Switching frequency in **Hertz (Hz)**
> - $L$ = Inductance in **Henries (H)**
> - $C$ = Capacitance in **Farads (F)**
> - $R$ = Load Resistance in **Ohms (Ω)**
> - $\Delta I_L$ = Inductor ripple current in **Amperes (A)**
> - $\Delta V_o$ = Output voltage ripple in **Volts (V)**

## 2.7 Isolated DC-DC Converters

Isolated converters use a **high-frequency transformer** to provide **galvanic isolation** between input and output. This is critical for:
- **Safety** in medical and industrial equipment
- **Voltage scaling** (step-up or step-down via turns ratio)
- **Multiple output** generation

### 2.7.1 Flyback Converter
- Based on buck-boost topology with coupled inductor (transformer)
- Energy stored in transformer magnetizing inductance during ON, released during OFF
- Used in low-to-medium power applications (< 200 W)
- Voltage transfer: $\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot \frac{D}{1-D}$

### 2.7.2 Forward Converter
- Uses a demagnetizing (reset) winding
- Energy transferred directly to load during ON state (true transformer action)
- More efficient than flyback for medium power
- Voltage transfer: $\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot D$

### 2.7.3 Push-Pull Converter
- Uses two switches alternately
- Center-tapped transformer primary
- Voltage transfer: $\frac{V_o}{V_s} = 2 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot D$

### 2.7.4 Half-Bridge and Full-Bridge Converters
- **Half-Bridge:** Two switches and two capacitors; used in medium power
- **Full-Bridge (H-Bridge):** Four switches; highest power applications (kW range)
- Voltage transfer (Full-Bridge): $\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot D$
- Voltage transfer (Half-Bridge): $\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot D$

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter in practice:**
> - The **voltage transfer ratio (M)** is the **single most important design parameter**. It dictates the duty cycle needed for a given input/output voltage.
> - The **minimum inductance** ensures the converter operates in CCM, avoiding discontinuous mode complications.
> - The **ripple equations** dictate filter sizing for power quality.
> - **Efficiency** depends on switching losses (proportional to $f_s$) and conduction losses in switches and inductors.
> - In **EV traction systems**, boost converters step up 400V battery to 800V for inverter input, while buck converters step down for auxiliary 12V/48V loads.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Buck Converter Voltage Transfer Ratio (CCM)

### Step 1: Define Switching States
A buck converter operating in steady state has two distinct sub-intervals per cycle:
- **State A:** Switch S is ON for duration $t_1 = D \cdot T_s$
- **State B:** Switch S is OFF for duration $t_2 = (1-D) \cdot T_s$

where $T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$ is the switching period.

### Step 2: Apply KVL During State A (Switch ON)
When the switch is ON, the source $V_s$ is directly connected to the LC filter. The freewheeling diode is reverse-biased. Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law:

$$V_s = V_L + V_o$$

Therefore, the voltage across the inductor is:

$$V_{L,ON} = V_s - V_o$$

### Step 3: Apply KVL During State B (Switch OFF)
When the switch is OFF, the inductor current freewheels through the diode. The voltage at the input of the filter drops to zero (or ground). The inductor sees only the negative of the output voltage:

$$V_{L,OFF} = -V_o$$

### Step 4: Apply Volt-Second Balance (Steady-State Condition)
In steady state, the average voltage across the inductor over one complete switching cycle must be zero (otherwise the inductor would saturate). This is the **volt-second balance** principle:

$$\int_0^{T_s} V_L(t) \, dt = 0$$

Substituting the two states:

$$V_{L,ON} \cdot t_1 + V_{L,OFF} \cdot t_2 = 0$$

$$(V_s - V_o) \cdot D \cdot T_s + (-V_o) \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s = 0$$

### Step 5: Simplify the Equation
Dividing both sides by $T_s$ (non-zero):

$$(V_s - V_o) \cdot D - V_o \cdot (1-D) = 0$$

Expanding:

$$V_s \cdot D - V_o \cdot D - V_o + V_o \cdot D = 0$$

$$V_s \cdot D - V_o = 0$$

### Step 6: Final Result
Solving for the transfer ratio:

$$\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = D}$$

This means for a buck converter, the output voltage is **always less than** the input voltage, and is linearly proportional to the duty cycle D.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Boost Converter Voltage Transfer Ratio (CCM)

### Step 1: State A — Switch ON (Duration D·T_s)
When switch S is closed, the input source $V_s$ is connected directly across the inductor L. The diode is reverse-biased, isolating the output stage. The capacitor C supplies the load current.

Applying KVL to the input loop:

$$V_s = V_L$$

$$V_{L,ON} = V_s$$

### Step 2: State B — Switch OFF (Duration (1-D)·T_s)
When switch S is open, the inductor current must continue to flow (current through an inductor cannot change instantaneously). It now flows through the forward-biased diode to the output stage.

Applying KVL around the loop containing the source, inductor, and output:

$$V_s = V_L + V_o$$

$$V_{L,OFF} = V_s - V_o$$

### Step 3: Apply Volt-Second Balance

$$V_{L,ON} \cdot D \cdot T_s + V_{L,OFF} \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s = 0$$

$$V_s \cdot D + (V_s - V_o) \cdot (1-D) = 0$$

### Step 4: Expand and Simplify

$$V_s \cdot D + V_s - V_s \cdot D - V_o + V_o \cdot D = 0$$

$$V_s - V_o + V_o \cdot D = 0$$

$$V_s = V_o \cdot (1 - D)$$

### Step 5: Final Result
Solving for the transfer ratio:

$$\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{1}{1-D}}$$

This means for a boost converter, the output voltage is **always greater than** the input voltage, and approaches infinity as D approaches 1 (in practice, losses limit this).

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Buck-Boost Converter Voltage Transfer Ratio (CCM)

### Step 1: State A — Switch ON (Duration D·T_s)
The switch S is closed, the diode D is reverse-biased. The source $V_s$ is applied across the inductor:

$$V_{L,ON} = V_s$$

The capacitor C supplies the load current, and the output voltage is maintained across the capacitor.

### Step 2: State B — Switch OFF (Duration (1-D)·T_s)
The switch S is open, the diode D is forward-biased. The inductor current now flows through the diode to the output (note: the diode polarity is reversed compared to boost, leading to inverted output).

The inductor sees the negative of the output voltage:

$$V_{L,OFF} = V_o$$

### Step 3: Apply Volt-Second Balance

$$V_s \cdot D + V_o \cdot (1-D) = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve for V_o
Rearranging:

$$V_s \cdot D = -V_o \cdot (1-D)$$

$$V_o = -V_s \cdot \frac{D}{1-D}$$

### Step 5: Final Result (Magnitude)
Taking the magnitude:

$$\boxed{\left\vert \frac{V_o}{V_s} \right\vert = \frac{D}{1-D}}$$

The negative sign indicates **polarity inversion** of the output.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Output Voltage Ripple (Buck Converter)

The output capacitor C must supply the AC component of the inductor current (the ripple). During the switch ON state, the capacitor charges; during switch OFF, the capacitor discharges.

### Step 1: Define Inductor Ripple Current
For an inductor with constant voltage $V_L$ applied:

$$V_L = L \cdot \frac{\Delta I_L}{\Delta t}$$

During the switch ON state ($\Delta t = D \cdot T_s$):

$$\Delta I_L = \frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot D \cdot T_s}{L}$$

### Step 2: Define Capacitor Ripple Voltage
The capacitor must absorb the AC part of the inductor current. The peak-to-peak ripple in the output voltage is:

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{\Delta Q}{C}$$

where $\Delta Q$ is the charge added/removed. Approximating the ripple waveform as a triangle:

$$\Delta Q = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{T_s}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta I_L}{2} = \frac{\Delta I_L \cdot T_s}{8}$$

### Step 3: Final Ripple Expression
Substituting and simplifying (using $V_s - V_o = V_s(1-D)$):

$$\boxed{\Delta V_o = \frac{V_o \cdot (1-D)}{8 \cdot L \cdot C \cdot f_s^2}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Boundary Between CCM and DCM

The boundary between Continuous and Discontinuous Conduction Mode is reached when the inductor current ripple $\Delta I_L$ equals **twice the average inductor current** $I_L$ (i.e., the minimum current is exactly zero).

| Converter | Critical Inductance $L_{min,c}$ | Critical Load Resistance $R_{crit}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Buck** | $\frac{(1-D) \cdot R}{2 f_s}$ | $\frac{2 f_s L}{1-D}$ |
| **Boost** | $\frac{D(1-D)^2 \cdot R}{2 f_s}$ | $\frac{2 f_s L}{D(1-D)^2}$ |
| **Buck-Boost** | $\frac{(1-D)^2 \cdot R}{2 f_s}$ | $\frac{2 f_s L}{(1-D)^2}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU numerical problems:** If the load resistance R is greater than $R_{crit}$ at a given D, the converter operates in **DCM**, and the simple volt-second balance equations are **no longer valid**. The DCM analysis requires a third state where the inductor current remains at zero.

## 3.6 Numerical Example: Buck Converter Design

**Problem:** A buck converter has $V_s = 24$ V, $D = 0.4$, $L = 100$ µH, $C = 47$ µF, $f_s = 50$ kHz, $R = 10$ Ω.

**Solution:**

Output voltage:
$$V_o = D \cdot V_s = 0.4 \times 24 = 9.6 \text{ V}$$

Output current (assuming ideal converter):
$$I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{9.6}{10} = 0.96 \text{ A}$$

Inductor ripple:
$$\Delta I_L = \frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot D}{f_s \cdot L} = \frac{(24 - 9.6)(0.4)}{50000 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5.76}{5} = 1.152 \text{ A}$$

Output voltage ripple:
$$\Delta V_o = \frac{V_o \cdot (1-D)}{8 \cdot L \cdot C \cdot f_s^2} = \frac{9.6 \times 0.6}{8 \times 100\times10^{-6} \times 47\times10^{-6} \times (50000)^2}$$

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{5.76}{8 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-9} \times 2.5 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{5.76}{94} \approx 0.0613 \text{ V} = 61.3 \text{ mV}$$

This represents a ripple of $\frac{61.3}{9600} \times 100 \approx 0.64\%$, which is excellent.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define a chopper. List the two main modes of operation of a step-down chopper with a neat circuit diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

A **chopper** is a power electronic device that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage by rapidly switching a semiconductor device (SCR, IGBT, MOSFET) on and off at high frequency.

A step-down chopper operates in two modes:
1. **Mode 1 (Switch ON):** The chopper (switch S) is closed; current flows from source $V_s$ to load through L. The freewheeling diode is OFF. Inductor stores energy.
2. **Mode 2 (Switch OFF):** The chopper is open; inductor current freewheels through the diode D, delivering stored energy to the load.

The average output voltage is $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ where $D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_s}$ is the duty cycle.

**(3 marks awarded for definition, modes, and circuit reference)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Compare isolated and non-isolated DC-DC converters. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Non-Isolated | Isolated |
|---|---|---|
| **Galvanic Isolation** | No isolation between input and output | Provides galvanic isolation via high-frequency transformer |
| **Safety** | Lower safety | Higher safety (suitable for medical/industrial) |
| **Complexity** | Simpler, fewer components | More complex (transformer, driver) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Efficiency at high step ratios** | Lower | Higher (transformer turns ratio helps) |
| **Examples** | Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost, Cuk, SEPIC | Flyback, Forward, Push-Pull, Half-Bridge, Full-Bridge |

**(3 marks awarded for comparison table and examples)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**a) Derive the expression for the average output voltage of a step-down chopper (buck converter) operating in continuous conduction mode. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Circuit Description**
A buck converter consists of a DC source $V_s$, controlled switch S, freewheeling diode D, filter inductor L, filter capacitor C, and load R, all connected in series-parallel as shown in the standard topology.

**Step 2: State A — Switch ON (0 to D·T_s)**
During this interval, switch S is closed, and diode D is reverse-biased. Applying KVL:
$$V_s = V_L + V_o \implies V_L = V_s - V_o$$

**Step 3: State B — Switch OFF (D·T_s to T_s)**
Switch S is open, diode D is forward-biased and conducts the freewheeling current:
$$V_L = -V_o$$

**Step 4: Volt-Second Balance**
In steady state, the average inductor voltage is zero:
$$(V_s - V_o) \cdot D \cdot T_s + (-V_o) \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s = 0$$

**Step 5: Algebraic Simplification**
$$V_s \cdot D - V_o \cdot D - V_o + V_o \cdot D = 0$$
$$V_s \cdot D = V_o$$
$$\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = D}$$

**(7 marks: 1 for circuit description, 2 for state analysis, 2 for volt-second balance, 2 for final derivation)**

---

**b) For a boost converter operating in CCM, derive the voltage transfer ratio $\frac{V_o}{V_s}$. A boost converter has $V_s = 12$ V, $D = 0.6$, $L = 200$ µH, $f_s = 25$ kHz, and $R = 20$ Ω. Calculate the output voltage and the average inductor current. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: State A — Switch ON (0 to D·T_s)**
Switch S is closed, diode D is reverse-biased. Source applied across inductor:
$$V_{L,ON} = V_s$$

**Step 2: State B — Switch OFF (D·T_s to T_s)**
Switch S is open, diode D is forward-biased. Inductor discharges into the load:
$$V_{L,OFF} = V_s - V_o$$

**Step 3: Volt-Second Balance**
$$V_s \cdot D \cdot T_s + (V_s - V_o) \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s = 0$$
$$V_s \cdot D + V_s - V_o - V_s \cdot D + V_o \cdot D = 0$$
$$V_s = V_o \cdot (1-D)$$
$$\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{1}{1-D}}$$

**Step 4: Numerical Calculations**
Given: $V_s = 12$ V, $D = 0.6$, $L = 200$ µH, $f_s = 25$ kHz, $R = 20$ Ω

Output voltage:
$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-D} = \frac{12}{1-0.6} = \frac{12}{0.4} = 30 \text{ V}$$

Output current:
$$I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{30}{20} = 1.5 \text{ A}$$

For a boost converter, the input current equals the average inductor current:
$$I_L = I_s = I_o \cdot (1-D) = 1.5 \times 0.4 = 0.6 \text{ A}$$

Wait — this is incorrect. For a boost converter, the input current IS the inductor current, and by power balance (assuming ideal):
$$V_s \cdot I_L = V_o \cdot I_o \implies I_L = \frac{V_o \cdot I_o}{V_s} = \frac{30 \times 1.5}{12} = 3.75 \text{ A}$$

**(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for $V_o$ calculation, 2 for $I_L$ calculation with power balance reasoning)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**a) With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a buck-boost converter in CCM. Derive its voltage transfer ratio. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Circuit Description**
The buck-boost converter has the inductor in series with the source, the switch to ground, and the diode reversed in polarity compared to the boost converter. The output is taken across the capacitor with **reversed polarity** with respect to the common ground.

**Step 2: State A — Switch ON (0 to D·T_s)**
Switch S is closed, diode D is reverse-biased. The source charges the inductor:
$$V_{L,ON} = V_s$$

The capacitor C supplies the load current and maintains the output voltage $V_o$ (with reversed polarity).

**Step 3: State B — Switch OFF (D·T_s to T_s)**
Switch S is open, diode D is forward-biased. Inductor current flows through the diode to the load:
$$V_{L,OFF} = V_o$$

**Step 4: Volt-Second Balance**
$$V_s \cdot D \cdot T_s + V_o \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s = 0$$
$$V_s \cdot D = -V_o \cdot (1-D)$$
$$\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = -\frac{D}{1-D}}$$

**Step 5: Analysis**
- For $D < 0.5$: $|V_o| < V_s$ (step-down mode)
- For $D > 0.5$: $|V_o| > V_s$ (step-up mode)
- The negative sign indicates **output polarity inversion**

**(7 marks: 1 for circuit, 2 for state analysis, 2 for derivation, 2 for analysis of operation regions)**

---

**b) Explain the working of a flyback converter with a neat circuit diagram. Derive its voltage transfer ratio. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Circuit Description**
A flyback converter is essentially a buck-boost converter where the inductor is replaced by a **coupled inductor (transformer)**. Energy is stored in the magnetizing inductance of the primary winding during switch ON, and transferred to the secondary during switch OFF.

**Step 2: Circuit Components**
- Primary switch S (MOSFET)
- Coupled inductor with primary $N_1$ and secondary $N_2$
- Secondary diode D
- Output capacitor C and load R

**Step 3: State A — Switch ON (0 to D·T_s)**
Switch S is closed; primary current ramps up linearly, storing energy in the transformer core. The secondary diode D is reverse-biased (dot convention). The output capacitor supplies the load:
$$V_{L1,ON} = V_s$$

**Step 4: State B — Switch OFF (D·T_s to T_s)**
Switch S is open. The primary voltage reverses polarity, causing the secondary voltage to become positive and forward-bias the diode. The stored magnetic energy is released to the load.

The secondary voltage is:
$$V_{L2,OFF} = -V_o$$

Reflected to the primary:
$$V_{L1,OFF} = -\frac{N_1}{N_2} \cdot V_o$$

**Step 5: Volt-Second Balance (on primary)**
$$V_s \cdot D + \left(-\frac{N_1}{N_2} \cdot V_o\right) \cdot (1-D) = 0$$
$$V_s \cdot D = \frac{N_1}{N_2} \cdot V_o \cdot (1-D)$$
$$\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot \frac{D}{1-D}}$$

**Step 6: Key Features**
- Galvanic isolation provided by the transformer
- The turns ratio $n = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$ provides additional voltage scaling
- Used in low-to-medium power applications (mobile chargers, TV SMPS)
- Energy transfer is via **magnetic storage**, not transformer action (unlike forward converter)

**(7 marks: 1 for circuit, 2 for state analysis, 2 for derivation, 2 for explanation of energy transfer mechanism)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder (Most Common):** Students often forget the **negative sign** in the buck-boost and Cuk converter transfer ratios. Always explicitly state that the output is **inverted**.
> 2. **Confusing CCM and DCM Equations:** The simple transfer ratios ($D$, $\frac{1}{1-D}$, etc.) are valid **ONLY for CCM**. In DCM, an extra state with $V_L = 0$ exists, and the equation becomes nonlinear.
> 3. **Forgetting "Freewheeling Diode" in Buck:** Many students draw the buck converter without the freewheeling diode, leading to discontinuous current and overvoltage on the switch.
> 4. **Wrong Inductor Voltage in Boost OFF State:** Common mistake is writing $V_{L,OFF} = V_o$ instead of $V_{L,OFF} = V_s - V_o$.
> 5. **Mixing Up Flyback and Forward:** Flyback stores energy in the transformer (like buck-boost); Forward uses true transformer action (like buck). Confusing them costs 2-3 marks.
> 6. **Not Drawing the Diode Direction Correctly:** In a buck converter, the diode anode is at ground, cathode at the switch node. Reverse this, and the analysis fails.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — DC-DC Converters**

### Core Concepts
- A **chopper** is a fast ON/OFF switch that converts fixed DC to variable DC by varying duty cycle D
- **D = T_on / T_s**, where T_s is the switching period
- All voltage transfer ratios are derived from **volt-second balance** on the inductor
- **CCM** assumes inductor current is always positive; **DCM** allows it to reach zero

### Voltage Transfer Ratios (CCM) — Must Memorize
- **Buck:** $V_o / V_s = D$ (step-down, $0 < D < 1$)
- **Boost:** $V_o / V_s = 1 / (1-D)$ (step-up, output > input)
- **Buck-Boost:** $V_o / V_s = -D / (1-D)$ (step-up/down, **inverted**)
- **Cuk:** $V_o / V_s = -D / (1-D)$ (inverted, smoother currents)
- **SEPIC:** $V_o / V_s = D / (1-D)$ (non-inverted buck-boost)

### Isolated Converter Highlights
- **Flyback:** Buck-boost with coupled inductor; stores energy in transformer; suitable for < 200 W
- **Forward:** Buck with transformer + reset winding; direct energy transfer; medium power
- **Push-Pull, Half-Bridge, Full-Bridge:** Used for higher power (kW range)
- **Transformer turns ratio** adds another degree of freedom for voltage scaling

### Design Equations
- **Buck ripple voltage:** $\Delta V_o = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{8 L C f_s^2}$
- **Minimum inductance (Buck):** $L_{min} = \frac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s}$
- **Minimum inductance (Boost):** $L_{min} = \frac{D (1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$
- **Minimum inductance (Buck-Boost):** $L_{min} = \frac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$

### KTU Exam Strategy
1. Always draw the **circuit diagram** clearly with component labels
2. **State the two modes** of operation explicitly (Mode 1 / Mode 2)
3. Apply **volt-second balance** rigorously — show all algebraic steps
4. State **boundary conditions** for CCM operation
5. For numerical problems, mention **assumed idealities** (lossless switch, diode, L, C)
6. Always check whether the answer is a **step-up, step-down, or inverted output**

### Common Real-World Applications
- **Buck:** Laptop CPU VRMs, LED drivers, battery chargers
- **Boost:** Solar PV MPPT, LED flash, regenerative braking
- **Buck-Boost:** Power factor correction, battery-powered systems with varying input
- **Flyback:** Mobile chargers (< 100 W), TV SMPS, standby supplies
- **Forward:** Industrial SMPS (100-500 W)
- **Full-Bridge:** EV chargers, telecom rectifiers, welding inverters (kW range)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Inverter?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> An **inverter** is a power electronic DC-to-AC converter that converts a fixed or variable DC input voltage into a symmetrical AC output voltage of desired magnitude and frequency. The output waveform is typically a quasi-square wave or pulse-width modulated (PWM) wave that approximates a sine wave.

In simple words: an inverter is the "opposite" of a rectifier. While a rectifier converts AC from the mains into DC, an inverter takes a DC source (like a battery or solar PV panel) and synthesises an AC voltage at its output.

### Real-World Analogy

Think of an inverter like a **musical conductor switching between two groups of musicians**:

- The DC source is the "raw energy" (think of a water tank at a height).
- The semiconductor switches (SCRs, IGBTs, MOSFETs) are the "musicians" turning ON and OFF in a precise sequence.
- The output AC waveform is the "music" — a smooth sinusoidal shape produced by carefully timed bursts of energy.

Just as a conductor orchestrates musicians to create a melody, the inverter's control circuit orchestrates the switches to create an AC waveform. The smoother the orchestration (PWM, multi-level), the closer the output is to a pure sine wave.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> Inverters are classified primarily as:
> 1. **Voltage Source Inverters (VSI)** — fed from a stiff DC voltage source.
> 2. **Current Source Inverters (CSI)** — fed from a stiff DC current source.
> 3. **Single-Phase vs Three-Phase** — based on output phases.
> 4. According to **conduction mode** — 180°, 120°.
> 5. According to **switching technique** — Square wave, PWM, SPWM, SVPWM.

---

## 1.2 Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)

> [!NOTE]
> A **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** has a **stiff DC voltage source** at its input. The output voltage waveform is independent of the load current. The load can be of any type — resistive, inductive, or capacitive.

Key features:
- DC link has a **large capacitor** → maintains constant DC voltage.
- The amplitude of the output AC voltage is determined by the DC input.
- Used in UPS systems, variable frequency drives (VFDs), solar inverters.

## 1.3 Current Source Inverter (CSI)

> [!NOTE]
> A **Current Source Inverter (CSI)** is fed from a **stiff DC current source** (typically a DC source in series with a large inductor $L$). The output current waveform is independent of the load.

Key features:
- DC link has a **large inductor** → maintains constant DC current.
- The output current is rectangular; output voltage depends on load.
- Used in high-power, high-voltage motor drives and induction heating.

## 1.4 Single-Phase Inverter Topologies

### (a) Series Inverter
The load is connected in **series with the commutation circuit** (an $L-C$ tank). The resonant $L$ and $C$ are used to commutate the thyristors naturally. Used for **high-frequency heating, sonar, fluorescent lamp drives**.

### (b) Parallel Inverter
The load is connected in **parallel with the commutation capacitor**. Capacitor commutates the thyristors. Provides a **square wave** output.

### (c) Bridge Inverter (Full-Bridge)
- **Half-Bridge:** 2 switches + 2 capacitors → produces $\pm V_s/2$ across load.
- **Full-Bridge:** 4 switches → produces $\pm V_s$ across load. The full-bridge is the **most widely used** single-phase VSI topology.

## 1.5 Three-Phase Inverter

A **three-phase VSI** has **6 switches** (S1–S6). It feeds a **3-phase Y or Δ connected load**. Each phase leg has 2 complementary switches. The output is a **6-step waveform** per electrical cycle, displaced by 120° between phases.

> [!TIP]
> The six-step waveform, although not sinusoidal, contains a strong fundamental component. With Fourier analysis, we find that the **fundamental RMS line voltage** is approximately **0.955 × V_dc** for 180° conduction.

## 1.6 Conduction Modes — 180° and 120°

| Mode | Conduction Period | Three-Phase Inverter Switches ON at Any Time |
|------|-------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| 180° | Each switch conducts for 180° | **3 switches** at any instant |
| 120° | Each switch conducts for 120° | **2 switches** at any instant |

The 180° mode gives a **6-step line-to-line output**, while the 120° mode leaves a **30° gap** (no conduction) every half-cycle, leading to a different harmonic profile.

## 1.7 PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) Inverters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PWM** is a switching technique where the **pulse widths** within each half-cycle are **varied** to shape the output. By comparing a **reference sine wave** (modulating signal) with a **high-frequency triangular carrier**, we get gating pulses — hence the name **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)**.

Advantages of PWM:
- **Low harmonic distortion** (lower-order harmonics suppressed).
- **Easy voltage/frequency control** via modulation index $m_a$.
- Reduced filter requirements.

Two common types:
- **Single-Pulse Width Modulation** — one pulse per half-cycle.
- **Multiple-Pulse Width Modulation** — multiple pulses per half-cycle.
- **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)** — pulse widths follow a sine reference.
- **Modified SPWM (MSPWM)** — third-harmonic injection to boost DC utilisation.

---
