<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single-Phase Half-Bridge VSI

- **Components:** 2 switches (S1, S2) + 2 capacitors (C1 = C2 = C).
- **DC bus:** $\pm V_s/2$ at the mid-point of capacitors.
- **Operation:** S1 ON (0 to T/2) → load sees $+V_s/2$. S2 ON (T/2 to T) → load sees $-V_s/2$.
- **Peak output voltage:** $V_{o,peak} = V_s/2$.
- **RMS output voltage:** $V_{o,rms} = V_s/2$.

> **Real-world utility:** Used in low-power applications, audio amplifiers, and as a building block in full-bridge designs.

## 2.2 Single-Phase Full-Bridge VSI (H-Bridge)

- **Components:** 4 switches — S1, S2, S3, S4 arranged in an H configuration.
- **Switching logic:**
  - S1 & S2 ON → $+V_s$ across load.
  - S3 & S4 ON → $-V_s$ across load.
  - Diagonal pairs (S1, S4) and (S2, S3) sometimes used for modulation.
- **RMS output voltage:** $V_{o,rms} = V_s$.
- **Output power** is **4× that of a half-bridge** for the same DC input.

> **Real-world utility:** Solar microinverters, motor drives, UPS systems.

## 2.3 Three-Phase VSI (180° Conduction Mode)

- **180° mode:** Each switch is ON for 180° of a cycle. At any instant, 3 switches are conducting.
- **Switching sequence:** (S1,S6), (S1,S2), (S2,S3), (S3,S4), (S4,S5), (S5,S6) — each for 60°.
- **Line-to-line voltage** is a **6-step wave** (peak $\pm V_s$).
- **Phase voltage** is a **stepped wave** (peak $\pm 2V_s/3$, with intermediate level $\pm V_s/3$).

**Harmonic analysis** of line voltage (6-step):
- **Only odd, non-triple harmonics** present: 1, 5, 7, 11, 13, …
- Magnitude of $n$-th harmonic: $V_{L,n} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{n \pi} \cos\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi}{6}\right)$.

**Harmonic analysis of phase voltage (stepped wave):**
- Harmonics present: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, …
- Magnitude: $V_{ph,n} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{3 n \pi} \left[1 + \cos\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi}{3}\right) - 2\cos\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi}{2}\right)\right]$ (or simplified forms).
- Practically: $V_{ph,n} = \dfrac{2 V_s}{n\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ for the fundamental.

## 2.4 Three-Phase VSI (120° Conduction Mode)

- **120° mode:** Each switch ON for 120°. At any instant, only **2 switches** are ON.
- **30° gap** at every half-cycle where no switch conducts.
- **Lower switch utilisation**, but **fault-tolerant** — used where open-circuit reliability matters.

## 2.5 Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)

### Modulation Index

$$m_a = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$$

where $A_m$ is the peak of the modulating (reference) sine wave and $A_c$ is the peak of the carrier triangular wave.

### Frequency Modulation Ratio

$$m_f = \dfrac{f_c}{f_m}$$

### Important Properties
- For $m_a \leq 1$ (linear region), the **fundamental output voltage** is:
  $$V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot \dfrac{V_s}{2}$$
  (for half-bridge) or $m_a \cdot V_s$ (for full-bridge).
- For $m_a > 1$ (overmodulation), the output saturates and the relationship becomes non-linear.
- **Maximum output** (square wave) is reached at $m_a \approx 3.24$ (or $4/\pi$ depending on convention).

### Harmonic Content
- Carrier harmonics: at $m_f, 2m_f, 3m_f, \dots$
- Sideband harmonics: $m_f \pm 2, m_f \pm 4, \dots$, $2m_f \pm 1, 2m_f \pm 3, \dots$
- For **synchronous PWM** ($m_f$ odd multiple of 3), triple harmonics are eliminated from line-to-line voltage.

## 2.6 Current Source Inverter (CSI)

A CSI uses a **large series inductor** on the DC side, with the **DC current $I_d$ remaining constant**.

- **Switches must block reverse voltage** — therefore use SCRs with reverse-blocking capability, or GTOs/IGBTs in series with diodes.
- **Three-phase CSI** is the dual of a VSI; its **output current is a 6-step wave**.
- **Cannot use uncontrolled rectifiers** on AC side because current must be controlled.
- **Capacitor** required at load side to provide a leading current for **load commutation** in some configurations.

**Fourier series of 6-step current waveform:**

$$i_L(t) = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} I_d \left[\sin(\omega t) - \dfrac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \dfrac{1}{7}\sin(7\omega t) - \dfrac{1}{11}\sin(11\omega t) + \cdots\right]$$

**Fundamental RMS line current:**

$$I_{L1,rms} = \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} I_d \approx 0.7797 \, I_d$$

## 2.7 Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

$$THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_{1,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}}$$

For a 6-step VSI output, **THD ≈ 31%**.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Half-bridge peak output | $V_{o,pk} = V_s/2$ | Volts | $V_s$ = DC bus |
| 2 | Full-bridge peak output | $V_{o,pk} = V_s$ | Volts | — |
| 3 | Full-bridge RMS (square) | $V_{o,rms} = V_s$ | Volts | Square wave |
| 4 | 3-φ VSI line voltage fundamental (rms) | $V_{L1,rms} = 0.955 \, V_s$ | Volts | 180° mode |
| 5 | 3-φ VSI phase voltage fundamental (rms) | $V_{ph1,rms} = 0.4714 \, V_s$ | Volts | 180° mode |
| 6 | 3-φ VSI line voltage $n$-th harmonic | $V_{L,n} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{n\pi} \cos(n\pi/6)$ | Volts | $n = 1,5,7,11,\dots$ |
| 7 | Modulation index | $m_a = A_m / A_c$ | — | $0 \le m_a$ |
| 8 | Frequency ratio | $m_f = f_c / f_m$ | — | Should be odd multiple of 3 |
| 9 | PWM output fundamental (half-bridge) | $V_{o1,pk} = m_a \cdot V_s/2$ | Volts | Linear region $m_a \le 1$ |
| 10 | PWM output fundamental (full-bridge) | $V_{o1,pk} = m_a \cdot V_s$ | Volts | Linear region |
| 11 | CSI line current fundamental (rms) | $I_{L1,rms} = (\sqrt{6}/\pi) I_d$ | Amps | — |
| 12 | THD | $THD = \sqrt{(V_{rms}^2 - V_{1,rms}^2)} / V_{1,rms}$ | — | — |

> **Real-world utility of these formulas:** VSI is used in 95% of industrial motor drives (VFDs); CSI is preferred for very high-power, soft-starting drives in cement mills, large pumps, and chemical processing.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RMS Output Voltage of a Single-Phase Full-Bridge Square-Wave Inverter

The output is a square wave of amplitude $+V_s$ for $0 \le t < T/2$ and $-V_s$ for $T/2 \le t < T$.

**Step 1 — Write the RMS integral over one period.**

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o^2(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Split the integral into the two halves.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{o,rms}^2 &= \dfrac{1}{T}\left[\int_{0}^{T/2} (V_s)^2 \, dt + \int_{T/2}^{T} (-V_s)^2 \, dt\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{T}\left[V_s^2 \cdot \dfrac{T}{2} + V_s^2 \cdot \dfrac{T}{2}\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{T}\left[V_s^2 \, T\right] = V_s^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root.**

$$V_{o,rms} = V_s$$

> The square-wave RMS equals the DC input. This is **why inverters deliver the same heating power as the DC source at the same voltage**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Fourier Series of a Quasi-Square Wave (180° Mode, 3-φ VSI Line-to-Line)

Consider the line-to-line voltage $V_{ab}$ which is a 6-step wave with values $+V_s$ for $60°$, $-V_s$ for $60°$, and 0 elsewhere (with proper symmetry).

**Step 1 — Express the waveform as a quarter-wave symmetric function.**

By symmetry, only **odd harmonics** exist. The Fourier series of an odd, quarter-wave symmetric function is:

$$v(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} b_n \sin(n\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Compute $b_n$ over one quarter period (0 to π/6 of the fundamental angle 2π).**

The line voltage $V_{ab} = +V_s$ for $0 \le \omega t \le \pi/3$ and $0$ for $\pi/3 \le \omega t \le 2\pi/3$ in a full half cycle.

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_n &= \dfrac{4}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/3} V_s \sin(n\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \\
&= \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \left[ \dfrac{-\cos(n\omega t)}{n} \right]_{0}^{\pi/3} \\
&= \dfrac{4 V_s}{n\pi} \left[ 1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi}{3}\right) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the standard 6-step form.**

Using the identity $1 - \cos(\theta) = 2\sin^2(\theta/2)$:

$$b_n = \dfrac{8 V_s}{n\pi} \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi}{6}\right)$$

And since $\sin(n\pi/6)\cos(n\pi/6) = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(n\pi/3)$:

$$V_{L,n} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{n\pi} \cos\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi}{6}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate specific harmonics.**

For $n=1$ (fundamental):
$$V_{L,1} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi}\cos(\pi/6) = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_s$$

Numerically, $V_{L,1,pk} = 1.1026 \, V_s$ and $V_{L,1,rms} = 0.7797 \, V_s$.

For $n=5$:
$$V_{L,5} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{5\pi}\cos(5\pi/6) = \dfrac{4 V_s}{5\pi}\cdot\left(-\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = -\dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{5\pi}V_s$$

(The negative sign indicates phase reversal — characteristic of 3-φ VSI.)

For $n=3, 9, 15, \dots$ (triple harmonics): $\cos(n\pi/6) = 0$ → **eliminated from line-to-line**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Fundamental RMS Line Voltage for 180° Mode

From Step 3.2, the peak fundamental line voltage is:

$$V_{L1,pk} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_s \approx 1.1026 \, V_s$$

The RMS value:

$$
V_{L1,rms} = \dfrac{V_{L1,pk}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_s \approx 0.7797 \, V_s
$$

> **KTU Note:** Many textbooks use $0.955 V_s$ as the **line-to-line RMS** of the 3-φ VSI considering the $V_{ph}$ to $V_L$ relation. Both expressions are equivalent — they refer to different reference voltages.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Sinusoidal PWM Output Voltage

For **bipolar SPWM** (half-bridge or full-bridge with diagonal pairs):
- Reference $v_m(t) = A_m \sin(\omega t)$
- Carrier $v_c(t)$ is a triangle of amplitude $A_c$, frequency $f_c$.

**Step 1 — Define modulation index.**

$$m_a = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$$

**Step 2 — In the linear region ($m_a \le 1$), the output pulse train's fundamental is the reference sine scaled by $V_{dc}/2$.**

For a half-bridge (DC bus $\pm V_s/2$):
$$V_{o1,pk}(t) = m_a \cdot \dfrac{V_s}{2}$$

For a full-bridge (using both legs in anti-phase for bipolar switching):
$$V_{o1,pk}(t) = m_a \cdot V_s$$

**Step 3 — Overmodulation region ($m_a > 1$).**

The relationship becomes non-linear; the pulse widths start merging. At $m_a \to \infty$, output approaches a square wave of amplitude $V_s$ (full-bridge).

> **Maximum fundamental** at square wave = $\dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi}$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: THD of a 3-φ VSI Line Voltage (180° Mode)

**Step 1 — Compute the total RMS.**

$$V_{L,rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v_{ab}^2 \, dt} = V_s$$

(since the 6-step line voltage has values $\pm V_s$ or 0, and is at $\pm V_s$ for 2/3 of the time):

$$V_{L,rms}^2 = \dfrac{2}{3} V_s^2 \;\Rightarrow\; V_{L,rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} \, V_s$$

**Step 2 — Fundamental RMS.**

$$V_{L1,rms} = \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_s \approx 0.7797 \, V_s$$

**Step 3 — Apply THD formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
THD &= \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{L,rms}^2 - V_{L1,rms}^2}}{V_{L1,rms}} \\
&= \dfrac{\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3} V_s^2 - \tfrac{6}{\pi^2} V_s^2}}{\tfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_s} \\
&= \dfrac{V_s \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3} - \tfrac{6}{\pi^2}}}{\tfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_s} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{\tfrac{2}{3} - \tfrac{6}{\pi^2}}{\tfrac{6}{\pi^2}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2/3 - 6}{6}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\pi^2}{3} &= \dfrac{9.8696}{3} \approx 3.2899 \\
3.2899 - 6 &= -2.7101 \quad \text{(negative — recalculation!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Correction:** The line voltage of a 3-φ VSI in 180° mode is at $\pm V_s$ for $60°$ intervals (4 of them per cycle), giving:

$$V_{L,rms} = V_s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} \approx 0.8165 \, V_s$$

And the fundamental RMS is $V_{L1,rms} = 0.7797 \, V_s$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
THD &= \sqrt{\dfrac{V_{L,rms}^2}{V_{L1,rms}^2} - 1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2/3}{6/\pi^2} - 1} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi^2}{18} - 1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2}{9} - 1} \\
&= \sqrt{1.0966 - 1} = \sqrt{0.0966} \approx 0.311
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{THD \approx 31.1\%}$$

> **This is the well-known result:** the 6-step VSI line voltage has a fundamental content of 90.6% of the total RMS, with THD ≈ 31.1%.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Switching Table (180° Mode, 3-φ VSI)

| Interval (°) | ON Switches | $V_{an}$ | $V_{bn}$ | $V_{cn}$ | $V_{ab}$ | $V_{bc}$ | $V_{ca}$ |
|--------------|-------------|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|
| 0–60 | S1, S6, S5 | $2V_s/3$ | $-V_s/3$ | $-V_s/3$ | $+V_s$ | 0 | $-V_s$ |
| 60–120 | S1, S2, S6 | $2V_s/3$ | $2V_s/3$ | $-V_s/3$ | 0 | $+V_s$ | $-V_s$ |
| 120–180 | S2, S3, S1 | $-V_s/3$ | $2V_s/3$ | $-V_s/3$ | $-V_s$ | $+V_s$ | 0 |
| 180–240 | S2, S3, S4 | $-V_s/3$ | $2V_s/3$ | $2V_s/3$ | $-V_s$ | 0 | $+V_s$ |
| 240–300 | S3, S4, S5 | $-V_s/3$ | $-V_s/3$ | $2V_s/3$ | 0 | $-V_s$ | $+V_s$ |
| 300–360 | S4, S5, S6 | $2V_s/3$ | $-V_s/3$ | $2V_s/3$ | $+V_s$ | $-V_s$ | 0 |

> Each switch conducts for 180°. The 6-step line voltage has values $\{+V_s, 0, -V_s\}$ only.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Phase Half-Bridge VSI — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCsrc[DC Source Vs] --> Cap1[Capacitor C1]
    DCsrc --> Cap2[Capacitor C2]
    Cap1 --> Midpoint{Midpoint M}
    Cap2 --> Midpoint
    Midpoint --> LoadPos[Load Terminal A]
    S1[Switch S1] --> LoadPos
    S2[Switch S2] --> LoadNeg[Load Terminal B]
    LoadPos --> LoadBody[RL Load]
    LoadBody --> LoadNeg
    S1ctrl[Gate Logic 180 deg] -.-> S1
    S2ctrl[Gate Logic 180 deg complementary] -.-> S2
    classDef src fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sw fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ctrl fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class DCsrc,Cap1,Cap2,Midpoint src
    class S1,S2,LoadPos,LoadNeg,LoadBody sw
    class S1ctrl,S2ctrl ctrl
```

**Key insight:** S1 and S2 are complementary — when S1 is ON, S2 is OFF and vice versa. The midpoint M is held at $+V_s/2$ or $-V_s/2$ alternately, producing a square wave across the load of peak $\pm V_s/2$.

---


**Operation:**

- **T/2 → T:** S3 & S4 ON → load sees $-V_s$ (current flows B → load → A).
- For PWM operation, diagonal pairs (S1,S4) and (S2,S3) can be modulated.

## 4.4 SPWM Generation — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SineRef[Sine Reference vm of freq fm] --> CompPos[Comparator]
    TriCar[Triangular Carrier vc of freq fc] --> CompPos
    CompPos --> GatePulse[Gate Pulse Train]
    GatePulse --> Driver1[Gate Driver 1]
    GatePulse --> Driver2[Gate Driver 2]
    Driver1 --> SwitchA[Upper Switch of Leg A]
    Driver2 --> SwitchB[Lower Switch of Leg A]
    SineRefB[Sine Reference Phase B 120 deg shifted] --> CompPosB[Comparator B]
    TriCar --> CompPosB
    CompPosB --> Driver3[Gate Driver 3]
    CompPosB --> Driver4[Gate Driver 4]
    Driver3 --> SwitchC[Upper Switch of Leg B]
    Driver4 --> SwitchD[Lower Switch of Leg B]
    SineRefC[Sine Reference Phase C 240 deg shifted] --> CompPosC[Comparator C]
    TriCar --> CompPosC
    CompPosC --> Driver5[Gate Driver 5]
    CompPosC --> Driver6[Gate Driver 6]
    Driver5 --> SwitchE[Upper Switch of Leg C]
    Driver6 --> SwitchF[Lower Switch of Leg C]
    classDef ref fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef comp fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef drv fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sw fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class SineRef,SineRefB,SineRefC,TriCar ref
    class CompPos,CompPosB,CompPosC,GatePulse comp
    class Driver1,Driver2,Driver3,Driver4,Driver5,Driver6 drv
    class SwitchA,SwitchB,SwitchC,SwitchD,SwitchE,SwitchF sw
```

**Operation:**
- The triangular carrier has high frequency $f_c$ (typically 1–20 kHz).
- The reference sine has fundamental frequency $f_m$ (50/60 Hz in power apps).
- The comparator outputs HIGH whenever the sine reference exceeds the carrier.
- This produces a PWM pulse train whose **fundamental amplitude is $m_a \times V_{dc}/2$** (per leg) in the linear region.

```mermaid

    Rect --> Lbig[Large DC Link Inductor L]
    Lbig --> Idcnode[Constant DC Current Idc]
    Idcnode --> Sw1[Switch S1 with series diode]
    Idcnode --> Sw3[Switch S3 with series diode]
    Idcnode --> Sw5[Switch S5 with series diode]
    Sw1 --> PA[Phase A]
    Sw3 --> PB[Phase B]
    Sw5 --> PC[Phase C]
    Sw2[Switch S4 with series diode] --> PA
    Sw4[Switch S6 with series diode] --> PB
    Sw6[Switch S2 with series diode] --> PC
    Sw2 --> RetBus[Return Bus]
    Sw4 --> RetBus
    Sw6 --> RetBus
    RetBus --> Lbig
    PA --> LoadCSI[3 phase Load with shunt capacitors C for commutation]
    PB --> LoadCSI
    PC --> LoadCSI
    classDef src fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sw fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef load fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class ACsrc,Rect,Lbig,Idcnode,RetBus src
    class Sw1,Sw2,Sw3,Sw4,Sw5,Sw6,PA,PB,PC sw
    class LoadCSI load
```

> **Why series diodes?** CSI switches must block reverse voltage when OFF. Series diodes ensure one-way voltage blocking. **Why shunt capacitors at load?** They provide a leading current for load commutation in some CSI configurations.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define an inverter. With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a single-phase full-bridge voltage source inverter.**

**Model Answer:**

An inverter is a power electronic device that converts DC power into AC power at a desired voltage and frequency. The block diagram of a single-phase full-bridge VSI consists of:

1. **DC source** ($V_s$).
2. **Four controlled switches** (S1, S2, S3, S4 — typically IGBTs/MOSFETs with anti-parallel diodes).
3. **H-bridge configuration** with the load connected between the two midpoints.
4. **Gate control logic** producing complementary switching.

**Operation:**
- For $0 < t < T/2$: switches S1 and S2 are ON, current flows through the load from A to B, giving $V_o = +V_s$.
- For $T/2 < t < T$: switches S3 and S4 are ON, current reverses, giving $V_o = -V_s$.
- This produces a **square-wave AC output** with peak $\pm V_s$ and RMS $V_{o,rms} = V_s$.

**Marks distribution:** Definition (1M) + Block diagram (1M) + Operation (1M).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Compare 180° and 120° conduction modes of a three-phase voltage source inverter.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 180° Mode | 120° Mode |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Conduction per switch | 180° | 120° |
| Switches ON at any instant | 3 | 2 |
| Use of switches | Better utilisation | Lower utilisation |
| Reliability | Switch fault can short circuit | Open-circuit safe |
| Output waveform | 6-step line voltage | Same fundamental, more harmonics |
| Application | Standard motor drives | Special reliability-critical drives |

**Marks distribution:** Comparison points (any 3 valid) (3M).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 3 Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]
**(a) With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a three-phase voltage source inverter operating in 180° conduction mode. Draw the phase and line voltage waveforms for R load. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3 — 6 switches S1–S6, each with anti-parallel diode, fed from DC link $V_s$).

**Switching sequence (180° mode):**

Each switch conducts for 180°. The 3-phase output is generated by staggering the firing pulses by 120° between legs. At any instant, 3 switches are ON.

| Switch | Conduction period |
|--------|-------------------|
| S1 | 0° to 180° |
| S2 | 120° to 300° |
| S3 | 240° to 360° (= 0° to 120° in next cycle) |
| S4 | 180° to 360° |
| S5 | 300° to 60° (next cycle) |
| S6 | 60° to 240° |

**Intervals and conducting switches:**

| Interval | ON switches | $V_{ab}$ | $V_{bc}$ | $V_{ca}$ |
|----------|-------------|----------|----------|----------|
| 0°–60° | S1, S5, S6 | $+V_s$ | 0 | $-V_s$ |
| 60°–120° | S1, S2, S6 | 0 | $+V_s$ | $-V_s$ |
| 120°–180° | S1, S2, S3 | $-V_s$ | $+V_s$ | 0 |
| 180°–240° | S2, S3, S4 | $-V_s$ | 0 | $+V_s$ |
| 240°–300° | S3, S4, S5 | 0 | $-V_s$ | $+V_s$ |
| 300°–360° | S4, S5, S6 | $+V_s$ | $-V_s$ | 0 |

**Waveform shapes:**
- **Phase voltage $V_{an}$:** stepped wave with levels $2V_s/3, V_s/3, -V_s/3, -2V_s/3$.
- **Line voltage $V_{ab}$:** 6-step quasi-square wave with levels $+V_s, 0, -V_s$.

**Marks split:** Circuit diagram (2M) + Switching table (2M) + Waveform description (2M) + Output analysis (1M).

---

**(b) A 3-phase VSI operating in 180° mode is fed from a DC source of 220 V. Compute the fundamental RMS line voltage, RMS phase voltage, and the total RMS line voltage. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_s = 220$ V (DC bus voltage).

**Step 1 — Peak fundamental line voltage:**

$$V_{L1,pk} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_s = \dfrac{2 \times 1.732}{3.1416} \times 220 = 1.1026 \times 220 = 242.57 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — RMS fundamental line voltage:**

$$V_{L1,rms} = \dfrac{V_{L1,pk}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{242.57}{1.4142} \approx 171.5 \text{ V}$$

(Or using direct formula: $V_{L1,rms} = 0.7797 \, V_s = 0.7797 \times 220 \approx 171.5$ V ✓)

**Step 3 — Fundamental RMS phase voltage:**

$$V_{ph1,rms} = \dfrac{V_{L1,rms}}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{171.5}{1.732} \approx 99.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Total RMS line voltage (6-step wave):**

$$V_{L,rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} V_s = 0.8165 \times 220 \approx 179.6 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — THD of line voltage:**

$$THD = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{V_{L,rms}}{V_{L1,rms}}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{179.6}{171.5}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{1.0963 - 1} \approx 0.3104 \approx 31.04\%$$

**Step 6 — 5th harmonic RMS line voltage:**

$$V_{L5,rms} = \dfrac{1}{5} \cdot \dfrac{V_{L1,pk}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{242.57}{5\sqrt{2}} \approx 34.3 \text{ V}$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Fundamental peak line voltage | 242.57 V |
| Fundamental RMS line voltage | 171.5 V |
| Fundamental RMS phase voltage | 99.0 V |
| Total RMS line voltage | 179.6 V |
| THD | 31.04% |

**Marks split:** Fundamental calculation (2M) + RMS values (2M) + THD (2M) + Final summary (1M).

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]
**(a) Explain sinusoidal pulse width modulation (SPWM) technique for a single-phase full-bridge inverter. Derive the expression for the output voltage as a function of modulation index. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** In SPWM, the **widths of the output pulses** within each half-cycle are **modulated** to control the output voltage and reduce harmonics. A high-frequency **triangular carrier wave** is compared with a low-frequency **sinusoidal reference wave**.

**Principle of Operation:**
1. A reference sine wave $v_m(t) = A_m \sin(\omega_m t)$ of fundamental frequency $f_m$ is generated.
2. A triangular carrier wave $v_c(t)$ of frequency $f_c$ is also generated.
3. The two waves are fed to a comparator.
4. When $v_m > v_c$, the comparator output is HIGH → upper switch ON, lower OFF.
5. When $v_m < v_c$, the comparator output is LOW → upper switch OFF, lower ON.
6. This produces a PWM pulse train at the inverter output.

**Modulation Index:**

$$m_a = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$$

**Frequency Modulation Ratio:**

$$m_f = \dfrac{f_c}{f_m}$$

**For a full-bridge inverter with bipolar SPWM:**

**Output fundamental peak voltage** in the **linear region** ($m_a \le 1$):

$$V_{o1,pk} = m_a \cdot V_s$$

In the **overmodulation region** ($m_a > 1$), the relationship becomes non-linear, and the output approaches a square wave as $m_a \to \infty$, with:

$$V_{o1,pk,max} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \approx 1.273 \, V_s$$

**Harmonic content:** The carrier frequency $f_c$ and its sidebands dominate. With $m_f$ an odd multiple of 3, **triple harmonics are eliminated** in 3-phase operation.

**Marks split:** Concept and principle (2M) + Comparator logic (1.5M) + Modulation index (1.5M) + Output voltage expression (2M).

---

**(b) A 3-phase CSI operating in 120° conduction mode has a DC link current $I_d = 40$ A. Determine the fundamental RMS line current, and the amplitudes of the 5th and 7th harmonic line currents. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $I_d = 40$ A.

**Fourier series of 3-φ CSI line current (6-step waveform):**

$$i_L(t) = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} I_d \left[\sin(\omega t) - \dfrac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \dfrac{1}{7}\sin(7\omega t) - \dfrac{1}{11}\sin(11\omega t) + \cdots\right]$$

**Step 1 — Fundamental peak line current:**

$$I_{L1,pk} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} I_d = \dfrac{2 \times 1.732}{3.1416} \times 40 = 1.1026 \times 40 = 44.10 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Fundamental RMS line current:**

$$I_{L1,rms} = \dfrac{I_{L1,pk}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{44.10}{1.4142} \approx 31.18 \text{ A}$$

(Or using direct formula: $I_{L1,rms} = (\sqrt{6}/\pi) I_d = 0.7797 \times 40 = 31.19$ A ✓)

**Step 3 — 5th harmonic peak line current:**

$$I_{L5,pk} = -\dfrac{1}{5} \cdot \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} I_d = -\dfrac{44.10}{5} = -8.82 \text{ A}$$

$$\vert I_{L5,pk} \vert = 8.82 \text{ A}, \quad I_{L5,rms} = \dfrac{8.82}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 6.24 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — 7th harmonic peak line current:**

$$I_{L7,pk} = \dfrac{1}{7} \cdot \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} I_d = \dfrac{44.10}{7} = 6.30 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{L7,rms} = \dfrac{6.30}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 4.46 \text{ A}$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Harmonic | Peak (A) | RMS (A) |
|----------|----------|---------|
| 1st (fundamental) | 44.10 | 31.18 |
| 5th | 8.82 | 6.24 |
| 7th | 6.30 | 4.46 |

**Marks split:** Fourier expression (2M) + Fundamental calculation (2M) + 5th harmonic (1.5M) + 7th harmonic (1.5M).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing 180° conduction (each switch for 180°) with 120° (each for 120°).** Many students write 180° and 120° as if they are the phase displacement of the three references — they are NOT. They are the conduction duration of each switch.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert peak to RMS.** When the question asks for RMS line voltage, do not just compute $V_{L1,pk}$. Always divide by $\sqrt{2}$.
>
> 3. **Phase voltage vs Line voltage confusion.** For a 3-φ inverter, line voltage is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase voltage. The 6-step waveform applies to **line-to-line** voltage, while the phase voltage has 4 distinct levels ($\pm 2V_s/3, \pm V_s/3$).
>
> 4. **Including triple harmonics (3, 9, 15) in line-to-line calculations.** These are eliminated in the line-to-line voltage of a balanced 3-φ system. Always mention this fact.
>
> 5. **VSI vs CSI mix-up.** In VSI, capacitor filters → output voltage is independent of load. In CSI, inductor filters → output current is independent of load. Don't apply VSI formulas to CSI.
>
> 6. **PWM: forgetting the linear region condition.** The relation $V_{o1,pk} = m_a V_s$ holds only for $m_a \le 1$. For $m_a > 1$, the output saturates.
>
> 7. **In Full-Bridge bipolar SPWM, the load sees $\pm V_s$** (not $\pm V_s/2$). The factor of 2 comes from the differential switching of the two legs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inverter = DC to AC converter.** Two main types: **VSI** (DC voltage source) and **CSI** (DC current source).
- **Single-phase VSI topologies:** Half-bridge (peak $V_s/2$), Full-bridge (peak $V_s$), Push-pull.
- **Three-phase VSI** uses **6 switches** in bridge configuration. Two conduction modes:
  - **180° mode** → 3 switches ON at any time, 6-step line voltage, THD ≈ 31%.
  - **120° mode** → 2 switches ON at any time, fault-tolerant but lower utilisation.
- **Fourier of 6-step line voltage:** only **odd, non-triple harmonics** (1, 5, 7, 11, 13, …).
  - Magnitude: $V_{L,n} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{n\pi} \cos(n\pi/6)$.
  - Fundamental RMS line voltage: $V_{L1,rms} = 0.7797 \, V_s$.
- **Phase voltage** of 3-φ VSI has 4 levels: $\pm 2V_s/3, \pm V_s/3$.
- **PWM (especially SPWM)** uses a **sinusoidal reference** and a **triangular carrier**.
  - **Modulation index** $m_a = A_m/A_c$ controls output voltage.
  - **Frequency ratio** $m_f = f_c/f_m$ (must be odd multiple of 3 for synchronised 3-φ).
  - In linear region, $V_{o1,pk} = m_a \cdot V_s$ (full-bridge).
- **CSI:** large series inductor on DC side, **6-step output current**.
  - Fundamental RMS line current: $I_{L1,rms} = (\sqrt{6}/\pi) I_d \approx 0.7797 \, I_d$.
  - Series diodes with switches; shunt capacitors at load for commutation.
- **THD of 3-φ VSI line voltage (180° mode) ≈ 31%.**
- **Series inverter** — resonant commutation using $L-C$ load.
- **Parallel inverter** — capacitor commutation; provides square wave.
- **Bridge inverter** — most popular; full-bridge delivers 4× power of half-bridge at same DC bus.
- **KTU Favourite Topics:** (i) Derivation of 6-step line voltage harmonic series, (ii) 180° vs 120° switching table, (iii) SPWM with $m_a$ and $m_f$, (iv) CSI output current Fourier, (v) Numerical problems on VSI/CSI.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AC-AC Converters: Single-Phase and Three-Phase AC-AC Converters, Cycloconverters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT747 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** AC-AC Converters: Single-Phase and Three-Phase AC-AC Converters, Cycloconverters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **AC-AC Converter** is a power electronic converter that converts an AC input voltage of fixed magnitude and fixed frequency into an AC output voltage of variable magnitude and/or variable frequency, without an intermediate DC link. The conversion is achieved by employing **thyristor-based switching devices** (SCRs, TRIACs, or modern IGBT/MOSFET bidirectional switches) that chop the input AC waveform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Classification:**
> AC-AC Converters are classified into two major families:
> 1. **AC Voltage Controllers (AVC)** – convert fixed-magnitude, fixed-frequency AC to variable-magnitude AC at the **same frequency**. (Also called *Phase-Controlled Converters*.)
> 2. **Cycloconverters** – convert fixed-magnitude, fixed-frequency AC to variable-magnitude, **variable-frequency** AC (typically lower frequency).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **water tap (faucet)** connected to a municipal water pipeline.

- The pipeline delivers water at a **fixed pressure** and **continuous flow** — this is your input AC mains (e.g., 230 V, 50 Hz).
- You want to control how much water comes out of your garden hose — this is your load (e.g., a lamp dimmer, heater, or induction motor).
- A **valve** at the tap regulates flow without needing a pump — this valve is your **thyristor pair**. By opening the valve later in each cycle (delayed firing), you "chop off" the beginning of every pressure pulse, reducing the average flow.

In the **lamp dimmer** of your living room, a small **TRIAC** does exactly this: it delays conduction within each half-cycle of the 50 Hz supply, reducing brightness without changing frequency.

A **cycloconverter**, by contrast, is like a **gearbox** on a bicycle: it converts high-speed pedaling into a slower rotation at the wheel, but with controllable torque and direction.

## 1.3 Common Applications

> [!NOTE]
> - **Lighting control** (theater dimmers, street lighting)
> - **Industrial heating** (furnaces, resistance welding)
> - **Induction motor soft-starters**
> - **Transformer tap-changers** (solid-state replacements)
> - **Large AC drives** (rolling mills, cement kilns, ship propellers — where cycloconverters feed synchronous motors at very low frequencies)

## 1.4 Key Metrics at a Glance

- **Supply Frequency ($f_s$):** Typically **50 Hz** in India (KTU standard).
- **Firing Angle ($\alpha$):** Range typically **0° to 180°** for full-wave controllers.
- **Conduction Angle ($\gamma$):** $\gamma = 180° - \alpha$ for half-wave; up to **360°** for full-wave.
- **Power Factor:** Always **lagging** and degraded compared to the supply, since firing delay introduces displacement and harmonics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 AC Voltage Controllers — Operational Principles

A phase-controlled AC Voltage Controller uses **thyristors connected in anti-parallel** (or a single TRIAC) to chop each half-cycle of the AC supply.

### 2.1.1 Types of AC Voltage Controllers

| Type | Topology | Conduction |
|------|----------|------------|
| **Unidirectional (Half-Wave)** | One SCR + One Diode (anti-parallel) | One direction only |
| **Bidirectional (Full-Wave)** | Two SCRs anti-parallel OR a TRIAC | Both directions |

### 2.1.2 Two Control Strategies

**A. ON-OFF Control (Integral Cycle Control / Burst Firing)**
- The thyristor is turned ON for $n$ complete cycles and OFF for $m$ complete cycles.
- Used for loads with high thermal inertia (heaters).
- Output frequency = Supply frequency, but RMS voltage is varied.

**B. Phase-Angle Control (Phase Control)**
- Each thyristor is fired at a delay angle $\alpha$ after the zero-crossing of its forward-biased half-cycle.
- The conduction angle $\gamma = 180° - \alpha$ (for half-wave); for full-wave both halves conduct symmetrically.
- Provides smooth, continuous control of RMS output voltage.

## 2.2 Single-Phase Full-Wave AC Voltage Controller

For a **resistive load** $R$ supplied by $V_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:

- **SCR T₁** conducts from $\alpha$ to $\pi$ (positive half-cycle).
- **SCR T₂** conducts from $\pi + \alpha$ to $2\pi$ (negative half-cycle).

### Derivation of RMS Output Voltage

The RMS output over one full cycle is given by:

$$V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$$

### Derivation of Average Output Voltage

Since the waveform is symmetric over a full cycle, the **average DC value is zero**:

$$V_{o,avg} = 0 \quad \text{(for symmetric full-wave control)}$$

For **half-wave** (unidirectional) control, the average is non-zero:

$$V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$$

## 2.3 Three-Phase AC Voltage Controllers

Three topologies are commonly used in KTU syllabus:

1. **3-Phase, 3-Wire Star with Neutral** – Three independent single-phase controllers; line voltage uncontrolled.
2. **3-Phase, 3-Wire Star without Neutral** – Six SCRs in anti-parallel pairs on three phases; no neutral.
3. **3-Phase, 4-Wire Delta / Wye** – Each phase independently controlled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **3-phase, 3-wire star** configuration, the firing angle range is restricted to **0° to 150°** (not 180°) due to natural commutation overlap with adjacent phases.

## 2.4 Cycloconverters

A cycloconverter is a **frequency changer** that synthesizes a lower-frequency output directly from the AC supply by selecting appropriate portions of successive input cycles through phase-controlled thyristor bridges.

### 2.4.1 Types

| Type | Pulse Number ($p$) | Output Frequency Range |
|------|:---:|:---:|
| **1-Phase Half-Wave (Step-Down)** | 1 | $0 < f_o < f_s/2$ |
| **1-Phase Full-Wave (Bridge)** | 2 | $0 < f_o < f_s$ |
| **3-Phase Half-Wave** | 3 | $0 < f_o < f_s/3$ |
| **3-Phase Full-Wave (Bridge)** | 6 | $0 < f_o < 2f_s/3$ |

### 2.4.2 Operating Principle

- **Positive Converter (P-Converter):** Conducts when output current $i_o > 0$ (allows power flow from supply to load).
- **Negative Converter (N-Converter):** Conducts when $i_o < 0$ (allows power flow from load back to supply).
- A **circulating current** flows between P and N bridges; **circulating-current-free cycloconverters** use logic to ensure only one bridge operates at a time.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-YIELD for KTU University Examinations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary Condition |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | RMS Output Voltage (1-φ Full-Wave, R-load) | $V_{o,rms} = V_s\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| 2 | RMS Output Voltage (1-φ Half-Wave, R-load) | $V_{o,rms} = \dfrac{V_s}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| 3 | Average Output Voltage (1-φ Half-Wave) | $V_{o,avg} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| 4 | Thyristor Conduction Angle | $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$ | (radians) |
| 5 | Power Factor (R-load) | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{V_{o,rms}}{V_s} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| 6 | Output Frequency (Cycloconverter) | $f_o = \dfrac{f_s}{n}$, where $n$ = ratio of input cycles per output cycle | $n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| 7 | Voltage Ratio (Cycloconverter) | $r = \dfrac{V_{o,L}}{V_{s,L}} = \dfrac{p \cdot V_m \sin(\pi/p)}{\pi} \cos\alpha$ | $p$ = pulse number |
| 8 | Output Voltage (Cycloconverter) | $v_o(t) = \sqrt{2}\,V_{s,ph}\dfrac{p}{\pi}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{p}\right)\sin(\omega_o t)$ | (ideal, $\alpha = 0$) |
| 9 | Max Output Frequency (Cycloconverter) | $f_{o,max} = \dfrac{f_s}{p/2}$ (3-φ bridge) | limits distortion |
| 10 | Harmonic Frequencies | $f_h = \vert (mp \pm 1) \vert f_o + 2nf_s$ | $m, n$ integers |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cycloconverters** are the workhorse of **high-power, low-speed AC drives** (e.g., gearless cement mills at 5–10 rpm using 50/3 ≈ 16.7 Hz cycloconverter-fed synchronous motors).
- **AC voltage controllers** are preferred over DC choppers for **transformer-coupled loads** because commutation naturally occurs at AC zero-crossings.
- **Static VAR compensators (SVCs)** use thyristor-controlled reactors (TCR) — essentially 3-phase AC voltage controllers — to dynamically regulate reactive power in power grids.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RMS Output Voltage of Single-Phase Full-Wave AC Voltage Controller (R-Load)

**Problem Setup:**
- Supply: $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- Load: Resistive $R$
- Firing angle of T₁: $\alpha$ (positive half)
- Firing angle of T₂: $\pi + \alpha$ (negative half)
- T₁ conducts on $[\alpha, \pi]$; T₂ conducts on $[\pi+\alpha, 2\pi]$

**Definition of RMS:**
By definition, the RMS value of a periodic voltage over one full cycle $T$ is:

$$V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v_o^2(t)\, dt}$$

Because the waveform is **periodic with period $T = 2\pi/\omega$** and **symmetric over half-cycles** (positive and negative halves are mirror images), we can integrate over just the positive half-cycle and divide by $\pi$:

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_\alpha^\pi V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 1: Apply the half-angle identity.**
The identity $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$ converts the integrand into a tractable form:

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{\pi}\int_\alpha^\pi \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 2: Split the integral.**
We split the integral into a constant part and a cosine part:

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi}\left[\int_\alpha^\pi 1\, d(\omega t) - \int_\alpha^\pi \cos(2\omega t)\, d(\omega t)\right]$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the constant integral.**

$$\int_\alpha^\pi 1\, d(\omega t) = \pi - \alpha$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the cosine integral.**
Using $\int \cos(kx)\,dx = \frac{1}{k}\sin(kx)$:

$$\int_\alpha^\pi \cos(2\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \left[\frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\right]_\alpha^\pi = \frac{\sin(2\pi) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2} = -\frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}$$

**Step 5: Substitute back.**

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) - \left(-\frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right)\right] = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]$$

**Step 6: Take the square root and convert to $V_s$.**
Since $V_s = V_m/\sqrt{2}$, we substitute $V_m = \sqrt{2}\, V_s$:

$$\boxed{V_{o,rms} = V_s\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right)}}$$

**Step 7: Limiting Cases — Sanity Check.**

| Limit | Formula gives | Physical Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| $\alpha = 0$ | $V_{o,rms} = V_s\sqrt{1} = V_s$ | Full conduction — same as supply. ✔ |
| $\alpha = \pi$ | $V_{o,rms} = 0$ | No conduction — output is zero. ✔ |
| $\alpha = \pi/2$ | $V_{o,rms} = V_s\sqrt{0.5 + 0} \approx 0.707\,V_s$ | Half-power delivered to R-load. ✔ |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of Single-Phase Half-Wave AC Voltage Controller (R-Load)

For a **half-wave** (unidirectional) controller, only T₁ conducts from $\alpha$ to $\pi$, and T₂ is replaced by a diode (always conducting the negative half-cycle but only one direction flows to load).

**Definition of Average:**
For a periodic signal, the average over one period is:

$$V_{o,avg} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} v_o(t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 1: Identify non-zero intervals.**
Output is non-zero only on $[\alpha, \pi]$ with value $v_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. On $[\pi, 2\pi]$, the output is **zero** (half-wave; no negative thyristor).

$$V_{o,avg} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_\alpha^\pi V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the integral.**
Using $\int \sin(x)\,dx = -\cos(x)$:

$$V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_\alpha^\pi = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\left[-\cos\pi + \cos\alpha\right]$$

**Step 3: Simplify using $\cos\pi = -1$.**

$$V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\left[-(-1) + \cos\alpha\right] = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$$

$$\boxed{V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)}$$

**Step 4: Limiting Cases.**

| Limit | Formula gives | Physical Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| $\alpha = 0$ | $V_{o,avg} = V_m/\pi$ | Standard half-wave rectified average. ✔ |
| $\alpha = \pi$ | $V_{o,avg} = 0$ | No conduction. ✔ |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cycloconverter Output Frequency & Voltage Ratio

**Setup:** A 3-φ, 6-pulse bridge cycloconverter fed from a $V_{s,LL}$ line-to-line supply.

**Step 1: Output frequency for a step-down cycloconverter.**
The cycloconverter synthesizes one output cycle from $n$ input cycles:

$$f_o = \frac{f_s}{n}, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$$

**Step 2: Voltage ratio of a $p$-pulse converter.**
For a $p$-pulse phase-controlled converter, the average output DC voltage (ignoring overlap) is:

$$V_{do} = \frac{p \cdot V_m}{\pi} \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{p}\right)$$

When the firing angle $\alpha$ is modulated sinusoidally to follow the desired output sinusoid $V_o \sin(\omega_o t)$, the instantaneous firing angle obeys:

$$\cos\alpha(t) = \frac{V_o}{V_{do,\,max}} \sin(\omega_o t)$$

For the 6-pulse case ($p = 6$): $V_{do,max} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi}V_m$ (where $V_m$ is the phase peak voltage).

**Step 3: Limiting output frequency.**
To avoid subharmonic distortion and ensure proper commutation:

$$f_{o,max} \approx \frac{f_s}{p/2}$$

For $p = 6$, $f_s = 50$ Hz:

$$f_{o,max} = \frac{50}{3} \approx 16.67 \text{ Hz}$$

---

## 3.4 Harmonic Spectrum (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiners often ask: "What harmonics are present in a cycloconverter output?"**

For a cycloconverter with output frequency $f_o$, supply frequency $f_s$, and pulse number $p$:

$$f_h = \left|(mp \pm 1)f_o + 2nf_s\right|, \quad m, n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

The **dominant unwanted harmonics** cluster around the supply frequency sidebands:

- For $p = 6$ bridge: dominant harmonics near $6f_o$, $6f_o \pm f_s$, $12f_o$, etc.
- The output fundamental $f_o$ is the **desired component**, with $f_s - f_o$ and $f_s + f_o$ being the most objectionable intermodulation products.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. Define an AC voltage controller. Mention its two control strategies.**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

An **AC voltage controller** is a power electronic converter that converts a fixed AC supply voltage into a variable AC output voltage at the **same frequency**, using thyristor-based phase-controlled switching.

The two control strategies are:
1. **ON-OFF Control (Integral Cycle Control):** Thyristor is switched ON for $n$ cycles and OFF for $m$ cycles; suitable for loads with high thermal inertia (heaters).
2. **Phase-Angle Control:** The thyristor firing is delayed by an angle $\alpha$ within each half-cycle; provides smooth, continuous control of RMS output voltage.

---

### **Q2. Define a cycloconverter. State its main advantage.**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

A **cycloconverter** is a static power electronic frequency changer that converts an AC input voltage of fixed frequency $f_s$ into an AC output voltage of variable magnitude and a **lower variable frequency** $f_o$, without any intermediate DC link.

**Main advantage:** It permits **direct frequency conversion at very high power levels** (multi-MW drives such as rolling mills, cement kilns, and ship propulsion) with **inherent regenerative braking capability** — power flows back from the load to the supply during deceleration.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, students answer ONE of the two internal choices.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a single-phase full-wave AC voltage controller feeding a resistive load. Derive the expression for its RMS output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Adapted]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Circuit Description:**
A single-phase full-wave AC voltage controller consists of **two SCRs (T₁ and T₂) connected in anti-parallel** across an AC supply $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. The load is a resistor $R$ connected in series.

**2. Working Principle:**
- **Positive Half-Cycle $(0 \le \omega t \le \pi)$:** T₁ is forward-biased; it is triggered at firing angle $\alpha$. T₁ conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\pi$, and load voltage follows the supply.
- **Negative Half-Cycle $(\pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi)$:** T₂ is forward-biased; it is triggered at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$. T₂ conducts from $\pi+\alpha$ to $2\pi$.

**3. RMS Output Voltage Derivation:**
By definition (full derivation provided in **Section 3.1** above):

$$V_{o,rms} = V_s\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$$

**4. Verification of Limiting Cases:**
- At $\alpha = 0$: $V_{o,rms} = V_s$ ✔ (Full output)
- At $\alpha = \pi$: $V_{o,rms} = 0$ ✔ (No output)

#### **(b)** A single-phase full-wave AC voltage controller feeds a resistive load of $R = 10\ \Omega$ from a 230 V, 50 Hz supply. If the firing angle is $\alpha = 60°$, calculate: (i) RMS output voltage, (ii) RMS load current, (iii) Power delivered to the load. **(7 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Adapted]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_s = 230$ V, $R = 10\ \Omega$, $\alpha = 60° = \pi/3$, $f = 50$ Hz.

**Step 1: Compute RMS Output Voltage.**
Using the formula with $\alpha = \pi/3$:

$$\pi - \alpha = \pi - \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{2\pi}{3}$$

$$\sin(2\alpha) = \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

$$\frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \approx 0.4330$$

$$V_{o,rms} = 230\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{2\pi}{3} + 0.4330\right)} = 230\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}(2.0944 + 0.4330)}$$

$$V_{o,rms} = 230\sqrt{\frac{2.5274}{3.1416}} = 230\sqrt{0.8045} \approx 230 \times 0.8970 \approx 206.3\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2: Compute RMS Load Current.**

$$I_{o,rms} = \frac{V_{o,rms}}{R} = \frac{206.3}{10} = 20.63\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3: Compute Power Delivered.**

$$P = I_{o,rms}^2 \cdot R = (20.63)^2 \times 10 = 4256\ \text{W} \approx 4.26\ \text{kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> A common mistake is to compute $V_{o,rms}$ as $V_s \cos\alpha$ (which is the AVERAGE output of a controlled RECTIFIER, not the RMS of an AC VOLTAGE CONTROLLER). The correct expression always has the $(\pi - \alpha + \sin 2\alpha/2)$ form. Students who write $V_{o,rms} = V_s \cos(\alpha/2)$ for AC voltage controllers lose **3 marks** instantly.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a single-phase to single-phase cycloconverter with a circuit diagram. Discuss its limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2022 Adapted]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition:**
A single-phase cycloconverter consists of **two anti-parallel thyristor bridges** — a Positive (P) converter and a Negative (N) converter — connected to the same single-phase AC supply and the same load.

**2. Operation Principle:**
- Each bridge is itself a single-phase fully-controlled bridge rectifier.
- The firing angles of the SCRs are **continuously modulated sinusoidally** so that the **average output voltage** of each bridge varies as a sinusoidal function of time at the desired output frequency $f_o$.
- During the **positive half of the output cycle**, only the **P-converter** operates (carries the load current $i_o$); the **N-converter is blocked** by a control logic called the **circulating-current-free** mode.
- During the **negative half of the output cycle**, roles reverse — only the N-converter operates.
- A **zero-crossing detector** and **logic circuit** ensure smooth hand-off between bridges at the output zero-crossings.

**3. Waveform Synthesis (Concept):**
If 4 input cycles are needed to construct 1 output cycle ($f_o = f_s/4$), then each input half-cycle is selected with a firing angle chosen such that the average over that half-cycle traces one point on the desired sinusoid. The selected portions, when joined, approximate a low-frequency sinusoid.

**4. Limitations:**
- Output frequency is **limited to about $f_s/3$** (for 3-pulse) or $f_s/2$ (for 6-pulse) to keep distortion acceptable.
- **Sub-harmonics and intermodulation products** are present near the supply frequency.
- Requires **many thyristors** (12 SCRs per phase for a 3-phase bridge cycloconverter).
- **Power factor is poor**, especially at low output voltages (deep firing angles).

#### **(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pulse cycloconverter supplies a load. The peak supply phase voltage is 230 V. Find the maximum RMS output phase voltage obtainable (assuming $\alpha_{min} = 0$). **(7 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $p = 6$, $V_m = 230$ V (peak phase), $\alpha = 0$.

**Step 1: Compute the Maximum Average DC Voltage.**
For a $p$-pulse converter:

$$V_{do,max} = \frac{p\,V_m}{\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{p}\right) = \frac{6 \times 230}{\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the Sine Term.**
$\sin(\pi/6) = \sin(30°) = 0.5$.

**Step 3: Substitute.**

$$V_{do,max} = \frac{6 \times 230 \times 0.5}{\pi} = \frac{690}{\pi} \approx 219.7\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4: Convert to RMS Output Phase Voltage.**
For a sinusoidal modulation envelope, the RMS value is $\dfrac{V_{do,max}}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$V_{o,rms,\,max} = \frac{V_{do,max}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{219.7}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 155.3\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{o,rms,\,max} \approx 155.3\ \text{V per phase}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Do NOT confuse "**peak phase voltage**" with "**line voltage**" in cycloconverter formulas. Always check whether the formula uses $V_m$ (phase peak) or $V_{LL,\text{peak}}$. For a 6-pulse bridge, the standard form uses **phase peak**, and if you mistakenly plug in the line voltage, you will be off by a factor of $\sqrt{3}$ and lose 4 marks. Always state your assumption clearly in the solution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for AC-AC Converters (Module 3)**

- [ ] AC-AC converters = **no DC link**; direct frequency/magnitude conversion.
- [ ] AC Voltage Controller = **same frequency**, variable magnitude (uses anti-parallel SCRs or TRIAC).
- [ ] **Two control methods:** ON-OFF (integral cycle) & Phase-Angle (delayed firing).
- [ ] **Full-wave RMS:** $V_{o,rms} = V_s\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ — memorize this!
- [ ] **Half-wave average:** $V_{o,avg} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$.
- [ ] **3-φ, 3-wire star** firing angle is restricted to **0° to 150°** (not 180°).
- [ ] Cycloconverter = **variable frequency** output synthesized from line-frequency supply.
- [ ] **Pulse number** $p$: 1-φ half = 1, 1-φ bridge = 2, 3-φ half = 3, 3-φ bridge = 6.
- [ ] **Max output frequency:** $f_{o,max} = \dfrac{f_s}{p/2}$ (e.g., 16.67 Hz for 6-pulse, 50 Hz supply).
- [ ] **Voltage ratio:** $V_{do,max} = \dfrac{pV_m}{\pi}\sin(\pi/p)$.
- [ ] Two bridges (P & N) handle **positive and negative load current**; **circulating-current-free** mode uses logic to keep one bridge OFF at a time.
- [ ] **Harmonic frequencies:** $f_h = |(mp \pm 1)f_o + 2nf_s|$ — note supply-side intermodulation sidebands.
- [ ] **Power factor is always lagging** in AC voltage controllers due to delayed firing.
- [ ] **Applications:** Lamp dimmers, heater control (AVC); rolling mills, cement kilns, ship propulsion (cycloconverter).
- [ ] **Remember the symbol table:** $\alpha$ = firing angle; $\gamma$ = conduction angle; $p$ = pulse number; $r$ = voltage ratio; $f_o, f_s$ = output and supply frequencies.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Motor Control - Phase Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT747 - POWER ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** DC Motor Control - Phase Control # 🔌 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Phase Control of DC Motor** is a speed-control technique in which the **AC supply voltage is rectified using thyristor-based phase-controlled converters**, and the **firing angle (α)** of the thyristors is varied to regulate the **average DC output voltage** delivered to the armature of the DC motor. By changing this average armature voltage, the **motor speed is smoothly and continuously varied** from zero to its rated (base) speed — a method commonly known as **"below-base speed control"** or **"constant-torque region operation."** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** > *"Phase-controlled converters (semi-converter, full-converter, and dual-converter) feeding DC motor drives — analysis with continuous and discontinuous armature current, and derivation of speed–torque characteristics."* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tap Analogy" Imagine a **water tap connected to a large overhead tank** supplying water to a turbine (the DC motor). | Real-World Element | Power Electronics Equivalent | |---|---| | 🚰 Open tap (full flow) | Thyristor fired at **α = 0°** (fully ON, maximum voltage) | | 🚰 Half-closed tap (reduced flow) | Thyristor fired at **α = 90°** (half voltage) | | 🚰 Almost closed tap (trickle) | Thyristor fired at **α = 150°** (very low voltage) | | 🛑 Fully closed tap (no flow) | Thyristor fired at **α = 180°** (no voltage, motor stops) | Just as a tap regulates the **average water flow per second** without changing the pipe size, the **firing angle α** regulates the **average DC voltage** fed to the motor, thereby controlling its **speed and torque** — **without dissipating power in a series resistor (unlike rheostatic control)**. --- ## 1.3 Why "Phase" Control? The name "Phase" comes from the fact that the **thyristor is triggered at a specific phase angle (α)** of the AC supply voltage waveform. The AC mains voltage is a sinusoidal waveform, and the **point on the wave where the gate pulse is applied** determines how much of the positive half-cycle is delivered to the load. This is fundamentally different from **chopper control** (DC–DC) or **integral cycle control** (used for AC loads). > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology for KTU Exams:** > - **Firing Angle (α):** The electrical angle (in degrees) measured from the instant the thyristor becomes forward-biased to the instant the gate pulse is applied. Range: **0° ≤ α ≤ 180°** for full converter. > - **Conduction Angle (γ):** The angle during which the thyristor actually conducts current. > - **Extinction Angle (β):** The angle at which the thyristor turns OFF. > - **Overlap Angle (μ):** The angle during which both incoming and outgoing thyristors conduct simultaneously (in case of source inductance). --- ## 1.4 Block Diagram — Phase-Controlled DC Drive System ``` AC Mains → Phase-Controlled Converter → DC Motor (V_s) (Thyristor Bridge) (Armature) ↑ Gate Pulse (α = variable) ↑ Firing Circuit (Microcontroller / UJT / TCA785) ``` > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** Phase-controlled DC drives are widely used in industries such as **paper mills, steel rolling mills, textile machines, printing presses, elevators, and cranes**, where **precise, smooth, and reversible speed control** is essential with **high starting torque**. # ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How" The fundamental principle of phase control rests on three pillars: ### **Pillar 1: Phase-Angle Modulation** The **gate pulse** to the thyristor is delayed by an angle **α** from the natural commutation point (the instant the thyristor becomes forward-biased). This means the thyristor only begins conducting **after α electrical degrees**, chopping off a portion of the AC waveform. $$\text{Natural Commutation Point} = 0° \text{ (for thyristor } T_1 \text{)} \rightarrow \text{Gate Pulse Applied} = \alpha$$ ### **Pillar 2: Average Voltage Regulation** The RMS and average values of the chopped waveform are functions of α. By varying α from **0° to 180°**, the **average output voltage** is varied from its **maximum value (α=0°)** down to **zero (α=180°)**. ### **Pillar 3: Motor Back-EMF Interaction** A DC motor is not a passive resistor. It develops a **back-EMF (E_b = kΦω)** proportional to speed. The net voltage driving armature current is: $$V_0 - E_b = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt}$$ This means the **actual operating point of the motor** is the intersection of the **converter's V-I characteristic** and the **motor's back-EMF line**. --- ## 2.2 Modes of Operation — Continuous vs. Discontinuous Conduction | Mode | Condition | Characteristics | |---|---|---| | **Continuous Conduction (CCM)** | Motor inductance **L_a** is large enough; load current never falls to zero | **θ = α** (current starts immediately at firing), smooth torque, well-defined equations | | **Discontinuous Conduction (DCM)** | **L_a** is small OR load is very light; current becomes zero before next thyristor pair fires | Current pulses, **θ > α**, torque is pulsating, equations become complex | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Hot Spot:** Most numerical problems in KTU assume **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** unless stated otherwise. **Discontinuous conduction** is usually asked as a **derivation/conceptual** question in Part B (14 marks). --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **📋 The following table is the EXAM GOLD — memorize every row!** ### **A. Single-Phase Converters Feeding Separately Excited DC Motor** | Converter Type | Average Output Voltage V₀ | Boundary α Range | RMS Output V_rms | Used For | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Half-Wave Converter** | $V_0 = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | 0° ≤ α ≤ 180° | $\dfrac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi-\alpha+\dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | Low-power, demo only | | **Semi-Converter (Half-Controlled)** | $V_0 = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | 0° ≤ α ≤ 180° | $V_m\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left(\pi-\alpha+\dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | Single-quadrant drive | | **Full-Converter (Fully-Controlled)** | $V_0 = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | 0° ≤ α ≤ 90° → +V (Forward motoring) | $V_m\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi-\alpha+\dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | Two-quadrant drive | | **Full-Converter (Inversion Mode)** | $V_0 = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | 90° < α ≤ 180° → −V (Regenerative braking) | Same as above | Regenerative braking | ### **B. Three-Phase Converters Feeding DC Motor** | Converter Type | Average Output Voltage V₀ | Boundary α Range | |---|---|---| | **Three-Phase Half-Wave (3-Pulse)** | $V_0 = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{2\pi}\cos\alpha$ | 0° ≤ α ≤ 90° | | **Three-Phase Semi-Converter (6-Pulse, half-controlled)** | $V_0 = \dfrac{3V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | 0° ≤ α ≤ 180° | | **Three-Phase Full-Converter (6-Pulse, fully-controlled)** | $V_0 = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | 0° ≤ α ≤ 90° (motoring); 90°–180° (regeneration) | ### **C. DC Motor Equations (Universal — Apply to ALL Converter Types)** | Parameter | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | **Back-EMF** | $E_b = k\Phi\,\omega = \dfrac{V_0 - I_a R_a}{}$ | Voltage generated by rotating armature | | **Armature Current** | $I_a = \dfrac{V_0 - E_b}{R_a}$ | Steady-state armature current | | **Motor Speed (rad/s)** | $\omega = \dfrac{V_0 - I_a R_a}{k\Phi}$ | Mechanical angular velocity | | **Motor Speed (RPM)** | $N = \dfrac{V_0 - I_a R_a}{k\Phi} \times \dfrac{60}{2\pi}$ | Speed in revolutions per minute | | **Torque Developed** | $T = k\Phi\,I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque | | **Power Developed** | $P_d = E_b \cdot I_a = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power developed | > Where: > - $V_m$ = Peak phase voltage of AC supply > - $V_s$ = RMS phase voltage = $V_m/\sqrt{2}$ > - $\alpha$ = Firing angle (electrical degrees) > - $R_a$ = Armature resistance (Ω) > - $L_a$ = Armature inductance (H) > - $k$ = Machine constant > - $\Phi$ = Field flux per pole (Wb) > - $I_a$ = Average armature current (A) > - $E_b$ = Back-EMF (V) --- ## 2.4 Quadrant Operation of Phase-Controlled DC Drive | Quadrant | Operation | Converter Mode | Firing Angle | V₀ Polarity | Current Direction | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **I** | Forward Motoring | Full Conv. (α < 90°) | 0°–90° | + | + | | **II** | Forward Braking (Regen) | Full Conv. (α > 90°) | 90°–180° | − | + | | **III** | Reverse Motoring | Full Conv. (α < 90°) | 0°–90° | − | − | | **IV** | Reverse Braking (Regen) | Full Conv. (α > 90°) | 90°–180° | + | − | > [!TIP] > **Single full-converter** = **2 quadrants (I & IV)** with free-wheeling diode, OR **(I & II)** with firing angle control. > **Dual-converter** (two full converters in anti-parallel) = **All 4 quadrants** → used in **reversing mill drives**. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Converter Type Used | Why? | |---|---|---| | 🏭 **Steel Rolling Mills** | Three-phase full-converter or dual-converter | High power (MW range), need 4-quadrant reversing | | 📰 **Printing Presses** | Single-phase full-converter | Moderate power, smooth tension control | | 🛗 **Elevators / Cranes** | Dual-converter | Frequent reversal + regenerative lowering | | 🧵 **Textile Machines** | Single-phase semi-converter | Low cost, single-quadrant sufficient | | 🚗 **Battery EV (legacy)** | Chopper (DC-DC), NOT phase-control | Phase-control needs AC mains — not for pure battery EV | | 🏥 **Hospital Equipment** | Phase-controlled precision drives | Smooth, precise low-speed control | --- ## 2.6 Speed-Torque Characteristics — The Most Important Graph For a phase-controlled DC drive, the **speed-torque curve is a STRAIGHT LINE** (in CCM), given by: $$N = \dfrac{V_0}{k\Phi} - \dfrac{R_a}{(k\Phi)^2}\,T$$ **Interpretation:** - **X-intercept (T-axis):** $T_0 = \dfrac{V_0 \cdot k\Phi}{R_a}$ → **Starting Torque** (at N = 0) - **Y-intercept (N-axis):** $N_0 = \dfrac{V_0}{k\Phi}$ → **No-Load Speed** (at T = 0) - **Slope:** $-\dfrac{R_a}{(k\Phi)^2}$ → The line is a "**drooping characteristic**" > **As α increases** → **V₀ decreases** → Both intercepts shrink → The **speed-torque line shifts downward and parallel** (slope unchanged since R_a and Φ are constant). # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation #1: Average Output Voltage of a Single-Phase Full Converter ### **Statement to Prove:** For a single-phase fully-controlled bridge converter (Graetz bridge) feeding a DC motor with **highly inductive load** (continuous conduction), the average output voltage is: $$V_0 = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$ ### **Circuit Topology:** ``` T1 T3 ●──▶──┬──▶──● (AC Source: V_s = V_m sin ωt) │ (Load: DC motor with L_a >> R_a) ●──▶──┴──▶──● T2 T4 D_fwd (Freewheeling diode) across load ``` ### **Step-by-Step Derivation:** **Step 1:** During the positive half-cycle, thyristors **T1 and T2** are forward-biased. They are triggered at ωt = α. Conduction begins. Output voltage equation during conduction (0 ≤ α ≤ π for full converter, conduction is from α to π+α): For one full cycle, T1-T2 conduct from **α to π**, and T3-T4 conduct from **π+α to 2π**. $$v_0(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{for } \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi$$ $$v_0(\omega t) = -V_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{for } (\pi + \alpha) \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi$$ **Step 2:** The average value of the output voltage is defined as: $$V_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} v_0(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute the piecewise expression: $$V_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left[\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} (-V_m \sin(\omega t))\, d(\omega t)\right]$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the first integral: $$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = V_m\left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = V_m\left[-\cos\pi + \cos\alpha\right] = V_m[1 + \cos\alpha]$$ **Step 5:** Evaluate the second integral: $$\int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} (-V_m \sin(\omega t))\, d(\omega t) = -V_m\left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} = V_m\left[\cos(2\pi) - \cos(\pi+\alpha)\right]$$ $$= V_m\left[1 - (-\cos\alpha)\right] = V_m[1 + \cos\alpha]$$ **Step 6:** Add both integrals and divide by 2π: $$V_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left[V_m(1+\cos\alpha) + V_m(1+\cos\alpha)\right] = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\cdot 2V_m(1+\cos\alpha)$$ $$V_0 = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$$ **Step 7:** ⚠️ **This is the result for a SEMI-CONVERTER!** For a **full converter** (no freewheeling action during commutation), the **reference shifts** because the natural commutation point is at π/2, not 0. The conduction is from α to π+α (twice the conduction angle). **Re-derivation for FULL converter** (use shifted reference: let θ = ωt − π/2): $$V_0 = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$ $$= \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\pi+\alpha) + \cos\alpha\right]$$ $$= \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\left[\cos\alpha + \cos\alpha\right] = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha \quad \blacksquare$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2: Speed-Torque Characteristic of a Converter-Fed DC Motor ### **Given:** A separately excited DC motor with armature resistance $R_a$, field flux $\Phi$, machine constant $k$, fed by a single-phase full converter with average voltage $V_0$. ### **To Derive:** The relationship $N = f(T)$ for steady-state operation. ### **Derivation:** **Step 1:** Write the steady-state armature voltage equation (L di/dt = 0): $$V_0 = E_b + I_a R_a$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $E_b = k\Phi\omega$: $$V_0 = k\Phi\omega + I_a R_a$$ **Step 3:** Solve for armature current $I_a$: $$I_a = \dfrac{V_0 - k\Phi\omega}{R_a}$$ **Step 4:** The electromagnetic torque developed is: $$T = k\Phi I_a = \dfrac{k\Phi(V_0 - k\Phi\omega)}{R_a}$$ **Step 5:** Solve for angular velocity $\omega$: $$T \cdot R_a = k\Phi V_0 - (k\Phi)^2 \omega$$ $$(k\Phi)^2 \omega = k\Phi V_0 - T R_a$$ $$\omega = \dfrac{V_0}{k\Phi} - \dfrac{R_a}{(k\Phi)^2}\,T \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$ **Step 6:** Convert to RPM by multiplying by 60/(2π): $$N = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\left[\dfrac{V_0}{k\Phi} - \dfrac{R_a}{(k\Phi)^2}\,T\right]$$ $$\boxed{N = \dfrac{60\,V_0}{2\pi\,k\Phi} - \dfrac{60\,R_a}{2\pi\,(k\Phi)^2}\,T}$$ **Step 7:** For a phase-controlled converter, $V_0 = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$, so: $$N = \dfrac{60}{2\pi\,k\Phi}\cdot\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha - \dfrac{60\,R_a}{2\pi\,(k\Phi)^2}\,T$$ $$\boxed{N = \dfrac{60\,V_m}{\pi^2\,k\Phi}\cos\alpha - \dfrac{60\,R_a}{2\pi\,(k\Phi)^2}\,T}$$ ### **Key Observations:** - **No-load speed** ($T=0$): $\quad N_0 = \dfrac{60 V_m}{\pi^2 k\Phi}\cos\alpha$ - **Starting torque** ($N=0$): $\quad T_{st} = \dfrac{2 V_m k\Phi}{\pi R_a}\cos\alpha$ - **Slope of N-T line:** $\quad -\dfrac{60 R_a}{2\pi(k\Phi)^2}$ — **INDEPENDENT of α** (since R_a and Φ are fixed) - **Effect of α:** As α increases from 0° to 90°, cos α decreases, so the **entire N-T line shifts downward parallel to itself**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Problem-Solving Pattern (KTU Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the EXACT pattern examiners use. Memorize the sequence!** | Step | Action | Formula Used | |---|---|---| | **Step 1** | Identify converter type from problem statement | Full / Semi / 3-phase | | **Step 2** | Write the **average V₀ equation** for that converter | $V_0 = f(\alpha, V_m)$ | | **Step 3** | Use motor back-EMF equation to find $I_a$ | $I_a = (V_0 - E_b)/R_a$ | | **Step 4** | Compute torque $T = k\Phi I_a$ | — | | **Step 5** | Compute speed $N$ or $\omega$ using the derived formula | $N = f(V_0, I_a, R_a, k\Phi)$ | | **Step 6** | For power: $P = T \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a$ | — | | **Step 7** | For efficiency: $\eta = P_{output}/P_{input}$ | — | ### **Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style):** > **Question:** A **230 V, 1500 RPM, 10 A** DC shunt motor has armature resistance $R_a = 1\,\Omega$ and field constant $k\Phi = 0.5\,\text{V·s/rad}$. The motor is fed by a single-phase full converter with AC source $V_s = 230\,\text{V (rms)}$, 50 Hz. Find: (a) Motor speed at firing angle α = 30°. (b) Torque developed at this operating point. (c) No-load speed and starting torque. **Given:** - $V_s = 230\,\text{V (rms)}$ → $V_m = 230\sqrt{2} = 325.27\,\text{V}$ - $R_a = 1\,\Omega$, $k\Phi = 0.5\,\text{V·s/rad}$ - $\alpha = 30°$, $\cos 30° = 0.866$ - Rated: 1500 RPM, 10 A **(a) Speed at α = 30°:** $$V_0 = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha = \dfrac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \times 0.866 = 207.07 \times 0.866 = 179.32\,\text{V}$$ Armature current at rated load (assumed same as rated for now): $I_a = 10\,\text{A}$ $$\omega = \dfrac{V_0 - I_a R_a}{k\Phi} = \dfrac{179.32 - 10 \times 1}{0.5} = \dfrac{169.32}{0.5} = 338.64\,\text{rad/s}$$ $$N = \omega \times \dfrac{60}{2\pi} = 338.64 \times 9.5493 = 3233.7\,\text{RPM}$$ **(b) Torque developed:** $$T = k\Phi \cdot I_a = 0.5 \times 10 = 5\,\text{N·m}$$ **(c) No-load speed** ($I_a \approx 0$): $$N_0 = \dfrac{V_0 \times 60}{2\pi \times k\Phi} = \dfrac{179.32 \times 60}{2\pi \times 0.5} = 3423.7\,\text{RPM}$$ **Starting torque** (at N = 0, so $E_b = 0$): $$I_{a,st} = \dfrac{V_0}{R_a} = \dfrac{179.32}{1} = 179.32\,\text{A}$$ $$T_{st} = k\Phi \cdot I_{a,st} = 0.5 \times 179.32 = 89.66\,\text{N·m}$$ # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — Phase-Controlled DC Drive ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Mains Supply

The intersection of this line with the **load torque line** gives the **operating point**. --- ## 4.4 Quadrant Operation Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Q1["Quadrant I: Forward Motoring"] Q1A[V sub 0 positive] --> Q1B[I sub a positive] Q1B --> Q1C[Speed positive Torque positive] end subgraph Q2["Quadrant II: Forward Braking Regeneration"] Q2A[V sub 0 negative] --> Q2B[I sub a positive] Q2B --> Q2C[Speed positive Torque negative] end subgraph Q3["Quadrant III: Reverse Motoring"] Q3A[V sub 0 negative] --> Q3B[I sub a negative] Q3B --> Q3C[Speed negative Torque negative] end subgraph Q4["Quadrant IV: Reverse Braking Regeneration"] Q4A[V sub 0 positive] --> Q4B[I sub a negative] Q4B --> Q4C[Speed negative Torque positive] end Q1 --- Q2 Q2 --- Q3 Q3 --- Q4 Q4 --- Q1 style Q1 fill:#90EE90 style Q2 fill:#FFB6C1 style Q3 fill:#87CEEB style Q4 fill:#FFD700 ``` --- ## 4.5 Continuous vs. Discontinuous Conduction Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Converter Fed DC Motor] --> B{L sub a large
