# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Explain the Constant Current — Constant Voltage (CC-CV) charging technique with suitable waveforms. Why is it preferred for Li-ion batteries?**

**Model Answer:**

The CC-CV method is a two-stage hybrid charging algorithm for Li-ion cells.

**Stage 1 — Constant Current (Bulk):** A constant current (typically 0.5C to 1C) is forced into the battery. The terminal voltage rises linearly until it reaches the upper limit **V_cv = 4.2 V/cell**.

**Stage 2 — Constant Voltage (Saturation):** The voltage is now held at 4.2 V. The current tapers down exponentially as the cell becomes charged. Charging terminates when current falls below **0.05C**.

**Waveform description:**
- **X-axis:** Time (t)
- **Y-axis (top):** Voltage rises linearly in CC, then flattens at 4.2 V in CV.
- **Y-axis (bottom):** Current is constant in CC, then exponentially decays in CV.

**Why preferred for Li-ion:**
- Prevents **lithium plating** that occurs at high voltage and high current.
- Avoids **over-voltage** damage to the separator and electrolyte.
- Extends **cycle life** (Li-ion can deliver 1000+ cycles at 80% SoC cutoff).
- Maximizes charge acceptance while preserving safety.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU marker awards full 3 marks only if the waveform is sketched and **both** stages are described with their physical reasons.

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Briefly explain the role of Wide Bandgap (WBG) semiconductors in modern power electronic drives for robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

Wide Bandgap semiconductors — primarily **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** and **Gallium Nitride (GaN)** — have revolutionized robotic drives due to their superior material properties.

| Property | Silicon (Si) | SiC | GaN |
|----------|-------------|-----|-----|
| Bandgap (eV) | 1.1 | 3.26 | 3.4 |
| Breakdown Field (MV/cm) | 0.3 | 3.0 | 3.3 |
| Electron Mobility (cm²/Vs) | 1400 | 900 | 1500 |
| Max Operating Temp (°C) | 150 | 200+ | 200+ |
| Switching Frequency Range | < 20 kHz | 50 kHz – 1 MHz | 100 kHz – 10 MHz |

**Impact on robotics:**
- **Higher f_sw** → smaller inductors and capacitors → compact servo drives.
- **Lower losses** → >99% efficiency, less cooling required.
- **Higher temperature tolerance** → drives can operate near motors without bulky heatsinks.
- **Faster dynamic response** → improved motion control bandwidth for precision robotics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 3-mark answer should mention **at least 3 advantages** and **one specific application** (e.g., SiC in EV traction, GaN in drone ESCs).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With a neat block diagram and current-voltage waveforms, explain the CC-CV charging method for a Li-ion battery. Derive the expression for total charging time.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Compare the Constant Current, Constant Voltage, and Pulse charging methods in terms of charging time, efficiency, battery stress, and suitable applications.

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A**

#### **Part (a) — Model Solution**

**Block Diagram Description:**

```
AC Mains → EMI Filter → PFC Rectifier → DC Link → Buck Converter → Battery
                                       ↑
                            Control Loop (Voltage & Current Sensors)
```

**Operating Principle:**

In CC-CV charging:

1. The battery management system (BMS) monitors terminal voltage and current.
2. **Phase 1 (CC):** The buck converter is operated in **current-mode control**, regulating the inductor current to a fixed value I_cc. The output voltage rises from V_min to V_cv (e.g., 3.0 V to 4.2 V per cell). This phase delivers approximately **80% of total charge**.

3. **Phase 2 (CV):** The controller switches to **voltage-mode control**, holding V_out = V_cv. The current now decays exponentially:

$$I_{chg}(t) = I_{peak} \cdot e^{-t/(R_{int} C_{diff})}$$

Charging ends when I_chg drops to I_term (0.05C).

**Derivation of total charging time:**

Time in CC phase:

$$t_{CC} = \frac{0.8 \cdot Q_{bat}}{I_{cc}}$$

Time in CV phase (using exponential decay):

$$t_{CV} = R_{int} \cdot C_{diff} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I_{peak}}{I_{term}}\right)$$

Total time:

$$\boxed{t_{total} = \frac{0.8 \cdot Q_{bat}}{I_{cc}} + R_{int} \cdot C_{diff} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I_{peak}}{I_{term}}\right)}$$

**Numerical Example:** For a 50 Ah Li-ion battery at 1C charge rate with τ = 60 s, I_peak = 50 A, I_term = 2.5 A:

$$t_{CC} = 0.8 \text{ h} = 48 \text{ min}$$

$$t_{CV} = 60 \cdot \ln(20) = 179.7 \text{ s} \approx 3 \text{ min}$$

$$t_{total} = 51 \text{ min}$$

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution**

| Parameter | Constant Current | Constant Voltage | Pulse Charging |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Charging Time | Shortest for bulk phase | Longest overall | Comparable to CC-CV |
| Charging Efficiency | 85 – 90% | 80 – 85% | 92 – 96% |
| Battery Stress (Temperature Rise) | High (no relaxation) | Lowest | Lowest (depolarization) |
| Risk of Overcharge | High (no V limit) | Low (current tapers) | Low (controlled pulses) |
| Complexity of Power Electronics | Low | Low | High (precise pulse control) |
| Best Application | Lead-acid (bulk) | Float / standby | Fast charging, Li-ion recovery |

**Conclusion:**
- **CC** is fast but unsafe for Li-ion.
- **CV** is safest but slow.
- **Pulse** offers the best balance, with reduced polarization and dendrite suppression, but requires complex control.

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Discuss the recent developments in power electronics for industrial automation. Highlight the role of SiC and GaN devices.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Explain the architecture of a modern battery charger for an electric vehicle. Include the role of PFC, isolated DC-DC, and bidirectional capability.

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B**

#### **Part (a) — Model Solution**

**Industrial Automation and Power Electronics — The New Era:**

Modern industrial automation — under the banner of **Industry 4.0** — has been transformed by the convergence of power electronics with communication, sensing, and AI. Key developments include:

1. **Active Front-End (AFE) Rectifiers:** Replace diode rectifiers to achieve **near-unity power factor (PF > 0.99)** and **low THD (< 5%)** while enabling **regenerative braking energy recovery** back to the grid.

2. **Wide Bandgap Devices (SiC and GaN):**
   - **SiC MOSFETs** in the 1.2 kV – 3.3 kV range have replaced IGBTs in motor drives up to 500 kW.
   - **GaN HEMTs** in the 100 V – 650 V range have replaced Si MOSFETs in low-voltage high-frequency applications (servo drives, drone ESCs).
   - **Net benefit:** 30% – 50% reduction in drive size, 50% reduction in cooling, 2× – 5× increase in switching frequency.

3. **Modular Multilevel Converters (MMC):** Used in high-power drives (ship propulsion, large rolling mills) for **near-sinusoidal output** with very low dv/dt stress on motor winddings.

4. **Integrated Motor Drives (IMD):** The inverter, gate driver, and control PCB are mounted directly inside the motor housing. ABB, Siemens, and Yaskawa have commercialized this concept, reducing cabinet space by up to 60%.

5. **Cyber-Physical Systems:** Drives now expose **PROFINET, EtherCAT, and TSN** interfaces for cloud-based monitoring, predictive maintenance, and digital twin synchronization.

**Why SiC and GaN are central:**
- They enable **higher efficiency** (>98%) which directly reduces **operating cost** and **carbon footprint**.
- They allow **higher power density**, critical in space-constrained robotic cells.
- They support **higher ambient temperatures**, eliminating the need for air-conditioned control cabinets.

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution**

**Modern EV Onboard Charger (OBC) Architecture:**

A Level-2 EV onboard charger (typically 6.6 kW to 22 kW) consists of three power stages:

**Stage 1 — Bridgeless Totem-Pole PFC Rectifier:**

- Converts single-phase or three-phase AC (e.g., 230 V / 400 V) into a regulated DC link voltage.
- Uses **GaN HEMTs** in a totem-pole configuration (no bridge rectifier, no diode losses).
- Achieves **PF > 0.99** and **THD < 3%**, meeting IEC 61000-3-2.
- Output DC link: typically 400 V (for 400 V battery) or 800 V (for 800 V architecture like Porsche Taycan).

**Stage 2 — Isolated DC-DC Converter (CLLC or DAB):**

- Provides **galvanic isolation** for safety.
- Steps the DC link voltage to battery voltage with high efficiency (96% – 98%).
- **DAB (Dual Active Bridge)** supports **bidirectional** power flow, essential for **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid)**.

**Stage 3 — Output Filter and BMS Interface:**

- LC filter smoothens the battery current.
- **CAN or SPI communication** with the BMS to dynamically adjust charging profile based on cell temperature and SoC.

**Bidirectional Capability:**

In modern OBCs, power flow can be reversed:
- **G2V (Grid-to-Vehicle):** Normal charging.
- **V2L (Vehicle-to-Load):** Power tools, home backup.
- **V2H (Vehicle-to-Home):** Emergency home supply.
- **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid):** Inject power back to grid during peak demand.

This requires **synchronized control** of both stages using a single DSP, with switching frequencies typically **100 kHz – 500 kHz** (DAB) for compact magnetics.

> [!NOTE]
> A 7-mark answer should include the **block diagram**, the **voltage levels at each stage**, and **at least 3 benefits** of bidirectional operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Reasons for Losing Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to label axes** in waveforms (e.g., "V vs t" or "I vs t") — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up units** (using mAh instead of Ah in Q = I·t) — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation steps** in the CC-CV total time question — show the integral expansion step by step.
> 4. **Using generic terms** like "efficient power electronics" without naming specific topologies (PFC, DAB, LLC) — costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring safety and standards** (IEC 61851, IEC 61000) when discussing chargers — examiner may deduct 1 mark.
> 6. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows** showing power flow direction.
> 7. **Confusing SiC and GaN ranges:** SiC is preferred for **high voltage (≥ 600 V)**, GaN for **low voltage (≤ 600 V) high frequency**.
